The result, fifty years later, is one of the tidiest and most affluent beach communities in the country. The three kilometre surf beach is superb, the safest swimming in Tairua Harbour is on the inside of the spit, and the nine hole golf course is surrounded by kahikatea forest. In January the grass airstrip is used by pilots flying themselves up from Auckland for the weekend. In winter the town almost completely empties out. Drive the streets, admire the gardens, swim, play minigolf in the central reserve, and treat yourself to fish and chips on the pier. Pauanui has a pleasantly unreal quality. It is exactly the beach holiday its designers imagined in nineteen sixty eight.

name: The Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Valley Hike
short: Climb to the dramatic rock spires of The Pinnacles for panoramic Coromandel views.
commentary: The Pinnacles Track in the Kauaeranga Valley is the Coromandel's premier hike. The track climbs through regenerating bush to the Pinnacles Hut, then a final scramble up metal stairways leads to the 759-metre summit. Views extend across the peninsula to both coasts. Can be done as a long day trip or overnight.

reviews.summary: The The Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Valley Hike adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Climb to the dramatic rock spires of The Pinnacles for panoramic Coromandel views. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Kauaeranga Valley road end, the starting point for the Coromandel's most iconic hike. The Pinnacles Track climbs steadily through native bush that was heavily logged for kauri in the 19th century but is now regenerating beautifully. Along the way, you will pass the remains of old kauri dams, engineering marvels that loggers used to flush timber downstream in artificial floods. The Pinnacles Hut sits at the treeline, a comfortable base if you are staying overnight. From there, the final push to the 759-metre summit involves metal stairways bolted into the rock face, climbing through gaps in the dramatic volcanic spires that give this place its name. At the top, the view is staggering, you can see both the Pacific Ocean and the Hauraki Gulf, with the entire peninsula laid out below. Start early if doing it as a day trip, the return takes seven to eight hours.

name: Kauaeranga Valley – The Pinnacles Walk
short: Climb to The Pinnacles for panoramic views of the Coromandel from a dramatic rocky summit at 759 m.
commentary: The Pinnacles Walk in the Kauaeranga Valley is a popular overnight tramp reaching 759 metres. The track passes through regenerating bush and historic kauri dam sites. The Pinnacles Hut accommodates 80 people, and the final climb to the summit uses fixed ladders and chain handholds through rocky terrain.

reviews.summary: The Kauaeranga Valley – The Pinnacles Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Climb to The Pinnacles for panoramic views of the Coromandel from a dramatic rocky summit at 759 m. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are heading up the Kauaeranga Valley towards The Pinnacles, the Coromandel's most popular overnight tramp. The track rises through regenerating bush — this valley was heavily logged for kauri timber in the 1800s, and along the way you pass the remains of massive kauri dams that were used to flush logs downstream in artificial floods. The bush has recovered remarkably, and the birdlife is rich. After about three hours, you reach Pinnacles Hut at 759 metres, an 80-bunk hut perched on a ridge. From here, the summit track gets serious — fixed ladders and chain handholds help you scramble up the final rocky pinnacles. From the top, the views are extraordinary: the entire Coromandel Peninsula spread out below you, the Pacific Ocean to the east, the Firth of Thames to the west. Try to time your ascent for sunrise — watching dawn light up the peninsula from this vantage point is unforgettable.

name: Port Jackson & Fletcher Bay
short: The very tip of the Coromandel Peninsula — a remote unsealed drive ending at one of the most beautiful campgrounds in the North Island.
commentary: From Coromandel Town a long unsealed road climbs over Te Hikuwai before descending to the white sand beaches of Port Jackson and Fletcher Bay. Start of the Coromandel Coastal Walkway.

reviews.summary: Port Jackson & Fletcher Bay draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The very tip of the Coromandel Peninsula — a remote unsealed drive ending at one of the most beautiful campgrounds in the North Island. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have committed to driving to the very tip of the Coromandel Peninsula and you should set aside half a day for the privilege. From Coromandel Town the road heads north along the Hauraki Gulf coast, through old gold mining country, and within an hour the seal runs out. You will spend the next forty kilometres on a winding unsealed road that climbs over headlands, dips into bays, and rewards every cautious driver with another lookout. At the end of the road, separated from each other by a steep ridge, are two of the most beautiful Department of Conservation campgrounds in the North Island. Port Jackson sits behind a long white sand beach with views across to Great Barrier Island. Fletcher Bay sits at the very tip, looking out over a clear blue strait. Between them runs the Coromandel Coastal Walkway, a spectacular three hour one way coastal track that many visitors complete with a shuttle pickup at the other end. Up here there is no shop, no fuel station, no mobile reception. Bring everything you need, including patience for the road. You will be rewarded with one of the quietest stretches of coastline within easy reach of Auckland.

name: Shakespeare Cliff Lookout
short: Ten minutes above Cooks Beach — one of the great Coromandel lookouts, looking directly across to Cathedral Cove and beyond to the Mercury Islands.
commentary: Shakespeare Cliff sits above the southern end of Cooks Beach. A 15-minute walk from the car park gives a clifftop view across Mercury Bay.

reviews.summary: Shakespeare Cliff Lookout draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Ten minutes above Cooks Beach — one of the great Coromandel lookouts, looking directly across to Cathedral Cove and beyond to the Mercury Islands. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Shakespeare Cliff, a modest promontory rising above the southern end of Cooks Beach on the eastern Coromandel coast. The name comes from a nineteenth century surveyor who thought the cliff resembled the silhouette of William Shakespeare, which with enough squinting you can just about make out. The walk up takes about fifteen minutes on a well formed track from the car park, and the pay off is one of the best views on the Coromandel Peninsula. From the top you look directly across Mercury Bay to the white cliffs of Cathedral Cove, south to Hahei Beach with its pohutukawa fringed curve, north to Shakespeare Cliff's neighbour Te Whanganui-a-Hei, and on a clear day out to the Mercury Islands themselves, twenty kilometres offshore. It was in these waters, in November seventeen sixty nine, that James Cook anchored the Endeavour to observe the transit of Mercury across the sun, giving him the first reliable longitude reading for New Zealand and giving Mercury Bay its name. Stand on the cliff, imagine the Endeavour at anchor below, and remember that a single event of eighteenth century astronomy put this quiet corner of the Coromandel on the world's charts.

name: Stony Bay & Coromandel Coastal Walkway
short: DOC campground at the far northern end of the Coromandel — trailhead for the spectacular Coromandel Coastal Walkway.
commentary: Stony Bay is a DOC campground at the end of the eastern gravel road around the far north Coromandel. The Coromandel Coastal Walkway (7 km) runs between Stony Bay and Fletcher Bay.

reviews.summary: Stony Bay & Coromandel Coastal Walkway draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. DOC campground at the far northern end of the Coromandel — trailhead for the spectacular Coromandel Coastal Walkway. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Stony Bay, which is near the end of the known road on the Coromandel Peninsula. To get here you have driven forty kilometres of winding gravel north from Coromandel Town, past the turnoff for Port Jackson, around headlands with no safety barriers and no phone reception, and you have probably wondered more than once whether you should have turned back. The DOC campground here is simple. Long drop toilets, flat grass, a tidal stream, a small stony beach, and the constant murmur of the Hauraki Gulf. Most people come to Stony Bay for one particular reason. This is the eastern end of the Coromandel Coastal Walkway, a seven kilometre one way track that runs across the very tip of the peninsula to Fletcher Bay. The walk takes three hours each way. You climb above white cliffs, cross sheep paddocks, pass above hidden bays that are accessible only by kayak, and finish at one of the wildest, least visited beaches in the upper North Island. A small shuttle service runs between the two bays if you do not want to double back. Pack everything. Bring water. Leave time. This is some of the quietest country on the Coromandel.

name: Tairua Harbour & Paku Summit
short: The tidal harbour town on the eastern Coromandel coast, crowned by the twin peaks of Paku — a ten-minute summit walk for one of the great Coromandel views.
commentary: Tairua sits on the eastern Coromandel coast, separated from its neighbour Pauanui by the harbour. Paku, a twin-peaked volcanic plug of 178 m, offers a short walk to a spectacular viewpoint.

reviews.summary: Tairua Harbour & Paku Summit draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The tidal harbour town on the eastern Coromandel coast, crowned by the twin peaks of Paku — a ten-minute summit walk for one of the great Coromandel views. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Tairua, a small town wrapped around a tidal harbour on the eastern Coromandel coast. Tairua is the quieter older sibling to Pauanui, its manicured neighbour on the far side of the harbour. The defining feature of the town is Paku, a twin peaked volcanic plug rising directly out of the sea at the northern head of the harbour. At a hundred and seventy eight metres Paku is small by mountain standards but its position makes it magnificent. A steep sealed road climbs most of the way up and a ten minute walking track takes you to the summit. From the top you look directly down on the entrance to Tairua Harbour, across to Shoe Island and the Alderman Islands offshore, north to Slipper Island, and south along the white crescent of Pauanui Beach. The climb is short enough to do before breakfast and spectacular enough to be remembered long after. Down in the town itself, the main street has a small and excellent bakery, a handful of good cafes, and a tidal estuary full of stingrays in summer that will nose right up to the edge of the pontoons. Tairua is one of those Coromandel towns that has changed very little in thirty years. That is, by any measure, its charm.

name: Thames Historical Town
short: Once the largest town in NZ — the Thames goldfield boomed to 18,000 people, twice the size of Auckland in 1870.
commentary: Thames was founded as a gold rush settlement in 1867. At its 1870 peak it was the largest town in New Zealand. The School of Mines, opened in 1886, is now a museum.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Thames Historical Town — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Once the largest town in NZ — the Thames goldfield boomed to 18,000 people, twice the size of Auckland in 1870. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are walking through Thames, and you should know that for a brief and astonishing time, this was the largest town in New Zealand. Gold was found here in eighteen sixty seven, and within three years more than eighteen thousand people had crowded into the new settlement around the Hauraki gulf. By eighteen seventy Thames had more residents than Auckland. The pubs ran twenty four hours, the harbour was full of supply ships, and a hundred and fifty stamper batteries pounded gold-bearing quartz day and night. The boom did not last. By the nineteen hundreds most of the easy gold was gone, and the population shrank to a tenth of its peak. What remained, and what you walk through today, is an unusually intact Victorian commercial town. The wide main street still has many of its original brick and timber buildings, and several have been beautifully restored as cafes and small shops. Visit the Thames School of Mines, opened in eighteen eighty six and now a museum, where you can see the original assay ovens and rock collections. The whole town breathes its history, gently and without exaggeration. You are walking through the ghost of a gold rush.

name: Thames Coast Road – SH25
short: Drive the dramatic coast road from Thames north along the Firth of Thames, carved into cliffs above the sea.
commentary: SH25 north of Thames follows the western coast of the Coromandel Peninsula along the Firth of Thames. The road is carved into coastal cliffs with pohutukawa trees overhead and the Firth below. The section passes through small settlements including Tapu, Te Puru, and Thornton Bay.

reviews.summary: The Thames Coast Road – SH25 route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the dramatic coast road from Thames north along the Firth of Thames, carved into cliffs above the sea. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving north from Thames on State Highway 25, and the road has been carved into the cliff face above the Firth of Thames. This is one of the most scenic coastal drives in the North Island, with pohutukawa trees arching overhead and the calm waters of the Firth stretching across to the Hauraki Plains. The road is narrow, winding, and occasionally closes after storms when slips block the way — a reminder that this coast is geologically active. Small communities cling to the narrow strip between the hills and the sea: Tapu, Te Puru, Thornton Bay — each with a few houses, a boat ramp, and not much else. At low tide, the Firth reveals extensive mudflats that are important feeding grounds for migratory shorebirds — godwits, knots, and oystercatchers that travel from as far as Alaska. The Thames coastline is also the northern edge of the Miranda shorebird reserve, one of the most important wetlands in the country. Drive slowly and enjoy the rhythm of the coast.

name: Coromandel Town
short: Where New Zealand's first gold rush began in 1852 — a small historic town with outsized character.
commentary: Gold was found in Driving Creek in late 1852 by sawyer Charles Ring — the country's first gold rush, a decade before Otago. It didn't last; the ore was too fragmented. But the town survived, then boomed again when kauri milling and later proper gold mining restarted in the 1860s–80s. Today Coromandel Town is a small, quirky, independent-minded place. The Coromandel Smoking Company sells excellent smoked mussels and fish. Tāne Tinorau's descendants still own Driving Creek. Visit on a weekday.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Coromandel Town — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Where New Zealand's first gold rush began in 1852 — a small historic town with outsized character. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: The Waterworks
short: An outdoor playground of water-powered sculptures built from old kitchen scraps and farm machinery — only on the 309 Road could this exist.
commentary: A privately operated park along the 309 Road of dozens of water-powered sculptures built from recycled materials. Family-friendly.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at The Waterworks is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. An outdoor playground of water-powered sculptures built from old kitchen scraps and farm machinery — only on the 309 Road could this exist. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You have stopped at The Waterworks, and you have done the right thing. Hidden in the bush along the 309 Road, halfway between Coromandel Town and Whitianga, this is one of the more delightfully odd attractions in the country. The park is the lifelong project of one family, built over decades from recycled materials, and it is exactly what the name suggests. The Waiau Stream runs through the property and powers dozens of moving sculptures, almost all of them constructed from kitchen utensils, farm scrap, old bicycle parts, and rusty machinery. Pots beat tunes on bells. A line of toilet bowls flush themselves in sequence. A bicycle wheels itself in slow circles. Children can spend hours here, working their way around the property, pulling levers, climbing on the larger pieces, and squealing at the next absurd contraption. Adults will spend the whole visit smiling at the patience and humour that built the place. There is an old fashioned cafe at the entrance. There is no electricity in the park, just water and gravity. The whole thing is unmistakably handmade, and unmistakably Coromandel. Allow at least an hour, more if you have kids.

name: Waihī Beach
short: An 11 km surf beach at the southern end of the Coromandel — linked by the Orokawa Bay coastal walk to a hidden family-perfect swim beach.
commentary: Waihī Beach is an 11-km surf beach that marks the southern end of the Coromandel east coast. The Orokawa Bay walkway (1.5 hours return) leads to a sheltered northern cove.

reviews.summary: Waihī Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. An 11 km surf beach at the southern end of the Coromandel — linked by the Orokawa Bay coastal walk to a hidden family-perfect swim beach. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Waihī Beach, which is where the Coromandel east coast effectively ends and the Bay of Plenty begins. The beach itself is eleven kilometres of open surf, stretching south from the Orokawa headland to the entrance of Tauranga Harbour at Bowentown. Waihī Beach is the biggest town on this stretch of coast, a permanent population of a few thousand, and a summer population many times that. The surf is steady. The swimming is patrolled between the flags in summer. Most visitors stay on the main beach, and that is fine. The genuinely special walk is from the northern end of the town. The Orokawa Bay Walkway climbs over the headland from Waihī Beach itself, takes about forty minutes each way, and delivers you to Orokawa Bay, a small sheltered cove of golden sand, fringed by pohutukawa, inaccessible by road and therefore almost always quiet. In summer the pohutukawa bloom red. In autumn the track is empty. Swim, picnic, walk back. If you want to keep going, the track continues beyond Orokawa to William Wright Falls, a small waterfall in the bush another thirty minutes further on. Pack water. Pack a towel. Allow three hours.

name: Waihi — Māori Gold Country
short: Gold country that was Ngāti Tamatera territory — the mines sit on ancestral Māori land.
commentary: The Waihi goldfield, one of the richest in NZ, sits on ancestral Ngāti Tamatera land. Māori were initially involved in prospecting and mining before being sidelined as industrial operations expanded. Treaty claims relating to the gold resource are ongoing.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Waihi — Māori Gold Country as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Gold country that was Ngāti Tamatera territory — the mines sit on ancestral Māori land. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waiomu Kauri Grove
short: A quiet two-hour bush walk up the Waiomu Stream to a grove of surviving mature kauri — one of the last on the western Coromandel.
commentary: The Waiomu Kauri Grove is reached by a 2-hour return walk up the Waiomu Stream on the western Coromandel coast. Several kauri of around 1000 years old stand at the head of the track.

reviews.summary: Waiomu Kauri Grove draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A quiet two-hour bush walk up the Waiomu Stream to a grove of surviving mature kauri — one of the last on the western Coromandel. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Waiomu Kauri Grove track, one of the quieter kauri walks on the Coromandel Peninsula. The track begins ten minutes north of Thames on the coast road, turns inland up the Waiomu Stream, and follows the stream back into the Coromandel Range. It is two hours return at a relaxed pace. For most of its length the track is flat and sheltered, with the stream on one side and regenerating native bush on the other. The point of the walk is the grove at the turn around. Several mature kauri stand here together, the oldest of them perhaps a thousand years old, their great silver grey trunks rising fifty metres before the first branch. The Coromandel lost perhaps ninety eight percent of its original kauri forest to the saws of the nineteenth century, and the small surviving remnants feel like the last pages of a much longer book. Before you enter the grove, use the boot cleaning station at the trailhead. Kauri dieback disease is carried in a single grain of soil and has already killed many of the peninsula's surviving trees. Scrub hard, walk only on the boardwalk, and give these last giants the respect they have earned by standing long.

name: Whangamata Bar Surf Break
short: Ride the legendary left-hand bar break at Whangamata, one of NZ's best waves.
commentary: The Whangamata bar break is a left-hand river mouth wave considered one of the best in New Zealand. It breaks over the sandbar at the harbour entrance, producing long, hollow lefts on a solid easterly swell. The wave is best suited to experienced surfers due to strong currents.

reviews.summary: The Whangamata Bar Surf Break adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride the legendary left-hand bar break at Whangamata, one of NZ's best waves. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Whangamata, home to one of New Zealand's most celebrated surf breaks. The bar break here is a left-hand river mouth wave that peels along the sandbar at the harbour entrance, and on its day, it is world-class. When a solid east or northeast swell hits at low to mid tide, the wave jacks up on the shallow bar and produces long, hollow lefts that can run for over 200 metres. This is not a wave for beginners, the currents around the harbour mouth are powerful and the takeoff zone is critical. But if you have the skill, it is a ride you will never forget. The main beach at Whangamata also offers more forgiving waves closer to shore. Surf reports and local advice are available from the surf shops in the village.

name: Driving Creek Railway
short: A quirky 2.75 km hand-built narrow-gauge mountain railway — begun by potter Barry Brickell in 1974 as a way to get clay down the hill.
commentary: Potter and conservationist Barry Brickell bought a worked-out kauri block above Coromandel town in 1973 and began hand-building a narrow-gauge railway to haul clay out of the hills. He never stopped. When he died in 2016 the Driving Creek Railway was 2.75 km long, with two spiral tunnels, five reversing switchbacks, three zig-zag sections, and a terminus called the Eyefull Tower. Operated daily by volunteers. The ride takes about an hour. The hills Barry began replanting in 1973 are now mature native bush — that's the real point of the place.

reviews.summary: Driving Creek Railway draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A quirky 2. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Hot Water Beach
short: Dig a hole in the sand at low tide and you'll hit water hot enough to cook in.
commentary: Hot Water Beach has a geothermal spring that rises through the sand at one particular spot, heating the beach to around 64°C just below the surface. Two hours either side of low tide you can dig a hole with a spade (rental available from the store opposite) and make your own natural spa pool. Timing matters — arrive at low tide or the sea covers the hot spot. The beach is beautiful but the surf is dangerous; the rip is one of the worst on the east coast. Swim between the flags only. No digging at high tide.

reviews.summary: Hot Water Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Dig a hole in the sand at low tide and you'll hit water hot enough to cook in. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: New Chums Beach / Wainuiototo
short: One of the last completely undeveloped white-sand beaches in the North Island — no road, 30 minutes walk in.
commentary: New Chums (Wainuiototo) is reached only on foot — park at the end of Whangapoua Beach, wade across the stream at low tide, walk over a bush-clad headland for 30 minutes, and you arrive at a kilometre of curved, completely undeveloped white sand backed by pōhutukawa forest. No road, no buildings, no toilets, no rubbish bins, no shops. Take your rubbish out. Don't go in rain (stream crossings). This is how the Coromandel coast used to look in the 1940s; there is sustained local effort to keep it that way.

reviews.summary: New Chums Beach / Wainuiototo draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. One of the last completely undeveloped white-sand beaches in the North Island — no road, 30 minutes walk in. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Valley
short: A classic overnight hike to the 759 m Pinnacles — through 19th-century kauri-driving country and up a spectacular rock summit.
commentary: The Kauaeranga Valley was one of the biggest kauri-logging operations in New Zealand from the 1870s to the 1920s; remains of the dams that 'drove' logs down the river are still visible along the track. The main walk climbs from the Kauaeranga Valley Road to the Pinnacles Hut (80 bunks, book ahead — this is DOC's most popular hut) then onto the Pinnacles Summit itself (759 m) via a series of wooden ladders. The summit view on a clear day spans both coasts of the peninsula. Allow two days. Not a walk to underestimate.

reviews.summary: Pinnacles / Kauaeranga Valley draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A classic overnight hike to the 759 m Pinnacles — through 19th-century kauri-driving country and up a spectacular rock summit. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Whangamatā Ocean Beach
short: A four-kilometre curve of white sand and one of the most reliable surf breaks on the east coast.
commentary: Whangamatā's Ocean Beach runs four kilometres from the harbour bar to Taumata Rock, and the surf break at the northern end (The Bar) produces consistent hollow right-handers on east and north swells. The permanent population is 4,000; in summer it quadruples. In 2005 the town famously fought — and won — an Environment Court battle to stop a proposed marina in the harbour, a decision later reversed and the marina eventually built. The harbour is still the town's heart. The beach is still the reason to come.

reviews.summary: Whangamatā Ocean Beach draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A four-kilometre curve of white sand and one of the most reliable surf breaks on the east coast. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Whitianga
short: The main east-coast Coromandel town — on Mercury Bay, where James Cook observed the transit of Mercury in 1769.
commentary: Whitianga sits on Mercury Bay, named by Cook in November 1769 after he observed the transit of Mercury across the sun from a nearby headland — one of the key astronomical observations of his first Pacific voyage. Local iwi Ngāti Hei hosted Cook's party for ten days and the contact was peaceful, uniquely so for early encounters. The town itself is a family-holiday centre: Buffalo Beach for the swim, the harbour passenger ferry (a 2-minute crossing) to Ferry Landing and Shakespeare Cliff for the views. Oysters, mussels and fresh fish at the harbour.

reviews.summary: Whitianga draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The main east-coast Coromandel town — on Mercury Bay, where James Cook observed the transit of Mercury in 1769. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Cape Kidnappers / Te Kauwae-a-Māui
short: The jaw of Māui's fish — a 140m cliff face and the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony.
commentary: Two stories, same piece of rock. In Māori tradition this is Te Kauwae-a-Māui — the jawbone Māui used to fish up the North Island. In European history it's 'Cape Kidnappers', named by Cook on 15 October 1769 after a Ngāi Te Whatuiāpiti party under Te Rangikoianake tried to free a young Tahitian boy, Taiata, whom they believed was being held captive on the Endeavour. Around 6,500 pairs of Australasian gannets nest on the cliff edge from September to April — the world's most accessible mainland colony. Three ways in: the low-tide walk from Clifton Beach (~3 hrs each way, check tides), tractor-trailer tours up the beach, or the overland route through Cape Kidnappers Station.

reviews.summary: Cape Kidnappers / Te Kauwae-a-Māui draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The jaw of Māui's fish — a 140m cliff face and the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Esk Valley
short: The northernmost Hawke's Bay wine sub-region — devastated by Cyclone Gabrielle in February 2023, now visibly coming back.
commentary: The Esk Valley sits at the foot of the climb onto the Napier–Taupō Road. Flat, fertile, wine country. At around 4 am on 14 February 2023, after two days of Cyclone Gabrielle rain, the Esk River jumped its banks. Within two hours houses were under eaves and vineyards were under two metres of silt. Petane Wines (winemaker Philip Barber dug out 12,000 bottles by hand), Sacred Hill (≈15 ha of Dartmoor vineyard now permanently under a new riverbed) and Linden Estate (≈6 ha under silt, cellar door torn apart) were among the worst hit. Eleven people died across Hawke's Bay that day. What you're driving through is the slow work of recovery — some vineyards replanted in the same rows, some moved uphill, some sold up. SH5 through Eskdale has been rebuilt in sections; the fresh tarmac is a scar.

reviews.summary: Esk Valley delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. The northernmost Hawke's Bay wine sub-region — devastated by Cyclone Gabrielle in February 2023, now visibly coming back. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

name: Art Deco Walking Tour, Napier
short: A daily guided walk through the most complete Art Deco city centre in the world.
commentary: Run by the Art Deco Trust from their visitor centre on Tennyson Street; daily 1-hour guided walks plus a longer afternoon option.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Art Deco Walking Tour, Napier — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A daily guided walk through the most complete Art Deco city centre in the world. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have signed up for an Art Deco walking tour in Napier and you are about to spend an hour learning to read a city. Almost everything in the central business district was built in the four years between nineteen thirty one and nineteen thirty five, after the Hawke's Bay earthquake on the third of February nineteen thirty one flattened the old Victorian centre in two and a half minutes. Two hundred and fifty six people died that morning. The rebuild started within weeks, and the architects in charge chose Art Deco, the newest and most optimistic style in the world at the time. Your guide will take you slowly along Emerson Street and Tennyson Street, pointing out the geometric sunbursts, the zigzag chevrons, the stylised Māori motifs, and the curved corner windows that mark the style. You will hear about the architect Louis Hay and the painter Charles Bourne, about the local concrete mixers who poured the foundations. You will be shown the National Tobacco Building in Ahuriri, with its great oak doors and roses, often picked as the most beautiful Art Deco building in the country. By the end of the walk you will see a city you missed when you arrived. Stay for a coffee at the Daily Telegraph Building afterward.

name: Napier Art Deco Walking Trail
short: Walk through the world's most complete Art Deco city, rebuilt after the devastating 1931 earthquake.
commentary: Napier's Art Deco quarter was built after the 3 February 1931 earthquake, which killed 256 people and levelled the city. The rebuild, completed mostly within two years, created one of the world's finest collections of Art Deco, Spanish Mission, and Stripped Classical architecture. The Art Deco Trust leads guided walking tours daily.

reviews.summary: The Napier Art Deco Walking Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk through the world's most complete Art Deco city, rebuilt after the devastating 1931 earthquake. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking through Napier's Art Deco quarter, and every building tells a story of resilience. On 3 February 1931, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck Hawke's Bay, killing 256 people and destroying most of Napier's commercial centre. The city was rebuilt in just two years in the architectural styles of the era — Art Deco, Spanish Mission, and Stripped Classical. The result is one of the most complete and concentrated collections of 1930s architecture anywhere in the world, rivalling Miami's South Beach. Look up as you walk Emerson Street and Tennyson Street — the geometric patterns, sunburst motifs, zigzag lines, and Maori-influenced designs on the facades are exquisite. The Daily Telegraph building, with its ornate sunbursts and zigzags, is a standout. Each February, the city hosts Art Deco Weekend, when thousands dress in period costume and the streets come alive with vintage cars, jazz bands, and 1930s glamour.

name: Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony Walk
short: Walk along dramatic coastal cliffs to the world's largest mainland gannet colony at Cape Kidnappers.
commentary: Cape Kidnappers hosts the world's largest and most accessible mainland gannet colony, with around 20,000 birds. The 8 km beach and cliff walk from Clifton is passable only at low tide. The cape was named by Captain Cook in 1769 after local Maori attempted to kidnap his Tahitian interpreter.

reviews.summary: The Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk along dramatic coastal cliffs to the world's largest mainland gannet colony at Cape Kidnappers. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are setting out along the beach from Clifton towards Cape Kidnappers, timing your walk with the low tide because the route passes beneath eroding cliffs that are cut off at high water. This is an 8-kilometre walk along the base of spectacular sandstone and mudstone cliffs banded in layers of geological time. The reward at the end is extraordinary: the world's largest mainland gannet colony, around 20,000 birds packed onto cliff-top platforms in a noisy, pungent, endlessly fascinating spectacle. From September to April, the colony is at its most active — adults soaring in on 1.8-metre wingspans, chicks begging for food, and pairs performing their elaborate greeting rituals of sky-pointing and bill-fencing. Captain Cook named this cape in 1769 after local Maori tried to take his young Tahitian interpreter, Tiata, from the Endeavour. Check the tide tables before you go and allow four to five hours return. This is one of those rare places where you can sit within metres of thousands of wild seabirds.

name: Cape Kidnappers Mountain Bike Trail
short: Ride the coastal trail to the world's largest mainland gannet colony at Cape Kidnappers.
commentary: Mountain biking to Cape Kidnappers combines a scenic coastal ride along the beach and clifftop tracks with a visit to the world's largest accessible mainland gannet colony. The 20-km return ride follows the beach at low tide and optional overland tracks. The colony is home to around 20,000 gannets from September to April.

reviews.summary: The Cape Kidnappers Mountain Bike Trail adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride the coastal trail to the world's largest mainland gannet colony at Cape Kidnappers. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Clifton, the start of an unforgettable mountain bike ride to Cape Kidnappers. The route follows the beach beneath towering striped sedimentary cliffs that have been tilted and folded by millions of years of tectonic activity, a geology lesson in living colour. Your destination is the world's largest accessible mainland gannet colony, home to around 20,000 Australasian gannets from September to April. The birds are utterly unfazed by human visitors, allowing you to watch their elaborate courtship rituals, feeding their chicks, and spectacular plunge-diving from just metres away. Timing is critical, you must check the tides before departing, as the beach section is only passable at low tide. The cape was named by Captain Cook in 1769 after local Maori attempted to kidnap his Tahitian interpreter. Allow four hours for the return trip with time at the colony.

name: Craggy Range / Tukituki Wineries
short: Hawke's Bay's most photographed winery — a vineyard cellar door under the great south face of Te Mata Peak.
commentary: Craggy Range is sited directly under Te Mata Peak. Known for its Sophia red blend and the Terrôir restaurant.

reviews.summary: Craggy Range / Tukituki Wineries delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. Hawke's Bay's most photographed winery — a vineyard cellar door under the great south face of Te Mata Peak. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

audio_story: You are standing in front of the Craggy Range cellar door, and you have probably already taken a photograph of the building. Behind the dark slate roof and white shingled walls, the southern face of Te Mata Peak rises four hundred metres almost vertically into the sky. There is no other winery view quite like it in the country. Craggy Range was established in nineteen ninety eight as a dedicated single vineyard producer. Its Sophia, a Bordeaux blend from the gravel terraces of the Gimblett Road, and its syrahs from the same area, are some of the most consistently awarded wines in Hawke's Bay. The cellar door pours both, and the staff are unusually patient and knowledgeable. The Terrôir restaurant on site has been one of the country's better fine dining destinations for years, with wood fired meats, garden vegetables, and a long list of by the glass options to accompany a tasting flight. You can also walk straight from the carpark up the Te Mata Peak track, which begins on the property. Do that first, work up an appetite, then come back down to the restaurant and reward yourself.

name: Kaweka Range Backcountry Track
short: Tramp through the rugged Kaweka Ranges to Mangatutu Hot Springs, deep in the North Island backcountry.
commentary: The Kaweka Forest Park covers 66,000 hectares of rugged hill country west of Hawke's Bay. The ranges rise to 1,724 metres at Kaweka J and are popular for tramping and hunting. The Mangatutu Hot Springs are a natural thermal pool in a remote bush-clad valley accessible by a full-day tramp.

reviews.summary: The Kaweka Range Backcountry Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Tramp through the rugged Kaweka Ranges to Mangatutu Hot Springs, deep in the North Island backcountry. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are heading into the Kaweka Ranges, 66,000 hectares of rugged backcountry that feels genuinely wild. These ranges separate Hawke's Bay from the volcanic plateau and rise to 1,724 metres at Kaweka J, where on a clear day you can see from Mount Ruapehu to the Pacific Ocean. The trails here are quieter than the more famous walks — you might see more deer than people. Red deer, introduced for hunting in the early 1900s, are abundant, and the Kawekas are one of New Zealand's most popular recreational hunting areas. Your destination is the Mangatutu Hot Springs, a natural thermal pool nestled in a bush-clad valley, where you can soak in warm mineral water surrounded by nothing but forest and birdsong. The track crosses tussock tops, drops through beech forest, and follows river valleys. DOC huts along the way are basic but welcome. This is not a manicured Great Walk — it is real backcountry tramping, and you need to be self-sufficient and prepared for changeable conditions.

name: Cape Sanctuary
short: The largest privately owned predator-fenced sanctuary in NZ — 2,500 hectares on the Cape Kidnappers peninsula.
commentary: Established 2006 by three landowners. Home to North Island brown kiwi, tuatara, takahē, kākā, hihi and other species. Limited public access via guided tours.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Cape Sanctuary earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. The largest privately owned predator-fenced sanctuary in NZ — 2,500 hectares on the Cape Kidnappers peninsula. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

audio_story: You are looking out toward the great Cape Kidnappers peninsula, and tucked behind its windswept cliffs is one of the most ambitious conservation projects in the country. Cape Sanctuary is twenty five hundred hectares of private land that, since two thousand and six, has been enclosed within a ten and a half kilometre predator-proof fence stretching across the neck of the peninsula. Inside, the three landowner families and a team of rangers have eliminated rats, possums, stoats and wild cats, and have begun reintroducing native species that vanished from this coast a century or more ago. Today the sanctuary is home to North Island brown kiwi, tuatara, kākā, hihi, takahē, and a growing population of seabirds drawn back by sound systems and decoys. It is the largest privately owned and managed sanctuary on the New Zealand mainland. Public access is limited and is generally only available through pre-booked guided tours, often combined with stays at the lodge on the property. From outside the fence, what you can see is a great green sweep of regenerating bush along the spine of the cape, and the satisfying knowledge that just beyond the next ridge, a slice of pre-human New Zealand is being patiently brought back to life.

name: Mohaka River White Water Rafting
short: Raft the remote Grade 3-5 rapids of the Mohaka River through pristine gorge country.
commentary: The Mohaka River is one of the North Island's premier white water rafting rivers, offering sections from Grade 3 to Grade 5 through deep gorges and native bush. Multi-day trips include wilderness camping. The river is also a renowned trout fishery.

reviews.summary: The Mohaka River White Water Rafting adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Raft the remote Grade 3-5 rapids of the Mohaka River through pristine gorge country. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the put-in for the Mohaka River, one of the most exhilarating white water experiences in the North Island. This river flows through deep gorges carved into the Kaweka Range, its rapids ranging from bouncy Grade 3 to the serious Grade 5 of the lower gorge. Your raft guide knows every rock and hydraulic on this river, which is essential because some of these rapids demand precise lines. Between the white water, the river is stunningly beautiful, flowing through pristine native bush with no roads, no buildings, and no cellphone signal. Multi-day trips include camping on riverside terraces under a canopy of stars, with the sound of the river as your soundtrack. The Mohaka is also one of New Zealand's finest trout rivers, and you might spot large browns holding in the pools between rapids. This is genuine backcountry adventure.

name: Pania of the Reef, Napier
short: The famous statue of Pania — a Māori maiden of the sea who fell in love with a young chief.
commentary: Pania of the Reef is Napier's most famous landmark. In Māori tradition, Pania was a maiden of the sea who came ashore each night to be with her lover Karitoki. When people tried to capture her, she returned to the sea forever and became the reef. The statue on Marine Parade has become Napier's icon.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pania of the Reef, Napier as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The famous statue of Pania — a Māori maiden of the sea who fell in love with a young chief. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Napier Parasailing
short: Parasail 150 metres above Hawke's Bay for Art Deco city and Cape views.
commentary: Parasailing from Napier's Marine Parade offers aerial views of the Art Deco city, Bluff Hill, and the sweeping arc of Hawke's Bay. Flights reach up to 150 metres and operate from a boat launch off the main beach. The bay's calm conditions make it one of the more reliable parasailing spots.

reviews.summary: The Napier Parasailing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Parasail 150 metres above Hawke's Bay for Art Deco city and Cape views. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Napier's Marine Parade, where a boat and parasail are ready to lift you high above one of New Zealand's most distinctive cities. As you ascend to 150 metres above Hawke's Bay, the entire Napier skyline reveals itself beneath you, a collection of Art Deco buildings rebuilt after the devastating 1931 earthquake that reshaped this city forever. The earthquake actually raised the seabed by up to two metres, creating the flat land where the airport and surrounding suburbs now sit. From your vantage point, you can see the full sweep of Hawke's Bay curving south towards Cape Kidnappers, the vineyards of the Heretaunga Plains stretching inland, and if conditions are clear, the snow-capped peaks of the Kaweka and Ruahine Ranges. The ride is peaceful, just the whisper of wind and an unbeatable view.

name: Ngaruroro River Kayaking
short: Kayak the braided Ngaruroro River through Hawke's Bay's dramatic gorge country.
commentary: The Ngaruroro River is one of the last unmodified braided rivers in the North Island, flowing from the Kaweka Range through gorge country to the Heretaunga Plains. Upper sections offer scenic kayaking through bush-clad gorges, while the lower braided reaches are more relaxed.

reviews.summary: The Ngaruroro River Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak the braided Ngaruroro River through Hawke's Bay's dramatic gorge country. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Ngaruroro River, one of the last great unmodified braided rivers in the North Island. This river flows from the remote Kaweka Range through dramatic gorge country before spreading across the Heretaunga Plains in a network of braided channels. The upper gorge sections offer kayaking through bush-lined canyons with Grade 2 rapids and crystal-clear pools that glow with a distinctive blue-green colour from the greywacke rock. The river supports excellent brown trout fishing, and you might spot large fish holding in the pools as you paddle over them. Below the gorge, the river braids across a wide shingle bed, and the paddling becomes more relaxed but no less scenic, with views to the ranges on both sides. The Ngaruroro is significant both ecologically and culturally, its waters sustain the productive orchards and vineyards of Hawke's Bay downstream. Bring your own kayak or arrange with local operators for guided trips through the best sections.

name: Ocean Beach Horse Trekking
short: Gallop along the wild black sand of Ocean Beach on horseback.
commentary: Horse trekking at Ocean Beach near Hastings takes riders along a remote stretch of wild black sand beach backed by cliffs. Treks cater to all experience levels, with the option to canter or gallop along the waterline. The beach is too dangerous for swimming but perfect for riding.

reviews.summary: The Ocean Beach Horse Trekking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Gallop along the wild black sand of Ocean Beach on horseback. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Ocean Beach, one of Hawke's Bay's wildest and most remote stretches of coastline. This is not a swimming beach, the surf is powerful and unpredictable, but from the saddle of a well-trained horse, it is absolutely magnificent. Your trek begins in the paddock where you will be matched with your horse, and after a safety briefing, you will head down the track to the beach. The black sand stretches for kilometres in both directions, backed by dramatic eroded cliffs and pounded by Hawke's Bay swells. Depending on your experience, you may walk gently along the waterline or open up to a full gallop with the wind in your face and spray flying from the hooves. There are few experiences in New Zealand quite as exhilarating as galloping along a deserted black sand beach with the Pacific Ocean roaring beside you.

name: Shine Falls / Te Puna o Te Taniwha
short: Hike through bush and farmland to the 58-metre Shine Falls, the North Island's tallest.
commentary: Shine Falls (Te Puna o Te Taniwha) is the tallest waterfall in the North Island at 58 metres. The 2.5-hour return walk crosses farmland and native bush along the Boundary Stream. Swimming at the base of the falls is popular in summer.

reviews.summary: The Shine Falls / Te Puna o Te Taniwha adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Hike through bush and farmland to the 58-metre Shine Falls, the North Island's tallest. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the trailhead for Shine Falls, home to a waterfall that many New Zealanders do not even know exists. At 58 metres, it is the tallest waterfall in the entire North Island, and the walk to reach it is a genuine delight. The track crosses open farmland with grazing sheep before entering native bush along the Boundary Stream. You will cross the stream several times on footbridges, passing through a forest of tree ferns and native broadleaf. Then the bush opens and there it is, a sheer curtain of water plunging over a limestone cliff into a deep, clear pool. The falls are named Te Puna o Te Taniwha, the spring of the taniwha, in Maori tradition. On a hot Hawke's Bay summer day, the pool at the base is perfect for swimming, though the water is bracing. Allow about two and a half hours for the return trip.

name: Te Mata Peak Summit Walk
short: Walk to the 399-metre summit of Te Mata Peak for sweeping views across Hawke's Bay and beyond.
commentary: Te Mata Peak rises 399 metres above the Heretaunga Plains near Havelock North. According to Maori legend, the peak is the body of a sleeping giant, Te Mata o Rongokako. Multiple walking and mountain biking tracks traverse the peak, and the summit is also accessible by road.

reviews.summary: The Te Mata Peak Summit Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to the 399-metre summit of Te Mata Peak for sweeping views across Hawke's Bay and beyond. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are climbing Te Mata Peak, and with every step the views expand across the Heretaunga Plains, the Pacific coast, and the distant Ruahine and Kaweka ranges. At 399 metres, this is not a high summit, but its position overlooking flat plains amplifies the sense of height dramatically. In Maori legend, this peak is the reclining body of the giant Te Mata o Rongokako, who was challenged to eat through the hills to prove his love for a chief's daughter. He choked and died, and his body became this ridge — if you look at the profile from the right angle, you can see the sleeping figure. The grassy slopes are dotted with sheep and swept by wind. On a clear day, you can see from Cape Kidnappers to the Mahia Peninsula. Multiple tracks criss-cross the peak — the ridge walk from the car park to the summit takes about 30 minutes and rewards you with one of the finest panoramas in the lower North Island.

name: Tukituki River Trail
short: A flat 32km cycle trail along the Tukituki River through orchards, vineyards and limestone bluffs.
commentary: Part of the Hawke's Bay Trails network, one of NZ's 22 Great Rides. Mostly off-road, links wineries and Te Mata Peak.

reviews.summary: Tukituki River Trail delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. A flat 32km cycle trail along the Tukituki River through orchards, vineyards and limestone bluffs. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

audio_story: You are riding the Tukituki River Trail, one of the easier and prettier sections of the wider Hawke's Bay Trails network, which is in turn one of the official Great Rides of New Zealand. The trail follows the gentle blue green Tukituki River for around thirty two kilometres from Waipawa down to the Pacific Ocean at Haumoana. There are almost no hills. The path is wide and well graded. You will pass orchards heavy with fruit in late summer, vineyards that turn gold in autumn, and dramatic white limestone bluffs above the river that have been named, with characteristic Hawke's Bay confidence, the Crab Farm Cliffs. You can hire bikes from operators in Hastings, Havelock North, or Napier, and many of them will do a one way drop off and pick up so you do not have to ride back. The trail connects naturally to several of the region's best wineries, including Black Barn and Craggy Range, and a relaxed afternoon can easily turn into a long lunch and a slow late return. Wear sunscreen. The Hawke's Bay sun is strong, and there is little shade along the gravel stretches. Take a picnic. Ride at the pace of the river.

name: Tukituki Valley Scenic Drive
short: Drive the Tukituki River valley past limestone formations and sheep stations to the remote Makaroro Valley.
commentary: The Tukituki Valley drive follows the Tukituki River inland from Havelock North through pastoral farmland to the rugged Ruahine Ranges. The route passes the Patangata limestone bluffs and leads to the Makaroro and Waipawa River valleys. The area is renowned for its trout fishing and pastoral beauty.

reviews.summary: The Tukituki Valley Scenic Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the Tukituki River valley past limestone formations and sheep stations to the remote Makaroro Valley. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving up the Tukituki Valley, leaving the vineyards and orchards of Hawke's Bay behind as the road narrows and climbs into the hills. The Tukituki River is one of the most beautiful in the region, winding through a valley of sheep stations and limestone outcrops. At Patangata, dramatic pale limestone bluffs rise above the river, catching golden light in the late afternoon. Further inland, the valley narrows and the Ruahine Ranges loom ahead, their summits often cloud-wrapped. This is renowned trout fishing country — the Tukituki and its tributaries hold good populations of brown and rainbow trout. The road eventually becomes gravel and enters the Makaroro Valley, where the bush closes in and the farming thins out. If you have time, walk to the Sunrise Hut in the Ruahine Ranges from the road end — a three-hour climb to an alpine hut with panoramic views. The Tukituki Valley is Hawke's Bay beyond the cellar doors, and it is quietly magnificent.

name: Waimarama Beach Surf
short: Surf uncrowded beach breaks at one of Hawke's Bay's premier surf beaches.
commentary: Waimarama is a popular surf beach south of Hastings with consistent beach breaks on easterly and southeasterly swells. The beach has a surf lifesaving club and is patrolled in summer. It offers more reliable swell than the sheltered Napier beaches.

reviews.summary: The Waimarama Beach Surf adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Surf uncrowded beach breaks at one of Hawke's Bay's premier surf beaches. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Waimarama Beach, one of Hawke's Bay's premier surf spots and a welcome contrast to the calmer waters further north. This exposed beach catches swell from the east and southeast, producing fun, punchy beach breaks that work across a range of tide states. The beauty of surfing here is the lack of crowds, unlike the packed lineups of the Bay of Plenty or Raglan, you will often share the waves with just a handful of locals. The Waimarama Surf Lifesaving Club has patrolled this beach since 1933, and the lifeguards can point you to the best banks on any given day. After your session, the beach itself is stunning, a golden crescent backed by farmland-covered hills. The small settlement has a store and takeaways, but not much else, which is exactly the point.

name: Wairoa & Mahia Coast
short: A small river town with a strong Māori majority, a lighthouse on the riverbank, and the gateway to Mahia.
commentary: Wairoa sits on the Wairoa River. Population around 4,500. The Portland Island lighthouse was relocated to the riverbank in 1961.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Wairoa & Mahia Coast — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A small river town with a strong Māori majority, a lighthouse on the riverbank, and the gateway to Mahia. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have arrived in Wairoa, a small river town in the northern Hawke's Bay, and you should slow down enough to actually notice it. The town sits on a wide brown river that loops once around the centre and then runs out to the sea four kilometres east. The population is around four and a half thousand and is one of the few in the country that is more than half Māori, mostly Ngāti Kahungunu and Ngāi Tāmanuhiri. The town carries that identity quietly and confidently. Walk along the river esplanade and you will pass several pou whenua, modern Māori sculptures, and the old Portland Island Lighthouse, a white wooden tower that was lifted from its original site on Portland Island in nineteen sixty one and reerected here as a riverside landmark. Wairoa is also the western gateway to the Māhia Peninsula, with its swimming beaches, surf breaks, and the Rocket Lab launch site at the southern tip. To the west the road climbs into the deep bush of Te Urewera and Lake Waikaremoana. Wairoa itself is a place travellers tend to drive through. Stop. Have a pie. Walk the river. The town will reward your attention.

name: Hawke's Bay Wine Country Cycle Trail
short: Cycle between world-class wineries on flat, scenic trails through New Zealand's oldest wine region.
commentary: The Hawke's Bay Trails network covers over 200 km of cycling and walking paths linking wineries, orchards, and coastal areas. Hawke's Bay is one of New Zealand's premier wine regions, particularly noted for Bordeaux-style reds and syrah. The Landscapes Ride connects multiple cellar doors on flat, easy trails.

reviews.summary: The Hawke's Bay Wine Country Cycle Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cycle between world-class wineries on flat, scenic trails through New Zealand's oldest wine region. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are pedalling through the heart of New Zealand's oldest wine region on the Hawke's Bay Trails, a network of over 200 kilometres of flat, easy cycling paths. The Landscapes Ride is the classic wine-tasting route, linking some of the region's best cellar doors through vineyards and orchards on the Heretaunga Plains. Hawke's Bay has been producing wine since 1851, and today it is best known for its rich, Bordeaux-style reds — cabernet sauvignon, merlot, and especially syrah, which thrives in the warm, dry climate. Stop at Craggy Range for their pinot noir with views of Te Mata Peak, or at Mission Estate, the country's oldest winery, established by Marist missionaries in 1851. The trails pass through avenues of plane trees, alongside the Ngaruroro River, and through the charming village of Havelock North. Cycling between wineries is both the responsible and the most enjoyable way to experience this region — and the flat terrain means even well-lunched riders will manage just fine.

name: Heretaunga Plains — Māori Agricultural Heartland
short: The fertile Heretaunga Plains were densely settled by Ngāti Kahungunu for centuries.
commentary: Ngāti Kahungunu cultivated kūmara, fern root, and other crops. The coast and rivers provided abundant seafood and eels.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Heretaunga Plains — Māori Agricultural Heartland as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The fertile Heretaunga Plains were densely settled by Ngāti Kahungunu for centuries. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Cape Kidnappers / Te Kauwae-a-Māui
short: The dramatic promontory where Cook was chased off in 1769 — and where Māui pulled up the North Island.
commentary: Cook named the cape after Te Aitanga-a-Hauiti attempted to seize a Tahitian crewmember. Māori name Te Kauwae-a-Māui refers to Māui''s fish hook.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Cape Kidnappers / Te Kauwae-a-Māui — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The dramatic promontory where Cook was chased off in 1769 — and where Māui pulled up the North Island. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are looking at Cape Kidnappers, the long dramatic white cliff promontory that closes the southern end of Hawke''s Bay. Māori call this place Te Kauwae a Māui, the jawbone of Māui, because in the foundational tradition, when the great ancestor Māui fished up the North Island from his canoe, the fish hook caught in what is now the eastern horn of the North Island. This promontory is the eastern arc of that ancient shape. Cook''s name for the place comes from an incident on the fifteenth of October, seventeen sixty nine. While the Endeavour was briefly anchored in the bay, a local party of Te Aitanga a Hauiti came out in waka, and in the course of a trading exchange, several attempted to seize a young Tahitian crew member who was working as interpreter. The crew fired muskets, killed one Māori, and the attempt failed. Cook sailed on, noting the cape in his journal as the place of the kidnappers. The modern cape is most famous for one of the largest mainland colonies of Australasian gannets in the world, around twenty thousand breeding pairs on the tiered ledges of the cliffs. Accessible by guided tractor tour, on foot along the beach at low tide, or by a private walking track above.

name: Clive — Battle of Hauraki, 1857
short: A small memorial in a small town — the site of an 1857 Māori-on-Māori skirmish that shaped the early colonial relationship with Heretaunga.
commentary: In 1857 a boundary dispute between Ngāti Kahungunu hapū and a visiting Waikato party ended in a short battle at Pākōwhai near Clive.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Clive — Battle of Hauraki, 1857 — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A small memorial in a small town — the site of an 1857 Māori-on-Māori skirmish that shaped the early colonial relationship with Heretaunga. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Clive, a small settlement on the Ngaruroro River between Napier and Hastings, and near a memorial that marks an episode from eighteen fifty seven that is rarely told in the general histories. A visiting Waikato war party had come to Hawke''s Bay partly for trade and partly to reassert older land claims. Tensions escalated with local Ngāti Kahungunu hapū, and on a February day a short but sharp engagement took place on the river flats near what is now the settlement of Pākōwhai. The deaths were not large in number, perhaps fewer than twenty on both sides combined, but the skirmish became politically significant because it happened in the presence of a number of colonial officials and missionaries who watched, took notes, and reported back to Governor Gore Browne. The report directly shaped the Crown''s later land purchase policy in Hawke''s Bay, since the Crown presented itself as the sole legitimate keeper of the peace between iwi. The memorial today, a simple stone with a plaque, stands near the road. A small site with a big footnote. Slow down, read the plaque, and keep going.

name: MTG Hawke''s Bay, Napier
short: The regional museum, art gallery and theatre of Hawke''s Bay — with outstanding Ngāti Kahungunu taonga and earthquake exhibits.
commentary: Museum Theatre Gallery, Napier. Major taonga collection includes the carved waka taua Taimutu. Earthquake exhibit is national in quality.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at MTG Hawke''s Bay, Napier — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The regional museum, art gallery and theatre of Hawke''s Bay — with outstanding Ngāti Kahungunu taonga and earthquake exhibits. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at MTG Hawke''s Bay, the combined museum, theatre and art gallery in central Napier. It sits on Marine Parade and occupies a striking modernist building opened in twenty thirteen alongside the preserved shell of the original nineteen thirties Masonic Hall. The museum holds one of the finest regional collections of Ngāti Kahungunu taonga anywhere, including the carved waka taua Taimutu and a number of whakairo whole panels rescued from abandoned marae in the early twentieth century. The permanent gallery on the nineteen thirty one Hawke''s Bay earthquake is outstanding, and in terms of public history is among the best single disaster exhibits in the country. It opens with a simulated three minute shake in a darkened room, followed by contemporary newsreel footage, survivor oral histories, and a walk through display of the reconstruction that created the art deco city outside. A separate gallery rotates contemporary Māori and Pākehā art. Allow at least two hours. If you are only going to visit one museum in Hawke''s Bay, this is the one, and the café is good enough to extend the visit into lunch. Excellent orientation before any deeper exploration of the region''s landscape and stories.

name: Napier Prison
short: New Zealand''s oldest prison — opened 1862 and closed 1993, now run as an audio-guided heritage experience.
commentary: Napier Prison opened 1862 on Bluff Hill. Housed some of NZ''s most notorious prisoners including murderers executed in its courtyard. Closed 1993. Now audio tours.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Napier Prison — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. New Zealand''s oldest prison — opened 1862 and closed 1993, now run as an audio-guided heritage experience. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Napier Prison, the oldest former prison in New Zealand. Built in eighteen sixty two, perched on the side of Bluff Hill above the port, the prison held male and sometimes female prisoners for one hundred and thirty years before finally closing in nineteen ninety three. The early decades were brutal. In the nineteen hundreds the prison was the site of several state executions, carried out in a small courtyard that visitors can still walk through. Over the years the walls held some of New Zealand''s most notorious criminals and also some quite ordinary people convicted of quite ordinary crimes. Several of the original small cells in the oldest wing have been preserved as they were when the institution was functioning, complete with the graffiti left by the prisoners who lived and died in them. Self guided audio tours run daily. The narrator is a former guard, and the tour moves through the exercise yard, the isolation cells, the hanging yard, and a reconstruction of the governor''s office. There is a deliberate heaviness to the atmosphere, not manufactured. If you have limited time in Napier, pair this with a walk through the art deco quarter, since the same nineteen thirty one earthquake that destroyed the city also cracked the prison''s outer walls, and the repairs you see today are part of that same aftermath.

name: Ōmarunui Battle Site
short: Where colonial forces and pro-government Māori pre-emptively attacked a Pai Mārire party in 1866 — one of the least-known battles of the NZ Wars.
commentary: 12 October 1866. Colonial forces and Ngāti Kahungunu under Renata Kawepo attacked a Hauhau party of ~120 at Ōmarunui. About 23 were killed; the rest taken prisoner.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ōmarunui Battle Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where colonial forces and pro-government Māori pre-emptively attacked a Pai Mārire party in 1866 — one of the least-known battles of the NZ Wars. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are at Ōmarunui, a small farm settlement near Taradale that on the twelfth of October, eighteen sixty six, was the site of one of the less commonly taught engagements of the New Zealand Wars. A party of around one hundred and twenty Pai Mārire followers had been travelling through inland Hawke''s Bay, apparently hoping to rally support among local iwi. The colonial government, concerned at the movement''s spread and acting on intelligence from Ngāti Kahungunu leaders who opposed Pai Mārire, decided to intercept the party before it reached Napier. A combined force of colonial militia and Ngāti Kahungunu warriors under the senior Hawke''s Bay rangatira Renata Kawepo moved on the camp before dawn. The engagement was short. Around twenty three of the Pai Mārire were killed. Most of the rest were taken prisoner and sent into exile on the Chatham Islands, joining the group that would later include Te Kooti. Among the colonial dead was the son of a prominent Napier merchant. Today the site is a quiet roadside reserve with an interpretive pou. A small engagement, but one that illustrates how complex the New Zealand Wars were, with Māori fighting on both sides of the colonial divide for their own reasons.

name: Te Mata Peak
short: The sleeping giant chief of Heretaunga — a 399m ridge with one of the great views and a great love story.
commentary: Te Mata is the ancestor chief of Waimārama. The ridge is said to be his sleeping body after he attempted to eat through the mountain for love of Hinerākau.

reviews.summary: Te Mata Peak draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The sleeping giant chief of Heretaunga — a 399m ridge with one of the great views and a great love story. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are on Te Mata Peak, the dramatic limestone ridge that rises three hundred and ninety nine metres above the Heretaunga Plains. The view is deservedly one of the most famous in Hawke''s Bay, reaching across the orchards and vineyards to Napier and Cape Kidnappers on a clear day. The Māori story of this ridge is a love story. Te Mata was a rangatira of Waimārama iwi on the coast, powerful in battle and handsome. The daughter of an enemy chief, a young woman named Hinerākau, was promised to him in marriage to secure peace between their peoples. Her father, still resentful, set an impossible bride price. Te Mata was to eat a path directly through the hills between the two iwi. Driven by love, he set to work. He had eaten part of the ridge when the task killed him. His body lies along the ridge today. The peak is his forehead, the long slope is his chest, and the drop into Havelock North is his feet. Hinerākau, in the oldest version of the story, threw herself from the cliff in grief. The whole country holds him. On the drive up, in the wind at the lookout, keep that story in mind as you look around.

name: Waipatu Marae, Hastings
short: Where the Māori Parliament movement was founded in 1892 — a landmark in nineteenth-century Māori politics.
commentary: Waipatu Marae hosted the first Kotahitanga Māori Parliament in June 1892, a pan-tribal political assembly that met annually into the early 1900s.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Waipatu Marae, Hastings as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where the Māori Parliament movement was founded in 1892 — a landmark in nineteenth-century Māori politics. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are passing Waipatu Marae, the principal marae of Ngāti Kahungunu in the Hastings area, and the birthplace of one of the great experiments in nineteenth century Māori politics. In June eighteen ninety two, rangatira from iwi across the North Island gathered at Waipatu for the first session of the Kotahitanga Māori Parliament, also called Te Paremata Māori. The parliament met annually for over a decade, rotating between marae, and passed its own legislation, elected its own members and speaker, and attempted to create a Māori political forum that could negotiate with the Crown on equal terms. The driving idea, rooted in Article Two of the Treaty of Waitangi, was tino rangatiratanga, the exercise of full Māori self government over Māori affairs. The colonial government never formally recognised Te Paremata Māori, and by the early nineteen hundreds the movement had declined in influence. But it left a lasting imprint on Māori political thinking. Many of the ideas and relationships built at Kotahitanga flowed directly into the later Young Māori Party of Āpirana Ngata and Māui Pōmare. Pass Waipatu with respect. This is a marae where the modern Māori political movement was, in a real sense, first born.

name: Pukeora Pā & CHB Settler Country
