short: The ancient pā above Waipukurau — home to generations of Ngāti Kahungunu before the Waipukurau Purchase of 1851.
commentary: Pukeora Pā sits above Waipukurau township. The Waipukurau Purchase (1851) transferred 279,000 acres of Central Hawke''s Bay to the Crown.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pukeora Pā & CHB Settler Country as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The ancient pā above Waipukurau — home to generations of Ngāti Kahungunu before the Waipukurau Purchase of 1851. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are at Pukeora, the prominent rounded hill above Waipukurau in Central Hawke''s Bay, and one of the great pre European pā of Ngāti Kahungunu in the Tamatea region. Terraces cut into the slopes by generations of occupiers are still visible, and the central defended platform on the summit is thought to date back at least four hundred years. The surrounding country, the rolling sheep and crop country of modern Central Hawke''s Bay, was known to Māori as Tamatea, and was among the richest food producing regions in the South Pacific before the nineteenth century. In April eighteen fifty one, rangatira of the area signed the Waipukurau Purchase, transferring around two hundred and seventy nine thousand acres to the Crown for a total price of four and a half thousand pounds. It was one of the largest land transactions in the North Island. Within a decade, most of the rich flats had been converted to vast sheep runs under a small number of pākehā leaseholders, and the economic dislocation to the iwi was profound. Drive the back roads around Waipukurau and Takapau. Look up to the hills. Every one of them is a pā, and each carries a name and a story that predate the sheep by centuries.

name: Wairoa — Museum & Māori History
short: The northernmost town of Hawke''s Bay — gateway to Tūhoe country and a district with its own deep Māori history.
commentary: Wairoa is the principal town of the northern Ngāti Kahungunu rohe and the coastal gateway to Tūhoe and Te Urewera. Local museum on the river.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Wairoa — Museum & Māori History as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The northernmost town of Hawke''s Bay — gateway to Tūhoe country and a district with its own deep Māori history. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You have arrived at Wairoa, the principal town of the northern Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. This is a smaller, quieter, and more distinctly Māori community than the larger centres further south. The Wairoa River runs straight through the middle of the town before emptying into the Pacific a short distance away, and from here State Highway thirty eight runs inland into the remote forested heart of Tūhoe country and Te Urewera. The Wairoa Museum, housed in a small former bank building near the river, tells the local story well. Exhibits cover the iwi history of the district, the coastal whaling and early missionary contact period, the central North Island land wars and the campaigns against Te Kooti, the difficult twentieth century history of land confiscations and returns, and the small town farming and forestry culture that has persisted since. Many of the families you will see in Wairoa carry names that go directly back to the founding families of the town. The historic Presbyterian church near the river dates from the eighteen nineties. The wooden wharf on the river was used into living memory for coastal steamers. Take thirty minutes. Drive the main street. Visit the museum. And then continue.

name: Heretaunga Tamatea Settlement Pou, Ahuriri
short: Pou commemorating the 2016 settlement — and the long story of how most of Heretaunga passed out of Ngāti Kahungunu hands.
commentary: The Heretaunga Tamatea settlement with the Crown (2016) addressed 19th century land loss including the 1851 Ahuriri Purchase and the 1867 Heretaunga block.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Heretaunga Tamatea Settlement Pou, Ahuriri as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Pou commemorating the 2016 settlement — and the long story of how most of Heretaunga passed out of Ngāti Kahungunu hands. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are at a memorial pou commemorating the Heretaunga Tamatea Treaty settlement of twenty sixteen. The Crown''s formal apology to the iwi and hapū of Heretaunga and Tamatea acknowledged a long and complicated sequence of nineteenth century land loss. In eighteen fifty one the Ahuriri Purchase transferred around two hundred and sixty five thousand acres, much of modern Napier included, to the Crown for a total of one thousand five hundred pounds. In eighteen sixty seven the Heretaunga block of one hundred and seventy thousand acres was sold to a small syndicate of ten settler landowners in one of the most controversial individual deals of the colonial era, after those landowners had extended credit to individual Māori and called in debts at critical moments. By the eighteen eighties most of the best lowland in Hawke''s Bay had passed out of Māori ownership. The twenty sixteen settlement included a Crown apology, a financial redress of around one hundred million dollars, and the return of a number of culturally significant sites including several important maunga. The pou itself, with carvings that represent the twenty one hapū of the settlement, stands at Ahuriri. Stop, read it, and understand how the landscape around you came to look as it does.

name: Lake Waikaremoana — The Sea of Rippling Waters
short: A sacred lake formed by a taniwha — the Great Walk circles a lake that Tūhoe consider an ancestor.
commentary: In Tūhoe tradition, the lake was formed when Haumapuhia was turned into a taniwha. The 2014 Te Urewera Act gave the area legal personhood — the first in the world.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Lake Waikaremoana — The Sea of Rippling Waters as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A sacred lake formed by a taniwha — the Great Walk circles a lake that Tūhoe consider an ancestor. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ōmarunui Battle Site
short: Site of the 1866 battle — a Pai Mārire gathering attacked by colonial forces, survivors imprisoned without trial.
commentary: Colonial forces attacked Pai Mārire followers. Survivors — men, women and children — were arrested and imprisoned on the Chatham Islands without trial.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ōmarunui Battle Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Site of the 1866 battle — a Pai Mārire gathering attacked by colonial forces, survivors imprisoned without trial. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ōtātara Pā Historic Reserve
short: One of NZ's most impressive archaeological sites — 33 hectares of pā terraces, ditches, and storage pits.
commentary: The complex includes two main pā — Ōtātara and Hikurangi — with extensive terracing, defensive ditches, and hundreds of storage pits. Walking tracks wind through with interpretation panels.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ōtātara Pā Historic Reserve as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of NZ's most impressive archaeological sites — 33 hectares of pā terraces, ditches, and storage pits. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Mata o Rongokako
short: The sleeping giant — Te Mata Peak is the body of the ancestor Rongokako who was tricked into eating through the hillside.
commentary: The ridgeline is the body of Rongokako who was challenged to eat through the range. He failed and his body became the hill. The profile of the sleeping giant is clearly visible from Havelock North.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Mata o Rongokako as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The sleeping giant — Te Mata Peak is the body of the ancestor Rongokako who was tricked into eating through the hillside. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waimārama — Ancestral Coastline
short: A Māori coastal settlement with cultural tours offering insights into the living heritage landscape.
commentary: The local hapū maintain an active marae and Waimārama Māori Tours offers visitors insights into fishing grounds, gardening sites, and wāhi tapu.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Waimārama — Ancestral Coastline as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A Māori coastal settlement with cultural tours offering insights into the living heritage landscape. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Welcome to Heretaunga
short: A short cultural welcome to the Heretaunga plains — the rohe of Ngāti Kahungunu.
commentary: PLACEHOLDER — do not ship until reviewed with Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Inc. Intended as a respectful, general-level welcome: 'You are entering the rohe of Ngāti Kahungunu, one of Aotearoa's largest iwi. The plains from Takapau to the Mohaka are Heretaunga — named for the bindings of a great canoe. We'll point out sites as we pass them; we won't tell stories that aren't ours to tell.'

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Welcome to Heretaunga as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A short cultural welcome to the Heretaunga plains — the rohe of Ngāti Kahungunu. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Lake Tūtira
short: The lake that became a book — Herbert Guthrie-Smith's forty-year study of one sheep station changed how New Zealand thinks about land.
commentary: Herbert Guthrie-Smith farmed this valley from 1882 to 1940, and kept notes the whole time — weather, birds, rainfall, erosion, flood, introduced species, everything. He published it in 1921 as *Tutira: the story of a New Zealand sheep station*: roughly 600 pages, a foundational work of New Zealand ecology. The 1953 edition added a chapter on the 1931 earthquake. The lake itself has been reshaped since — the earthquake altered drainage, and both Cyclone Bola (1988) and Cyclone Gabrielle (2023) dumped serious silt. The short walkway to the water's edge takes ten minutes; the longer Tūtira Walkway up into regenerating bush is ~2 hrs return, on land Guthrie-Smith himself replanted.

reviews.summary: Lake Tūtira draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The lake that became a book — Herbert Guthrie-Smith's forty-year study of one sheep station changed how New Zealand thinks about land. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Māhia Peninsula / Te Māhia-mai-tawhiti
short: A thousand-year-old waka landing site that now launches rockets — the rohe of Rongomaiwahine.
commentary: Te Māhia-mai-tawhiti — 'the sound heard from a distance' — is the homeland of Ngāti Rongomaiwahine, who descend from the high-ranking ancestor Rongomaiwahine through both the Tākitimu and Kurahaupō canoes. Tradition holds that Whatonga, grandson of the navigator Toi, settled here after arriving from Hawaiki. Centuries later a brief shore-whaling industry ran from the west coast (1830s). In 2016 Rocket Lab, leasing land from the Māori-owned Tawapata South Incorporation, built Launch Complex 1 on the southern tip. On 21 January 2018 *Still Testing* reached orbit — the first orbital launch from New Zealand, and the first orbital launch from a private spaceport anywhere. Same peninsula. Very different centuries.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Māhia Peninsula / Te Māhia-mai-tawhiti as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A thousand-year-old waka landing site that now launches rockets — the rohe of Rongomaiwahine. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mission Estate / La Grande Maison
short: New Zealand's oldest winery — French Marist missionaries since 1851, through two relocations, a flood, and the 1931 earthquake.
commentary: French Marist missionaries arrived in Hawke's Bay in 1851 with vines for sacramental wine, establishing their first site at Pakowhai. Inter-iwi conflict forced a move to Meeanee in 1858, and a later flood drove the mission uphill to Greenmeadows in 1909 — the seminary building itself cut into 11 sections, rolled on logs and pulled by traction engines over 5 km across 2 days. Twenty-two years later, on 3 February 1931, the earthquake collapsed the stone chapel while two priests and seven seminarians were at prayer; all nine were killed. There's a memorial on the site. Today Mission Estate is a working cellar door and restaurant, and the annual Mission Concert is one of NZ's biggest outdoor events. A hundred and seventy-five years of wine from the same valley.

reviews.summary: Mission Estate / La Grande Maison delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. New Zealand's oldest winery — French Marist missionaries since 1851, through two relocations, a flood, and the 1931 earthquake. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

name: Mōhaka Viaduct
short: 95 metres tall and 277 metres long — the tallest railway viaduct in Australasia, built across a depression and an earthquake.
commentary: Designed by PWD engineers John Lelliot Cull and William Langston Newnham, construction began in 1930. The Depression halted work in 1931 — the same year the Hawke's Bay earthquake tore through the region and damaged the half-finished line. For five years the unfinished piers sat in the gorge. Work resumed in 1936; the viaduct opened in July 1937, having been completed in about a year once the money returned. It's a Heritage New Zealand Category 1 structure (listing no. 4418) and still carries freight to Gisborne. The best pull-off view is from SH2 at Raupunga. The Mōhaka river below is a taonga river to Ngāti Pāhauwera.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Mōhaka Viaduct — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. 95 metres tall and 277 metres long — the tallest railway viaduct in Australasia, built across a depression and an earthquake. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Bluff Hill Lookout / Mataruahou
short: The hill that rose 2 metres in the 1931 quake — now the best view of the port.
commentary: In 1931 this hill literally grew. The earthquake pushed Mataruahou up by about two metres, and dried out 3,500 hectares of sea floor to create the land Napier airport now sits on. From up here you can see exactly how the coastline changed.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Bluff Hill Lookout / Mataruahou — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The hill that rose 2 metres in the 1931 quake — now the best view of the port. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Marine Parade / Art Deco Napier
short: The world's most complete Art Deco seafront — rebuilt in 30 months after the 1931 quake.
commentary: On February 3rd, 1931, a 7.8 earthquake flattened Napier in two and a half minutes. 256 people died. The rebuild started within weeks — and they chose Art Deco, the newest style in the world at the time. What you see now is almost unchanged from 1933.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Marine Parade / Art Deco Napier — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The world's most complete Art Deco seafront — rebuilt in 30 months after the 1931 quake. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Napier–Taupō Road (SH5)
short: A 145 km mountain highway with a 150-year history — Māori track, Cobb & Co coach road, and twice-rebuilt modern SH5.
commentary: SH5 climbs from Napier to Taupō through some of the North Island's most technically challenging terrain. The line you're driving was first a pre-European Māori inland track, then a surveyed coach road cut through dense forest in the 1870s; Cobb & Co began scheduled coaches on 1 January 1872. A chain of stockades, change stations and hotels grew along it — Te Pōhue, Tarawera, Rangitaiki, Opepe — some still standing, some marked only by graves. Pre-WWII freight companies stashed spare gearboxes at the summits because the climbs were famous for wrecking them. Major realignments in the 1950s — enabled by the then-new technology of aerial photogrammetry — cut the summit of Turangakumu by 100 m. In February 2023, Cyclone Gabrielle closed the entire corridor: washouts at Te Pōhue, the Esk Valley floor under silt, and slip damage the whole length. NZTA has been rebuilding ever since. What you're driving is the latest version of one of NZ's oldest roads.

reviews.summary: The Napier–Taupō Road (SH5) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A 145 km mountain highway with a 150-year history — Māori track, Cobb & Co coach road, and twice-rebuilt modern SH5. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Ocean Beach / Waipuka
short: A wild golden-sand surf beach between Cape Kidnappers and Waimarama — whaling station, shipwreck, and a living Māori community.
commentary: Known locally as Waipuka, this is the rawest coast in Hawke's Bay — long sand, heavy surf, a scatter of baches, few amenities. In 1838, William Morris set up the Rangaiika shore whaling station here. In 1887 the coastal steamer *Go Ahead* wrecked offshore; her iron boiler is still wedged in the rocks at the southern end. Two sea arches at low tide, nesting shags on the cliffs, and one of the stronger rips on the east coast — summer lifeguard patrols only, swim between the flags. The small community here would prefer not to be over-promoted; visit quietly.

reviews.summary: Ocean Beach / Waipuka draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A wild golden-sand surf beach between Cape Kidnappers and Waimarama — whaling station, shipwreck, and a living Māori community. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Shine Falls / Boundary Stream
short: Hawke's Bay's tallest waterfall — 58 metres off a limestone cliff, deep in a DOC Mainland Island where the birds are coming back.
commentary: Shine Falls is a 58-metre horsetail waterfall inside the Boundary Stream Mainland Island — a DOC reserve where intensive predator control (since the late 1990s) has restored bird life to levels you rarely hear on the mainland. The walk from Heays Access Road is about 1 hour 45 minutes each way through kanuka, kawakawa, kōwhai and tītoki, with a shallow pool and long-fin eels at the base of the falls. Cyclone Gabrielle (February 2023) severely damaged the track and bridges; DOC has been reopening sections in stages. Always check the DOC status page before driving out.

reviews.summary: Shine Falls / Boundary Stream draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Hawke's Bay's tallest waterfall — 58 metres off a limestone cliff, deep in a DOC Mainland Island where the birds are coming back. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Taumata­whakatangi­hanga­koauau­o­Tamatea­turi­pukaka­piki­maunga­horo­nuku­pokai­whenua­ki­tana­tahu
short: The hill with the 85-letter name — a whole story compressed into one word, about Tamatea and a kōauau flute.
commentary: A 305-metre hill near Pōrangahau, southern Hawke's Bay. The 85-letter name is the Guinness World Record for longest place name in an English-speaking country. It tells the story of the explorer Tamatea-pōkai-whenua, who after a battle in this area lost his brother and remained here, mourning, playing his kōauau (bone flute) to his beloved. The name is the song. A longer 105-letter version exists in some tellings. The hill itself is on private farmland — the sign at the base is the public experience, and it's enough.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Taumata­whakatangi­hanga­koauau­o­Tamatea­turi­pukaka­piki­maunga­horo­nuku­pokai­whenua­ki­tana­tahu as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The hill with the 85-letter name — a whole story compressed into one word, about Tamatea and a kōauau flute. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Mata Peak / Te Mata o Rongokako
short: The 'face of Rongokako' — a 399m ridge with views from the Ruahines to the Pacific.
commentary: The ridgeline here is the body of the giant Rongokako, lying on his back. The story goes he was challenged to eat his way through the hill to prove his love for a Pakipaki chief's daughter, and the shape of the ridge — head to feet — is where he finally choked. Drive up at sunset for the colour; walk up at dawn for the silence.

reviews.summary: Te Mata Peak / Te Mata o Rongokako draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The 'face of Rongokako' — a 399m ridge with views from the Ruahines to the Pacific. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Te Pōhue
short: Former Cobb & Co coach stop — a small township whose three hotels each came to an unlucky end.
commentary: Te Pōhue is a scattered township around a small lake, about 55 km north of Napier, on the climb before Titiokura Saddle. It was the mid-morning change-of-horses and feed stop for Cobb & Co coaches on the Napier–Taupō run, which launched on 1 January 1872. The first hotel was built c. 1869. The second burnt down in 1897 when sparks from a farmer's burn-off landed on the shingle roof. The third — the Lake Hotel, built 1899 by Joseph King — was destroyed by the 1931 Hawke's Bay earthquake. What's here now is a rural crossroads: school, fire station, homes, and a servo that has saved a lot of drivers on the climb ahead.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Te Pōhue — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Former Cobb & Co coach stop — a small township whose three hotels each came to an unlucky end. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Titiokura Saddle
short: 736 m on the Maungaharuru Range — one of the great roadside views on the North Island.
commentary: The high point of SH5 on the Hawke's Bay side: 736 metres, on the ridgeline of the Maungaharuru Range. East is the entire Hawke's Bay basin down to the Pacific; west is the Kaweka Range and the Mohaka River valley. The saddle sits on the old Māori inland track; missionary William Colenso climbed it in the 1840s, and in the 1870s a colonial stockade was built here as part of the NZ Wars–era chain of outposts along the coaching road. Pre-WWII truckies used to burn out gearboxes on the climb up; transport firms kept spare gearboxes stashed at the summit. A lookout and pull-off are on the ridge.

reviews.summary: Titiokura Saddle draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. 736 m on the Maungaharuru Range — one of the great roadside views on the North Island. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Waipawa
short: Central Hawke's Bay's quiet historic town — 1860s settler heritage, still-working rural New Zealand.
commentary: Laid out in 1860 by runholder Frederick Abbott, who called it Abbottsford — a name the other settlers quietly ignored in favour of the original Māori name, Waipawa. Long before the ford that gave the place its colonial reason to exist, this was a major Ngāti Kahungunu occupation: the plains here are the rohe of Ngāi Toroiwaho and Ngāti Whatuiāpiti, with kainga, gardens and eel weirs along the river. The Central Hawke's Bay land was opened to runholders after Donald McLean's huge 1851 Crown purchases — over 254,000 hectares across three blocks. What makes the town worth a stop now is what didn't happen: Waipawa never boomed, so the 1870s–1920s shopfronts, the old CHB County Council chambers and one of New Zealand's last genuinely in-use country-town pubs are all still here. Two marae on the edge of town — Mataweka and Nohomaiterangi — belong to the same hapū who've been here all along. Coffee at the Red Bridge café.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waipawa — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Central Hawke's Bay's quiet historic town — 1860s settler heritage, still-working rural New Zealand. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Blenheim Wine Country
short: The largest wine region in the country — 27,000 hectares of mostly Sauvignon Blanc between Blenheim and the coast.
commentary: Marlborough produces around 77% of New Zealand's wine by volume, and overwhelmingly Sauvignon Blanc — a style the region effectively invented internationally. The first commercial vines were planted by Montana (now Brancott) in 1973 on land that had been considered too dry for anything but grazing. The current ~27,000 hectares makes this by far NZ's biggest wine region. Cloudy Bay, Brancott, Villa Maria, Cloudy Bay, Dog Point, Seresin — all have cellar doors, most within 15 minutes of Blenheim. Cycle the vineyards: hire bikes, follow the flat Wairau Valley Wine Trail.

reviews.summary: Blenheim Wine Country delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. The largest wine region in the country — 27,000 hectares of mostly Sauvignon Blanc between Blenheim and the coast. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

name: Edwin Fox Maritime Museum
short: The ninth-oldest ship in the world — an 1853 teak merchantman that carried convicts, soldiers, and migrants.
commentary: The *Edwin Fox* was launched in Bengal in 1853. Over 70 years of service, she carried cargo to Britain, convicts to Australia (1858–59), troops to the Crimea, and nine loads of British and Scandinavian migrants to New Zealand (1870s). She was decommissioned in 1897 and used as a freezer-hulk at Picton until 1965. The present museum preserves what's left: the largely-original teak hull, sitting in a purpose-built dry dock. Only five older wooden ships survive worldwide, and three of them are warships — the *Edwin Fox* is the oldest wooden merchant ship in the world.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Edwin Fox Maritime Museum is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The ninth-oldest ship in the world — an 1853 teak merchantman that carried convicts, soldiers, and migrants. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Havelock
short: 'Greenshell mussel capital of the world' — and the place Ernest Rutherford went to school.
commentary: Havelock sits at the head of the Pelorus Sound. The Marlborough Sounds produce most of the world's farmed green-lipped mussels — *Perna canaliculus* — and Havelock is where they're boated in, processed, and sent worldwide. The town branding is self-aware: 'Mussel capital.' Eat at The Mussel Pot. Secondarily, Havelock is where Ernest Rutherford attended primary school (his family moved here in 1883, when he was 12); the small museum has a modest exhibit. Also the departure point for Pelorus Sound mail-boat cruises.

reviews.summary: Havelock delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. 'Greenshell mussel capital of the world' — and the place Ernest Rutherford went to school. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

name: Kenepuru Sound
short: A 70 km one-lane road to nowhere, along the edge of one of the quietest Sounds.
commentary: Kenepuru Road is 70 km of winding, single-lane, partly-sealed coastal road — one of the most beautiful dead-ends in New Zealand. It follows the edge of Kenepuru Sound out to Portage and beyond. There's one small grocery store in the middle, two small lodges, some baches, and a lot of water. Fill your tank in Havelock. Add 2 hours to any estimate. The road is frequently closed by slips; the 2021 storms damaged it badly and sections are still being repaired. Worth it for the sense of being somewhere genuinely remote.

reviews.summary: The Kenepuru Sound route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A 70 km one-lane road to nowhere, along the edge of one of the quietest Sounds. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Cloudy Bay Shore Whaling

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Cloudy Bay Shore Whaling — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Cloudy Bay, the wide shallow bay that opens onto the eastern end of the Wairau plain. In the eighteen twenties and thirties this was one of the earliest sites of European settlement in the South Island, with shore whaling stations operating along the beaches south of the Wairau bar. Whaling crews lived alongside local Māori, often marrying into Rangitāne and Ngāti Toa families and raising mixed-heritage children who went on to trade and farm in the region. The oil of right whales and sperm whales was rendered in try-pots on the sand, a hard and stinking business. Whalers like John Guard at Kakapo Bay and Jacky Guard's enterprises further north pioneered European settlement before any formal colonisation. Today Cloudy Bay is famous for sauvignon blanc and long flat stony beaches, but the stonework of old try-pots and whalers' graves can still be found if you know where to look. Look at the lagoon, the low dunes, and the Wairau Bar, and remember that European settlement on this coast began not with farms or churches but with harpoons.

name: French Pass (Te Aumiti)

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at French Pass (Te Aumiti) — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at French Pass, the narrow and wildly tidal gap between D'Urville Island and the South Island mainland. In eighteen twenty seven the French navigator Jules Dumont d'Urville threaded his ship Astrolabe through this channel, the first European to do so. The tidal race here can run at eight knots and swirl in whirlpools that can spin a small boat. The Māori name Te Aumiti refers to a famous local taniwha, Kaikai-a-waro, said to guide and protect waka through the passage. A small settlement sits at the land end of the pass, and a viewing platform looks directly down into the churning water. Pelorus Jack, a Risso's dolphin who between eighteen eighty eight and nineteen twelve escorted ships through the nearby Pelorus Sound, became world famous, and was the first individual marine creature protected by law in any country. Sit at the viewing platform, watch the water tear through the gap, and appreciate how this speck of ocean has been a guidepost for navigators Māori and European alike.

name: Havelock and the Two Nobel Laureates

reviews.summary: Havelock and the Two Nobel Laureates delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

audio_story: You are in Havelock, a small green town at the head of the Pelorus Sound, famous for green-lipped mussels and for being the schooling place of two of the country's greatest scientists. Ernest Rutherford, who would later split the atom and win the nineteen oh eight Nobel Prize in Chemistry, attended Havelock School as a boy. Decades later William Pickering, who would become director of the Jet Propulsion Laboratory and lead the first American missions to the outer planets, attended the same school. Two Nobel scale minds from a village in the Marlborough Sounds. The old Havelock School building still stands, and a small museum in town tells their story. Rutherford's family home at nearby Brightwater is another pilgrimage site. Havelock is also the launching point for the Pelorus Sound mail boat, which still delivers groceries and post to remote bays. Stop at the bronze statue of the young Rutherford, admire the simple timber school, and remember that world-changing curiosity can start in an ordinary classroom with a clever, well-taught child.

name: Perano Whaling Station Arapaoa Island

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Perano Whaling Station Arapaoa Island — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Fishing Bay on Arapaoa Island, the last commercial whaling station in New Zealand. The Perano family began shore-based whaling here in nineteen eleven, using high-speed chase boats to pursue humpback whales that migrated through Cook Strait each winter. The family killed, towed, and rendered whales on the beach for nearly five decades. By the nineteen sixties humpback numbers were collapsing. The station finally closed in nineteen sixty four, the last commercial catch in the country. Today the old corrugated sheds, try-pots, and slipways remain on the bay, protected as a heritage site. Humpbacks are slowly returning to the strait. A memorial walk explains the operation and its end. As you read the panels, think about how this steep beach, once thick with the smell of whale oil and the sound of flensing knives, now sees the same species swimming past the shore as tourist boats try to spot them. Arapaoa is a place where extraction ended and recovery may yet succeed.

name: Edwin Fox Museum Picton

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Edwin Fox Museum Picton is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You are at the Edwin Fox Museum in Picton, home to the oldest surviving merchant sailing ship in the world. Built in eighteen fifty three in Calcutta of Burmese teak, the Edwin Fox carried British troops to the Crimean War, then transported convicts to Australia, before taking migrant families, including Irish and Scottish settlers, to Lyttelton and Picton in the eighteen seventies. She ended her long life as a freezer hulk at Picton, where her original refrigeration equipment helped launch the frozen meat trade that transformed New Zealand's economy. When frozen meat exports began in eighteen eighty two, ships like this made it possible to feed British cities from South Island farms. The Edwin Fox was nearly lost to rot and demolition, but a local trust saved her. Today her timbers lie in a specially built dry dock, where you can walk her decks and smell the old hold. Stand below her bow, put your hand on her planking, and imagine troops, convicts, migrants, and frozen lamb carcasses, all lives this one ship has carried.

name: Ship Cove (Meretoto)

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Ship Cove (Meretoto) — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Meretoto, known to Europeans as Ship Cove, at the northern end of Queen Charlotte Sound. Between seventeen seventy and seventeen seventy seven Captain Cook visited this sheltered bay five times on his three Pacific voyages, using it as a base to resupply with water, timber, and wild greens, and to meet with local Māori. The cove's steep bush walls were quiet, its water deep enough for large ships, and the streams clear. Cook planted gardens here of cabbage and turnip, the first European vegetables deliberately grown in New Zealand. He met with local chiefs and established a pattern of cautious trade and diplomacy. A monument raised in nineteen thirteen marks his landings, and a paepae carved with the waka Kurahaupō stands as a meeting place of peoples. Te Ātiawa o Te Waka a Māui hold mana whenua here and share the story. Ship Cove is also the start or end of the Queen Charlotte Track. Stand on the beach, listen for tui, and picture a wooden ship riding at anchor in this bay two and a half centuries ago.

name: Te Rauparaha's Southern Raids

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Rauparaha's Southern Raids as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are on the Wairau plain, across which in the eighteen twenties and eighteen thirties the Ngāti Toa war chief Te Rauparaha swept with devastating effect. After leading his iwi south from Kawhia to Kapiti, Te Rauparaha obtained muskets and used them to strike deep into the South Island, capturing Kāpiti and then crossing the strait to raid Rangitāne, Ngāti Māmoe, and Ngāi Tahu settlements. His campaigns reached as far south as Kaikōura and the Canterbury plains. The demographic effects were severe. Whole communities were displaced or killed, while survivors fled into the hills. By the time Europeans began buying land in Marlborough, Ngāti Toa held rights of conquest over much of it, which is why Te Rauparaha could speak for the Wairau in eighteen forty three. These raids are remembered in many iwi, a shaping reality that preceded the colonial era. Look across the Wairau plain today with new eyes. The quiet farmland you see hides the tracks of musket war parties who reshaped the whole southern North Island and upper South Island within a generation.

name: Tua Marina Monument and Wairau Affray

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Tua Marina Monument and Wairau Affray — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Tua Marina, a quiet stream crossing north of Blenheim. Here on the seventeenth of June eighteen forty three the first serious armed conflict between Māori and the British Crown after the Treaty of Waitangi took place. Surveyors from the New Zealand Company had begun laying out sections on the Wairau plains, ignoring Ngāti Toa chief Te Rauparaha's clear statement that the land had not been sold. A party of armed settler constables and magistrates, led by Police Magistrate Henry Thompson, attempted to arrest Te Rauparaha and Te Rangihaeata at their camp. A shot was fired, Te Rangihaeata's wife Rongo was killed, and the ensuing fight left twenty two of the settlers and four Māori dead. A stone memorial erected in eighteen sixty nine on the small hill at Tua Marina records the names of those who died. Governor FitzRoy later concluded the settlers had acted unlawfully, a decision that outraged colonists but prevented wider war. Stand at the monument and remember a confrontation that foreshadowed the long land wars to come.

name: Awatere Valley Wine Drive
short: Explore the wilder, less-visited wine valley south of the Wairau producing bold reds and aromatics.
commentary: The Awatere Valley lies south of the main Wairau Valley wine region and offers a drier, more extreme climate that produces distinctive wines, particularly pinot noir, syrah, and aromatic whites. The valley road follows the Awatere River inland past scattered vineyards and high-country sheep stations toward the Molesworth Station.

reviews.summary: The Awatere Valley Wine Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Explore the wilder, less-visited wine valley south of the Wairau producing bold reds and aromatics. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving into the Awatere Valley, and this is Marlborough's wilder side. While the Wairau Valley gets most of the attention and the tour buses, the Awatere sits behind a range of hills in its own microclimate — hotter, drier, and windier. The wines from here have a different character: more concentrated, with a mineral edge that sommeliers get excited about. The road follows the Awatere River past vineyards that give way to high-country stations, and if you keep driving long enough you will reach the boundary of Molesworth, New Zealand's largest farm at 180,000 hectares. This is frontier Marlborough, where wine country meets the wild interior.

name: Blenheim to Picton Drive (SH1)
short: The gateway drive from the ferry terminal through wine country to Marlborough's capital.
commentary: The 28 km drive from Picton to Blenheim on SH1 is the first stretch of road most visitors encounter when arriving in the South Island by ferry. The route descends from the Sounds hills through the Elevation vineyard and into the Wairau Plain. For many, this is their introduction to the Marlborough wine region.

reviews.summary: The Blenheim to Picton Drive (SH1) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. The gateway drive from the ferry terminal through wine country to Marlborough's capital. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving from Picton to Blenheim, and if you have just stepped off the Interislander or Bluebridge ferry, this is your introduction to the South Island. The road climbs out of Picton's harbour through bush-covered hills before dropping into the Wairau Valley, and somewhere along this descent the landscape shifts from maritime to viticultural. The first vineyards appear on the hillsides, and by the time you reach the valley floor, you are surrounded by them. This 28-kilometre drive has converted more visitors to the idea of stopping in Marlborough than any tourism campaign ever could. The vines are impossibly orderly against the wild backdrop of the Richmond Range, and in late summer the grapes hang heavy on the wires, filling the air with a sweetness you can almost taste through the car window.

name: Cullen Point Lookout
short: A fifteen-minute climb above Havelock for the best view over the inner Pelorus Sound — no crowds, a good payoff.
commentary: Cullen Point is a small DOC reserve above Havelock with a 30-minute return walk to a viewing platform over the inner Pelorus.

reviews.summary: Cullen Point Lookout draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A fifteen-minute climb above Havelock for the best view over the inner Pelorus Sound — no crowds, a good payoff. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Cullen Point, a small DOC reserve perched above the small town of Havelock at the head of the Pelorus Sound. Havelock itself is a little fishing town, famous for its green-shell mussels, but most visitors drive through it without stopping. They should stop, because five minutes out of town there is one of the nicest short walks in Marlborough. The Cullen Point track is thirty minutes return, steep in places but never difficult, and climbs through regenerating beech forest to a wooden viewing platform on the top of the ridge. The platform looks almost straight down onto Havelock Marina, where a small forest of fishing boats and pleasure craft float on mirror water, and out across the inner Pelorus Sound with its classic Marlborough pattern of deep drowned valleys stretching away in every direction. On a sunny morning, with mist still burning off the water below, the view is one of the loveliest short-walk rewards in the country. You will often have it to yourself. The track is well graded, easy to follow, and signposted from the main road. There is a small car park at the trailhead. Wear sturdy shoes.

name: Marlborough Sounds Dolphin Cruise
short: Cruise the Marlborough Sounds to see dolphins, seals, and rare king shags.
commentary: Wildlife cruises in the Marlborough Sounds offer encounters with bottlenose dolphins, New Zealand fur seals, blue penguins, and the globally rare king shag. The sheltered waters of Pelorus and Queen Charlotte Sounds provide excellent viewing conditions year-round.

reviews.summary: The Marlborough Sounds Dolphin Cruise adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Cruise the Marlborough Sounds to see dolphins, seals, and rare king shags. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the gateway to the Marlborough Sounds, one of New Zealand's richest marine environments. Your cruise will navigate the sheltered waterways where bottlenose dolphins frequently ride the bow wave, sometimes in pods of twenty or more. The Sounds are the only home of the king shag — a critically rare seabird with a global population of just a few hundred — and your boat will pass close to their nesting colonies on rocky outcrops. New Zealand fur seals bask on sun-warmed rocks, and if you're lucky, a little blue penguin might pop up alongside the boat. The landscape is extraordinary too — endless green headlands, hidden bays, and the deep blue channels between the sounds. With over 1,500 kilometres of coastline packed into a relatively small area, the Sounds are a microcosm of everything that makes New Zealand's coast special.

name: Grovetown Lagoon
short: A restored freshwater lagoon just north of Blenheim — with excellent birdlife and a volunteer-led wetland restoration story.
commentary: Grovetown Lagoon is a relict oxbow of the Wairau River. Since 2001 the Te Whānau Hou Grovetown Lagoon Inc. trust and Marlborough District Council have jointly restored the site.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Grovetown Lagoon earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. A restored freshwater lagoon just north of Blenheim — with excellent birdlife and a volunteer-led wetland restoration story. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

audio_story: You have arrived at Grovetown Lagoon, a kilometre north of Blenheim. Grovetown is a relict oxbow of the Wairau River, left behind when the main river channel shifted centuries ago. For most of the twentieth century the lagoon was neglected, edged with willows, and slowly choking on sediment and introduced pest fish. Then in two thousand and one, a community led restoration project took it on. Two decades of work by volunteers, supported by local iwi, the district council, and various funders, have steadily cleared the exotic trees, replanted tens of thousands of native species along the margins, and slowly restored the water quality. The restoration is ongoing, which is half the interest of visiting. A well formed walking track about two and a half kilometres long loops the lagoon. Birdlife is excellent. Pūkeko, swans, grey teal, Australasian shoveler, and the occasional bittern are all regular. Interpretive boards tell the story of the restoration. Schools come here. Scientists visit from overseas. It is a small, quiet, volunteer driven triumph, and a rare example in New Zealand of a community freshwater wetland that has been properly brought back. Bring binoculars. Walk slowly. The best birdwatching is early in the morning.

name: Helicopter Wine Tour Marlborough
short: Helicopter between Marlborough's finest wineries with aerial vineyard views.
commentary: Helicopter wine tours combine scenic flying over the Marlborough wine region with exclusive cellar door visits at premium wineries. Routes may include flights over the Sounds, the Wither Hills, and vineyard landscapes. These premium experiences include tastings and often a gourmet lunch.

reviews.summary: The Helicopter Wine Tour Marlborough adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Helicopter between Marlborough's finest wineries with aerial vineyard views. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the helipad for what might be the most luxurious wine-tasting experience in New Zealand. Your helicopter will lift off over the patchwork of Marlborough's vineyards — rows of vines stretching to the hills in perfect geometric lines — before landing at exclusive cellar doors that most visitors never reach. Between tastings, you'll fly over the Wither Hills, the Marlborough Sounds, and the vineyard landscapes that produce over 75% of New Zealand's wine. From the air, the scale of the wine industry here is astonishing — hundreds of vineyards quilting the Wairau and Awatere valleys in every shade of green and gold. A gourmet lunch at one of the region's top restaurants rounds out an experience that combines world-class wine, spectacular scenery, and the sheer thrill of helicopter flying. This is Marlborough at its most indulgent.

name: Marlborough Sounds Jet Ski Tour
short: Explore the Marlborough Sounds by jet ski through sheltered bays and channels.
commentary: Guided jet ski tours explore the Marlborough Sounds, covering distances and accessing bays that would take hours by kayak. Tours often include wildlife encounters and stops at remote beaches. The sheltered waterways provide ideal conditions for jet skiing year-round.

reviews.summary: The Marlborough Sounds Jet Ski Tour adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Explore the Marlborough Sounds by jet ski through sheltered bays and channels. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the starting point for a jet ski adventure through the Marlborough Sounds. While kayaking the Sounds is beautiful, jet skiing lets you cover far more ground and access remote bays and channels that would take hours to reach under paddle power. Your guided tour will take you racing across the sheltered waterways, past bush-clad headlands and through narrow channels, with stops at secluded beaches accessible only from the water. The Sounds' 1,500 kilometres of coastline means there's always somewhere new to explore, and your guide knows the best spots for wildlife, scenery, and sheer riding enjoyment. Keep watch for dolphins — they sometimes race alongside the jet skis. The combination of speed, scenery, and the freedom of the open water makes this one of the most exhilarating ways to experience the Sounds.

name: Queen Charlotte Sound Kayaking
short: Kayak the sheltered waters of Queen Charlotte Sound through native bush-clad coves.
commentary: Queen Charlotte Sound offers superb sea kayaking through sheltered waterways, bush-clad headlands, and secluded bays. Multi-day paddle options follow the Queen Charlotte Track with accommodation in lodges or DOC campsites. Dolphins, seals, and king shags are regular sightings.

reviews.summary: The Queen Charlotte Sound Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak the sheltered waters of Queen Charlotte Sound through native bush-clad coves. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Marlborough Sounds, a vast network of drowned river valleys that creates over 1,500 kilometres of sheltered coastline. Queen Charlotte Sound is the jewel of this system, and by kayak you'll explore it the way it was meant to be experienced — silently, intimately, and at water level. Your paddle will take you past bush-clad headlands, into secluded bays where the only footprints on the sand might be a weka's, and through channels where bottlenose dolphins often bow-ride alongside kayaks. The Sounds are home to the rare king shag, found nowhere else on earth, and you'll pass their nesting colonies on rocky outcrops. The water is sheltered and calm, making this suitable for paddlers of all abilities. Multi-day options allow you to kayak sections of the Queen Charlotte Track, staying in lodges or campsites along the way.

name: Meretoto / Ship Cove
short: Cook's favourite NZ anchorage — five visits between 1770 and 1777, trading with local Māori.
commentary: Cook spent over 170 days here across five visits. The cove was an active Māori settlement. A memorial marks the start of the Queen Charlotte Track.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Meretoto / Ship Cove as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Cook's favourite NZ anchorage — five visits between 1770 and 1777, trading with local Māori. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Molesworth Station
short: The largest farm in New Zealand — 180,000 hectares of tussock, river valleys and mountain ranges in the Marlborough backcountry.
commentary: Molesworth is a DOC-managed recreation reserve and working high-country cattle station. Public vehicle access is seasonal, usually late December to Easter.

reviews.summary: Molesworth Station draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The largest farm in New Zealand — 180,000 hectares of tussock, river valleys and mountain ranges in the Marlborough backcountry. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are driving into Molesworth Station, and you are about to spend the day in the largest single farm in New Zealand. Molesworth sprawls across one hundred and eighty thousand hectares of tussock valleys, braided rivers, and high mountain country between the Awatere and Wairau valleys. The road in is long and unsealed, climbing through river gorges, fording small streams, and crossing windswept saddles where the only company may be a harrier hawk hanging on the updraft. Molesworth is a working cattle station, farmed with patience and care for several thousand beef cattle, but it is also a DOC recreation reserve, and the back road from Hanmer Springs to Blenheim through its heart is open to public vehicles only in summer, typically from late December to Easter. There are simple campgrounds, a visitor centre at the main homestead, and a handful of historic farm buildings dating to the eighteen sixties. The scale of the landscape is not quite like anywhere else in the country. Fuel up before you start. Carry water. Drive slowly. The trip takes the better part of a day, and you will not be the same person at the other end.

name: Molesworth Station Road
short: Drive through NZ's largest farm — 180,000 hectares of high-country station open only in summer.
commentary: The Molesworth Road crosses Molesworth Station, New Zealand's largest farm at 180,000 hectares, connecting the Awatere Valley to Hanmer Springs. The road is only open to public vehicles from late December to early March and traverses remote tussock high country at over 1,000 m elevation. The station runs 10,000 cattle and has a dramatic history of ecological degradation and restoration.

reviews.summary: The Molesworth Station Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive through NZ's largest farm — 180,000 hectares of high-country station open only in summer. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving through Molesworth Station, and at 180,000 hectares, you are on the largest farm in New Zealand. The road is only open for about ten weeks each summer — snow and ice close it for the rest of the year — and it feels like driving through the set of a Western. The tussock country rolls away in every direction, golden and vast, with the Inland Kaikoura Range rising to the east. Molesworth has a troubled history: in the early twentieth century, rabbits and overgrazing stripped the land almost bare, and it took decades of DOC management to bring it back. Today, 10,000 cattle graze here in summer, mustered by helicopter and horseback. There are no services, no cell signal, and no shortcuts. Carry water, fuel, and patience.

name: Nydia Track
short: A beautiful two-day bush-and-coast walk from Kaiuma Bay in Pelorus Sound to Nydia Bay and out to Duncan Bay — quiet, remote, and well-benched.
commentary: The Nydia Track is a 27 km two-day DOC walk between Kaiuma Bay and Duncan Bay in the outer Pelorus Sound. Two saddles, a remote bay, and a historic sawmill setting.

reviews.summary: Nydia Track draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A beautiful two-day bush-and-coast walk from Kaiuma Bay in Pelorus Sound to Nydia Bay and out to Duncan Bay — quiet, remote, and well-benched. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are heading for the Nydia Track, a twenty seven kilometre walking track that crosses from Kaiuma Bay on the inner side of the Pelorus Sound over two low forested saddles to the remote Nydia Bay and out again to Duncan Bay. The Nydia Track is everything the Queen Charlotte Track is not. Where the Queen Charlotte is busy with mountain bikes and day walkers and well served with lodges, the Nydia is quiet, often empty, and cuts through country that feels genuinely remote. The standard plan is to tramp in one day from Kaiuma Bay to Nydia Bay, stay at either the DOC hut or the beautiful private Nydia Lodge, and walk out the second day to Duncan Bay. Each day is around six hours. The forest is mature beech on the ridges and regenerating kahikatea in the valleys. Birdlife is excellent, especially bellbird and tui. Nydia Bay itself is one of the quietest inhabited bays in the Marlborough Sounds, with a small community of long-term residents and no road access. Water taxi transfers are available at either end. Carry all your food and water. This is a proper small wilderness walk, only an hour by boat from the main Picton ferry terminal.

name: Pelorus River — Te Hoiere Canoe Route
short: An ancient Māori canoe route connecting the Marlborough Sounds to the interior — part of the pounamu trade network.
commentary: Te Hoiere (Pelorus River) was an important Māori canoe route connecting the sheltered Marlborough Sounds to the interior and across to the West Coast pounamu sources. The river and its tributaries provided a network of water highways through the mountainous terrain.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pelorus River — Te Hoiere Canoe Route as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. An ancient Māori canoe route connecting the Marlborough Sounds to the interior — part of the pounamu trade network. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Pelorus River Kayaking & Hobbit Scene
short: Kayak the crystal-clear Pelorus River — the barrel scene location from The Hobbit.
commentary: The Pelorus River was used to film the barrel-riding scene in The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug. Guided kayak trips explore the remarkably clear river through native bush. The Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve features deep swimming holes and ancient lowland podocarp forest.

reviews.summary: The Pelorus River Kayaking & Hobbit Scene adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak the crystal-clear Pelorus River — the barrel scene location from The Hobbit. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Pelorus River, a waterway so beautifully clear it was chosen by Peter Jackson to film the famous barrel-riding escape scene in The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug. Your kayak trip will take you downstream through crystal-clear pools and gentle rapids framed by native bush that has barely changed since the days of the dinosaurs. The Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve protects some of the finest remaining lowland podocarp forest in the Marlborough region, with towering rimu and kahikatea trees. Between paddling, you'll discover deep swimming holes where the water glows emerald green over smooth river stones. The river is gentle enough for beginners yet interesting enough for experienced paddlers, and the combination of cinematic fame and natural beauty makes it one of the hidden gems of the South Island.

name: Kaipupu Wildlife Sanctuary, Picton
short: A predator-free sanctuary on a peninsula in Queen Charlotte Sound — combining conservation with Māori cultural values.
commentary: Kaipupu Point is a mainland island sanctuary managed in partnership with local iwi. The project reflects the Māori principle of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) applied to modern conservation.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Kaipupu Wildlife Sanctuary, Picton earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. A predator-free sanctuary on a peninsula in Queen Charlotte Sound — combining conservation with Māori cultural values. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

name: Picton to Havelock Drive (SH6)
short: Drive the winding road between Picton and Havelock through the heart of the Marlborough Sounds.
commentary: The 35 km drive from Picton to Havelock on SH6 follows the shores of Grove Arm and Mahakipawa Arm through the Marlborough Sounds. The road winds past mussel farms, native bush, and sheltered bays. Havelock is the 'Greenshell Mussel Capital of the World,' with the Sounds producing over 60% of New Zealand's farmed mussels.

reviews.summary: The Picton to Havelock Drive (SH6) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the winding road between Picton and Havelock through the heart of the Marlborough Sounds. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving from Picton to Havelock, and the road winds through the Marlborough Sounds like it cannot quite make up its mind. Every curve reveals another sheltered bay, another arm of the sea reaching into the hills, and in the water you will see the dark lines of mussel farms stretching across the channels. The Sounds produce over 60 percent of New Zealand's greenshell mussels, and Havelock at the road's end calls itself the Greenshell Mussel Capital of the World. The mussels are farmed on ropes suspended from long lines, and they grow fat and sweet in these clean, plankton-rich waters. In Havelock, a plate of mussels — steamed, or baked with garlic butter — is not just recommended, it is practically compulsory. The town sits at the confluence of the Pelorus and Kaituna rivers, a working fishing village that has resisted the temptation to become quaint.

name: Port Underwood — Whaling & Conflict
short: A sheltered bay with violent early contact history — whaling, raids, and inter-tribal conflict.
commentary: One of the first whaling stations in the Sounds. The bay saw interactions between Māori, whalers, and sealers ranging from peaceful trade to violent conflict.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Port Underwood — Whaling & Conflict as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A sheltered bay with violent early contact history — whaling, raids, and inter-tribal conflict. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Port Underwood Road
short: A remote coastal drive to historic whaling bays on the exposed southern Sounds coast.
commentary: Port Underwood Road winds around the exposed southeastern coast of the Marlborough Sounds to a series of bays that were major whaling stations in the 1820s-1840s. Ocean Bay, Robin Hood Bay, and Oyster Bay all had shore-based whaling operations. The Wairau Affray of 1843, one of the first armed conflicts between Maori and European settlers, took place nearby.

reviews.summary: The Port Underwood Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A remote coastal drive to historic whaling bays on the exposed southern Sounds coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the Port Underwood Road, and this is about as remote as you can get while still being in Marlborough. The road narrows and turns to gravel as it winds around headlands above a wild, south-facing coast. In the 1820s and 30s, these bays were the centre of a brutal whaling industry — Shore whalers from Sydney and London set up stations at Ocean Bay and Robin Hood Bay, hunting southern right whales as they migrated past. The remains of try-works where blubber was rendered can still be found if you know where to look. It was in this area, in 1843, that the Wairau Affray erupted — a confrontation between settlers and Ngati Toa chief Te Rauparaha that left 22 Europeans and 4 Maori dead, one of the first armed clashes of colonisation.

name: Queen Charlotte Track
short: A 70 km coastal track winding through the Marlborough Sounds between Ship Cove and Anakiwa.
commentary: The Queen Charlotte Track covers 70 km from Ship Cove (where Captain Cook landed in 1770) to Anakiwa, typically walked in 3-5 days. The track follows ridgelines between Queen Charlotte Sound and Kenepuru Sound, offering constant water views. It is one of the few major NZ tracks that permits mountain biking on most sections.

reviews.summary: 70km of stunning coastal track above the Marlborough Sounds — rated New Zealand's best multi-day walk by many reviewers for its combination of scenery, accessibility, and the fact you can have your pack water-taxied ahead. The views over the sounds from the ridgeline are breathtaking.

audio_story: You are starting the Queen Charlotte Track at Ship Cove, the very spot where Captain James Cook anchored the Endeavour in January 1770. A water taxi has dropped you at this roadless bay, and for the next 70 kilometres you will follow ridgelines and shorelines through the Marlborough Sounds. The track rises to saddles where you can see water on both sides — Queen Charlotte Sound to the north, Kenepuru Sound to the south — then drops through lush coastal forest to secluded bays. Unlike most of New Zealand's great walks, the Queen Charlotte allows mountain biking on all but one section, and boat transfers mean you can walk it in stages. Each night you descend to the shore, where lodges and campsites sit at the water's edge and the only sounds are birdsong and the gentle lap of the tide.

name: Queen Charlotte Track Mountain Biking
short: Mountain bike the 70 km Queen Charlotte Track through the Marlborough Sounds.
commentary: The Queen Charlotte Track stretches 70 km from Ship Cove to Anakiwa through the Marlborough Sounds. Mountain bikers can ride most of the track (sections restricted seasonally for walkers), with water taxi services providing boat access and luggage transfers. The coastal bush scenery is superb.

reviews.summary: The Queen Charlotte Track Mountain Biking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Mountain bike the 70 km Queen Charlotte Track through the Marlborough Sounds. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the start of one of New Zealand's most scenic mountain bike rides. The Queen Charlotte Track winds 70 kilometres through the Marlborough Sounds, tracing ridgelines and coastline between Ship Cove and Anakiwa. Captain Cook visited Ship Cove five times during his Pacific voyages, and the views from the track's high points explain why — this is a landscape of extraordinary beauty, with forested ridges dropping to turquoise bays in every direction. The riding is mostly intermediate level, with flowing singletrack through native bush, climbs to stunning saddle viewpoints, and descents to secluded bays. Water taxis carry you and your luggage between accommodation options, so you can ride light and enjoy the experience. Allow two to three days for the full track, or choose a day section for a taste of what the Sounds have to offer.

name: Rārangi Beach & Monkey Bay
short: A long empty stony beach north of Blenheim — and the tiny hidden Monkey Bay sea cave at its northern end.
commentary: Rārangi is a grey stone and driftwood beach at the base of the Richmond Range. Monkey Bay is a small rock-walled cove accessed by a short walk.

reviews.summary: Rārangi Beach & Monkey Bay draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A long empty stony beach north of Blenheim — and the tiny hidden Monkey Bay sea cave at its northern end. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are driving out to Rārangi, a long quiet stone beach on the northern side of Cloudy Bay, and you are probably heading for Monkey Bay. Most people do not come all the way to Rārangi just for the main beach, which is a wind swept sweep of grey stones and grey driftwood and sometimes grey sky, dramatic and lonely and not really for swimming. They come for the short walk around the northern headland to Monkey Bay, a tiny hidden cove barely fifty metres across, surrounded by rocky cliffs on three sides. At one end of the cove, a short natural tunnel has been carved through the headland by the sea, and you can walk through it into a small second chamber where the only sound is the sea and the surprised laughter of the next group walking in. At low tide, the pool in the centre of the cove is calm enough to swim in. Time your visit to the tide, bring a picnic, and take the easy short walk up onto the headland for a view back over the whole bay. You can make a very good morning of it. Just be back before the tide turns.

name: Renwick Wine Cycle Trail
short: Flat easy cycling between 20-plus cellar doors in a small area around Renwick — the classic Marlborough wine-tasting day out on two wheels.
commentary: Renwick sits in the heart of the Wairau Valley. Over 20 cellar doors are within a 10 km cycling radius.

reviews.summary: Renwick Wine Cycle Trail delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. Flat easy cycling between 20-plus cellar doors in a small area around Renwick — the classic Marlborough wine-tasting day out on two wheels. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

audio_story: You have arrived in Renwick, a small town ten kilometres west of Blenheim that is the unofficial cycling capital of Marlborough wine country. Renwick's genius is geographical. Within a ten kilometre radius of the town centre, there are more than twenty cellar doors, almost all of them reachable by a dedicated flat cycling network that avoids the main roads. Half a dozen local bike hire companies will set you up with a bike, a helmet, a map, a picnic lunch, and a list of cellar doors to taste at. If you want, they will drop off a car at your final tasting so you do not need to cycle home in a wine-fuelled condition. The trail takes in famous names like Cloudy Bay, Brancott, Yealands, and Allan Scott alongside tiny family producers working out of single tanks. The tasting focus is, of course, Sauvignon Blanc, for which Marlborough is world famous, but many cellar doors also showcase Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and sparkling wine. A full day of cycling and tasting for most fit adults will be around thirty kilometres, with plenty of stops. Book a lunch at one of the vineyard restaurants. Drink plenty of water between tastings. This is one of the great gentle cycling days of New Zealand.

name: Marlborough Sounds Sailing
short: Sail the pristine waterways of the Marlborough Sounds on a chartered yacht.
commentary: The Marlborough Sounds offer some of the best cruising waters in New Zealand, with sheltered anchorages, consistent winds, and stunning bush-clad scenery. Day charters and multi-day sailing trips explore Queen Charlotte and Pelorus Sounds, with options from skippered yachts to bareboat hire for experienced sailors.

reviews.summary: The Marlborough Sounds Sailing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Sail the pristine waterways of the Marlborough Sounds on a chartered yacht. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at one of the finest sailing grounds in the Southern Hemisphere. The Marlborough Sounds offer over 1,500 kilometres of sheltered coastline, consistent winds, and countless pristine anchorages where you can drop the hook and have an entire bay to yourself. Under sail, you'll glide past forested headlands that plunge into deep water, through narrow channels where the wind funnels perfectly, and into hidden bays where the only sounds are birdsong and the gentle lap of water on the hull. Whether you charter a skippered yacht for the day or take a bareboat for a multi-day cruise, the Sounds reward sailors with ever-changing scenery and reliably good conditions. Drop anchor in a quiet cove, dive off the stern for a swim, and watch the sunset paint the hills gold. This is sailing as it should be — unhurried, beautiful, and free.

name: Marlborough Sounds Scuba Diving
short: Dive the sheltered Marlborough Sounds with diverse marine life and wrecks.
commentary: The Marlborough Sounds offer sheltered diving with visibility of 5-15 metres depending on conditions. Sites include rocky reef walls, kelp forests, and the Mikhail Lermontov wreck — the largest diveable shipwreck in the Southern Hemisphere. Marine life includes crayfish, octopus, nudibranchs, and occasional dolphins.

reviews.summary: The Marlborough Sounds Scuba Diving adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Dive the sheltered Marlborough Sounds with diverse marine life and wrecks. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at one of the South Island's most diverse diving destinations. The Marlborough Sounds offer a range of dive sites from sheltered rocky reefs teeming with crayfish and colourful nudibranchs to the Mikhail Lermontov — a 155-metre Russian cruise liner that sank in Port Gore in 1986, making it the largest diveable shipwreck in the Southern Hemisphere. The ship lies on her side in 36 metres of water, and penetration dives reveal eerily preserved staterooms and corridors. For less extreme diving, the reef walls of the Sounds are home to octopus, blue cod, and forests of kelp that sway in the current. The sheltered waters mean diving is possible year-round, though summer offers the best visibility at up to 15 metres. Whether you're exploring a wreck frozen in time or drifting along a reef, the Sounds deliver world-class diving in a uniquely beautiful setting.

name: Pelorus Mail Boat Cruise
short: Ride the working mail boat that delivers to remote homesteads in the Pelorus Sound.
commentary: The Pelorus Mail Boat has been delivering mail, supplies, and passengers to isolated Pelorus Sound homesteads since the early 1900s. The full-day cruise from Havelock covers over 100 km of waterways, stopping at farms accessible only by boat. It is one of the last working mail boat runs in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: The Pelorus Mail Boat Cruise route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Ride the working mail boat that delivers to remote homesteads in the Pelorus Sound. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are boarding the Pelorus Mail Boat at Havelock, and today you will deliver the mail. This is not a tourist cruise pretending to be something it is not — this is a working service that has run since the early 1900s, connecting remote Sound homesteads to the outside world. Over the next seven hours, the boat will cover more than 100 kilometres of sheltered waterways, pulling into tiny jetties where farmers collect their post, groceries, and sometimes livestock supplies. Some of these families are the only residents in their entire bay.
