audio_story: You are boarding a jet boat at Glenorchy, and minutes from now you will be deep in a roadless wilderness that most people only see in Lord of the Rings. The Dart River flows from a glacier in Mt Aspiring National Park, and its braided channels shift constantly across a wide gravel bed. The jet boat skims across water just inches deep, spinning through channels barely wider than the hull — the Hamilton jet was invented in New Zealand precisely for rivers like this. As you travel deeper into the valley, beech forest closes in on both sides, and the mountains grow taller and wilder. There are no roads here, no buildings, just the river, the forest, and the sound of the engine echoing off rock walls. At the turnaround point, the Dart Glacier hangs in the valley head, blue and ancient, the source of all this water.

name: Te Awa Whakatipu / Dart River
short: A glacial river flowing through ancestral Ngāi Tahu territory — part of the ancient pounamu trail network.
commentary: The Dart River valley was part of the network of trails connecting pounamu sources. The braided glacial river, flanked by towering mountains, was a highway for trade and travel. Ngāi Tahu maintain their cultural connections to the valley.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Awa Whakatipu / Dart River as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A glacial river flowing through ancestral Ngāi Tahu territory — part of the ancient pounamu trail network. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Doubtful Sound / Patea Overnight Cruise
short: Cruise the vast, remote Doubtful Sound — three times longer and ten times larger than Milford.
commentary: Doubtful Sound / Patea is three times longer and ten times the area of Milford Sound. Reaching it requires a boat across Lake Manapouri and a bus over Wilmot Pass. The fiord's remoteness means fewer visitors and a profound sense of wilderness. A permanent pod of about 60 bottlenose dolphins lives in the fiord, and the 'Sound of Silence' experience — where all boat engines are turned off — is iconic.

reviews.summary: The Doubtful Sound / Patea Overnight Cruise route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cruise the vast, remote Doubtful Sound — three times longer and ten times larger than Milford. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are entering Doubtful Sound, and the first thing you notice is the silence. Getting here takes effort — a boat across Lake Manapouri, a bus over Wilmot Pass — and that remoteness is exactly the point. Doubtful Sound is three times longer than Milford and ten times its area, but receives a fraction of the visitors. Captain Cook named it 'Doubtful Harbour' in 1770 because he was not sure he could sail out again if the wind changed. On the overnight cruise, there is a moment when the skipper cuts the engine and asks everyone to be completely quiet. In that silence, you hear the fiord breathe — waterfalls murmuring on distant walls, birdsong echoing across the water, and sometimes the soft exhalation of bottlenose dolphins surfacing nearby. About 60 dolphins live permanently in the fiord, one of the most stable and studied pods in New Zealand.

name: Lake Wakatipu Fishing Charter
short: Fish for trophy brown and rainbow trout in the crystal waters of Lake Wakatipu.
commentary: Lake Wakatipu and its tributaries are renowned for trophy-sized brown and rainbow trout. Guided fishing charters use a mix of fly fishing, trolling, and spin techniques. The lake's remote western arms are only accessible by boat and hold some of the region's largest fish.

reviews.summary: The Lake Wakatipu Fishing Charter adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Fish for trophy brown and rainbow trout in the crystal waters of Lake Wakatipu. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the shores of Lake Wakatipu, one of the South Island's premier trout fisheries. The lake and its feeder streams hold brown and rainbow trout that regularly exceed five pounds, with trophy fish pushing well beyond that. Your guide knows exactly where the big ones lie — in the cold, clear tributary mouths, along the drop-offs, and in the remote western bays that most anglers never reach. Whether you prefer fly fishing in the gin-clear rivers or trolling the deep lake waters, the combination of world-class fish and jaw-dropping mountain scenery makes this one of the finest fishing experiences in the Southern Hemisphere. The water clarity here has to be seen to be believed.

name: Glenorchy
short: 45 minutes up Lake Whakatipu from Queenstown — the road is the point. Gateway to the Dart Valley.
commentary: The Glenorchy-Queenstown road hugs the west side of Lake Whakatipu for 45 kilometres — consistently ranked one of the world's great scenic drives. Glenorchy itself is the last town; beyond it the road turns to gravel and runs into the Dart Valley, the filming location for Rivendell, Isengard and Lothlórien in Peter Jackson's Lord of the Rings. The Routeburn Track, one of New Zealand's Great Walks, starts 20 minutes past the town at the Routeburn Shelter.

reviews.summary: 45 minutes of spectacular Lake Wakatipu scenery culminates in Glenorchy — a tiny town at the head of a breathtaking valley used as Middle-earth by Peter Jackson. Visitors love the horseback rides, jet boating, and the jaw-dropping Paradise location for hiking. The drive itself is rated among NZ's best.

name: Queenstown to Glenorchy Road
short: A 45-minute lakeside drive considered one of the most beautiful short drives in the world.
commentary: The 46 km road from Queenstown to Glenorchy follows the western shore of Lake Wakatipu beneath the Richardson Mountains. The drive passes through beech forest, alongside crystal-clear lake waters, and past filming locations used in the Lord of the Rings trilogy. Glenorchy, at the head of the lake, is the gateway to the Routeburn, Rees-Dart, and Greenstone-Caples tracks.

reviews.summary: The Queenstown to Glenorchy Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A 45-minute lakeside drive considered one of the most beautiful short drives in the world. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving from Queenstown to Glenorchy, and this 46 kilometres of road is regularly called one of the most scenic short drives on Earth. Lake Wakatipu sits below you like a sheet of beaten silver, its waters reflecting the mountains on both sides. The lake is the third-largest in New Zealand and has a mysterious seiche — a rhythmic rise and fall of water level every 27 minutes that Maori attributed to the heartbeat of a sleeping giant. The road hugs the western shore, ducking through stands of beech forest and past rocky bluffs. You will recognise some of this landscape from the Lord of the Rings films — Isengard, Lothlorien, and several battle scenes were filmed in the flats around Glenorchy. The tiny town itself sits at the head of the lake, quietly magnificent, a gateway to some of New Zealand's finest backcountry.

name: Skyline Gondola / Bob's Peak
short: 450m straight up to the best Queenstown panorama — plus a luge, a café, and the Ledge bungy.
commentary: The Skyline Gondola has been running since 1967 — the steepest passenger lift cable car in the Southern Hemisphere, 730m long at a 37° average grade. At the top: the restaurant with the view that sells New Zealand internationally, the Skyline luge (two tracks, 800m combined), and the Ledge Bungy for a 47m jump into Queenstown Bay.

reviews.summary: The Skyline Gondola delivers some of New Zealand's most dramatic panoramas — Lake Wakatipu, The Remarkables, and the town below laid out in one sweep. The luge is called great fun by families and adults alike. Reviewers recommend combining the gondola with a sunset dinner at the restaurant.

name: Greenstone & Caples Track
short: Walk a 60 km loop through two glacier-carved valleys once used as a Maori pounamu trading route.
commentary: The Greenstone and Caples tracks form a 61 km loop from the head of Lake Wakatipu, typically walked in 4-5 days. The Greenstone Valley was a major Maori trade route for pounamu (greenstone), with the stone carried from the West Coast over the alpine passes to the eastern settlements. The two valleys run parallel, linked by McKellar Saddle at 945 m.

reviews.summary: The Greenstone & Caples Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk a 60 km loop through two glacier-carved valleys once used as a Maori pounamu trading route. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking the Greenstone-Caples loop, and your boots are following a path that Maori traders wore into the landscape centuries ago. The Greenstone Valley — its English name is no coincidence — was a major route for carrying pounamu from the West Coast to the settlements of the east. Maori would cross the mountain passes with loads of raw greenstone, one of the most valued materials in pre-European New Zealand, harder than steel and beautiful enough to shape into taonga that were treasured for generations. The two valleys run parallel, connected by McKellar Saddle, and the loop takes you through wide, tussock-floored valleys flanked by beech-covered mountains. This is gentler walking than the Routeburn next door, and the huts are quieter, but the sense of walking a route with deep history gives every step a weight that pure scenery alone cannot match.

name: Glacier Explorer Heli Tour
short: Helicopter over glaciers, fiords, and alpine peaks with a snow landing.
commentary: Multiple operators offer helicopter tours from Queenstown Airport covering Milford Sound, the glaciers of the Southern Alps, and the remote Fiordland wilderness. Many tours include a glacier or alpine snow landing. Flight times range from 30 minutes to 3+ hours.

reviews.summary: The Glacier Explorer Heli Tour adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Helicopter over glaciers, fiords, and alpine peaks with a snow landing. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Queenstown's heliport, your launch pad for one of the most breathtaking aerial experiences in the Southern Hemisphere. Your helicopter is about to lift you above the Remarkables and carry you deep into landscapes that are almost impossible to reach any other way. Depending on your chosen route, you could be flying over the sapphire waters of Milford Sound, landing on an ancient glacier high in the Southern Alps, or hovering above the remote fiords of Fiordland. The pilot will set down on a pristine snowfield so you can step out into absolute silence, surrounded by peaks and ice. From up here, you truly grasp the scale of New Zealand's South Island — vast, wild, and staggeringly beautiful.

name: Glenorchy Horse Trekking
short: Ride horses through Lord of the Rings landscapes in the Glenorchy backcountry.
commentary: Horse trekking from Glenorchy takes riders through the braided river valleys, native beech forest, and mountain scenery used as filming locations in the Lord of the Rings and Hobbit trilogies. Rides cater to all experience levels, from gentle riverside walks to backcountry adventures.

reviews.summary: The Glenorchy Horse Trekking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride horses through Lord of the Rings landscapes in the Glenorchy backcountry. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived in Glenorchy, the small settlement at the head of Lake Wakatipu that served as the gateway to Middle-earth during the Lord of the Rings filming. Now you're going to explore this landscape the old-fashioned way — on horseback. Your horse will carry you through braided river flats edged by towering mountains, into pockets of ancient beech forest draped in moss, and across open meadows where the scenery is so cinematic it feels like a film set — because it literally was. These are the lands of Isengard, Lothlórien, and Ithilien. No experience is necessary; the horses know these trails by heart. Just settle into the saddle, breathe in the mountain air, and let the landscape unfold around you.

name: Kawarau Bridge Bungy
short: The world's first commercial bungy jump — 43 metres off an 1880 suspension bridge, running since 1988.
commentary: A. J. Hackett and Henry van Asch opened Kawarau Bridge Bungy on 12 November 1988, turning an adrenaline stunt into an industry. The bridge itself is a Category 1 heritage structure from 1880, built for the gold coaches to Cromwell. The jump is 43 metres; you can touch the water on request. Around half a million people have jumped here.

reviews.summary: The Kawarau Bridge Bungy adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. The world's first commercial bungy jump — 43 metres off an 1880 suspension bridge, running since 1988. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Kepler Track Great Walk
short: A 60 km loop track with stunning alpine ridgeline walking above the Fiordland lakes.
commentary: The Kepler Track is a 60 km loop starting and finishing at the Lake Te Anau control gates. It climbs to 1,400 m on the Kepler Mountains, traversing an exposed alpine ridgeline with panoramic views of Lakes Te Anau and Manapouri. The track was purpose-built as a Great Walk in the late 1980s, making it one of the best-designed multi-day walks in the country.

reviews.summary: The Kepler Track Great Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A 60 km loop track with stunning alpine ridgeline walking above the Fiordland lakes. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking the Kepler Track, and today's ridgeline section is going to test your nerve and reward your eyes in equal measure. The 60-kilometre loop starts gently at the Lake Te Anau control gates and follows the lakeshore through forest before climbing steeply to Luxmore Hut. But the next day is the showpiece: you walk along an exposed alpine ridge at 1,400 metres with Lakes Te Anau and Manapouri spread out on either side like blue mirrors, and the peaks of Fiordland marching to the horizon. The track was purpose-built in the late 1980s to relieve pressure on the Milford and Routeburn, and the designers did a remarkable job — the route finds every possible viewpoint and dramatic moment. In bad weather, the ridgeline can be genuinely dangerous, with wind and visibility dropping fast, so check the forecast and be prepared to use the emergency shelters.

name: TSS Earnslaw Lake Wakatipu Cruise
short: Cruise Lake Wakatipu on the century-old TSS Earnslaw — the last coal-fired steamship in the Southern Hemisphere.
commentary: The TSS Earnslaw is a 1912 vintage twin-screw steamer that cruises Lake Wakatipu from Queenstown to Walter Peak High Country Farm. It is the last remaining coal-fired passenger steamship in the Southern Hemisphere. The ship has been in continuous operation for over 110 years and was originally built to service lakeside farms before road access existed.

reviews.summary: The TSS Earnslaw Lake Wakatipu Cruise route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cruise Lake Wakatipu on the century-old TSS Earnslaw — the last coal-fired steamship in the Southern Hemisphere. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are boarding the TSS Earnslaw, and the smell of coal smoke and hot oil hits you before you even step onto the deck. This twin-screw steamer has been cruising Lake Wakatipu since 1912 — over 110 years of continuous service — making her the last coal-fired passenger steamship operating in the Southern Hemisphere. Below deck, you can watch the stokers feeding coal into the twin furnaces, the fireboxes glowing orange, the pistons driving with a rhythm that has not changed in a century. The Earnslaw was built to service the sheep farms that lined Lake Wakatipu before roads existed — she carried wool, livestock, and people to and from the remote stations. Today she cruises to Walter Peak, where the old homestead gardens still bloom beneath the mountains. As you stand on deck watching the Remarkables slide past, the steam whistle blows, and for a moment the twenty-first century feels very far away.

name: The Ledge Bungy
short: Launch freestyle from Bob's Peak — the only bungy where you choose your own style.
commentary: The Ledge Bungy at Bob's Peak offers a 47-metre jump with a difference — there's no ankle harness. Instead, a chest and hip harness lets you run, flip, or twist off the platform however you choose. Night jumps are available, adding city lights and starlit skies to the experience.

reviews.summary: The The Ledge Bungy adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Launch freestyle from Bob's Peak — the only bungy where you choose your own style. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at The Ledge Bungy, perched on Bob's Peak 400 metres above Queenstown. Unlike traditional bungy jumps, The Ledge uses a body harness instead of ankle ties, meaning you can run, flip, spin, or swan-dive off the platform in any style you choose. At 47 metres, the drop is shorter than the Nevis, but the panoramic views of Lake Wakatipu and the Remarkables range are unmatched. If you're here after dark, you're in for something truly special — night jumps under the stars with Queenstown's lights glittering below. It's the most creative bungy in New Zealand, and the only limit is your imagination.

name: Skyline Luge Queenstown
short: Race gravity-powered luge carts down scenic tracks on Bob's Peak.
commentary: The Skyline Luge offers gravity-fuelled racing on purpose-built tracks on Bob's Peak. Two tracks of varying difficulty wind downhill with banked corners and tunnels. The scenic track is 800 metres and the advanced track is 1.6 km with sharper turns and steeper gradients.

reviews.summary: The Skyline Luge Queenstown adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Race gravity-powered luge carts down scenic tracks on Bob's Peak. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Skyline Luge, one of Queenstown's most fun family-friendly adventures. You'll hop into a gravity-powered luge cart and race down purpose-built tracks that wind through the mountainside on Bob's Peak. The scenic track eases you in at 800 metres, but the advanced track stretches to 1.6 kilometres with sharp banked corners, tunnels, and enough speed to get your heart pumping. The best part? A chairlift whisks you back to the top so you can do it all over again. With Lake Wakatipu and the mountains as your backdrop, even the chairlift ride is worth the trip. Kids and adults alike find this one impossibly addictive.

name: Milford Sound Kayaking
short: Paddle beneath towering Milford Sound cliffs and waterfalls by kayak.
commentary: Kayaking Milford Sound puts you at water level beneath 1,200-metre cliff faces and cascading waterfalls like Stirling Falls. Guided tours operate year-round and frequently encounter fur seals, dolphins, and penguins. The fiord's calm waters make it suitable for most fitness levels.

reviews.summary: The Milford Sound Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Paddle beneath towering Milford Sound cliffs and waterfalls by kayak. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Milford Sound, known to Maori as Piopiotahi, and you're about to experience this fiord the way it was meant to be seen — from the water, at your own pace. Your kayak will carry you beneath sheer granite walls that rise over 1,200 metres from the dark water, past the thundering curtain of Stirling Falls, and into hidden coves where fur seals laze on sun-warmed rocks. Rudyard Kipling called this the eighth wonder of the world, and from a kayak you understand why. The silence between paddle strokes is profound, broken only by birdsong and the distant rumble of waterfalls. Keep your eyes on the water — bottlenose dolphins and Fiordland crested penguins are regular visitors here.

name: Milford Road (SH94)
short: New Zealand's most spectacular highway — 120 km from Te Anau to Milford Sound through Fiordland.
commentary: State Highway 94, the Milford Road, runs 120 km from Te Anau to Milford Sound, crossing the Divide and passing through the 1.2 km Homer Tunnel. It is widely regarded as one of the world's most scenic drives, with highlights including Mirror Lakes, the Hollyford Valley, the Avenue of the Disappearing Mountain, and the dramatic Cleddau Valley descent to the fiord.

reviews.summary: The Milford Road (SH94) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. New Zealand's most spectacular highway — 120 km from Te Anau to Milford Sound through Fiordland. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the Milford Road, and this is not just a road — it is a pilgrimage. From Te Anau, SH94 heads northwest through beech forest and tussock flats before entering the mountains proper. The road stops at several unmissable spots: Mirror Lakes, where the Earls Mountains reflect perfectly on still days; the Avenue of the Disappearing Mountain, an optical illusion of perspective; and the vast Hollyford Valley opening to the east. Then comes the Homer Tunnel — 1.2 kilometres blasted through solid granite over 19 years, its mouth framed by cascading waterfalls after rain. The descent through the Cleddau Valley on the other side is almost vertical, the road switchbacking past ancient forest and thundering falls. When Milford Sound finally appears at the road's end, with Mitre Peak rising 1,692 metres straight from the water, you understand why this journey exists.

name: Milford Sound / Piopiotahi Cruise
short: Cruise beneath Mitre Peak and towering waterfalls in New Zealand's most famous fiord.
commentary: Milford Sound / Piopiotahi was carved by glaciers over 100,000 years ago and stretches 15 km from the head to the Tasman Sea. Mitre Peak rises 1,692 m directly from the water. The fiord receives an average of 6,813 mm of rain annually, feeding temporary waterfalls that cascade down sheer rock faces after storms. Bottlenose dolphins, fur seals, and Fiordland crested penguins are regularly seen.

reviews.summary: The Milford Sound / Piopiotahi Cruise route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cruise beneath Mitre Peak and towering waterfalls in New Zealand's most famous fiord. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are cruising Milford Sound, and Mitre Peak is doing exactly what it does in every photograph — rising 1,692 metres straight out of the water like a monument to vertical ambition. But photographs cannot prepare you for the scale. The fiord was carved by glaciers over 100,000 years ago, and its walls are sheer, dark, and glistening with waterfalls. Milford receives nearly seven metres of rain each year, making it one of the wettest inhabited places on Earth, and after heavy rain, hundreds of temporary waterfalls appear on every cliff face. The boat takes you past Stirling Falls, where the spray drenches the deck, and out toward the Tasman Sea where fur seals haul out on the rocks. Rudyard Kipling called Milford Sound the eighth wonder of the world, and standing on the deck with the mountains closing in overhead, you understand that he was not exaggerating.

name: Milford Track Great Walk
short: Walk the 'finest walk in the world' — 53 km through Fiordland's heart to Milford Sound.
commentary: The Milford Track covers 53.5 km from Glade Wharf on Lake Te Anau to Sandfly Point near Milford Sound, typically walked in 4 days. It crosses Mackinnon Pass at 1,154 m and passes Sutherland Falls, at 580 m one of New Zealand's tallest waterfalls. The track was dubbed 'the finest walk in the world' by a London newspaper in 1908 and has maintained that reputation.

reviews.summary: The Milford Track Great Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk the 'finest walk in the world' — 53 km through Fiordland's heart to Milford Sound. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are beginning the Milford Track, and since 1908, when a London newspaper christened it the finest walk in the world, no one has seriously argued otherwise. The 53 kilometres start with a boat ride across Lake Te Anau to Glade Wharf, then follow the Clinton River through towering beech forest to the base of Mackinnon Pass. The climb to the pass at 1,154 metres is the great challenge, but from the memorial cairn at the top, the view into the Arthur Valley is a reward beyond measure. On the descent, a side track leads to Sutherland Falls — 580 metres of water falling in three leaps, the spray creating its own weather system. The final day follows the Arthur River down to Sandfly Point, where a boat carries you out to Milford Sound. You arrive tired, damp, and completely certain you have just done something extraordinary.

name: Queenstown Bike Park & Trails
short: Gondola-accessed downhill mountain biking with world-class trails above Queenstown.
commentary: The Queenstown Bike Park on Bob's Peak offers gondola-accessed downhill mountain biking with trails for all abilities. The network includes flowing berms, jumps, and technical singletrack. The Queenstown Trail network also provides over 130 km of off-road cycling around the Wakatipu basin.

reviews.summary: The Queenstown Bike Park & Trails adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Gondola-accessed downhill mountain biking with world-class trails above Queenstown. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Queenstown's mountain biking hub, where a gondola ride delivers you to the start of some of the Southern Hemisphere's best downhill trails. The Queenstown Bike Park on Bob's Peak offers everything from gentle flowing trails for beginners to gnarly technical descents that have hosted international competitions. Load your bike onto the gondola, ride to the top, and let gravity do the work as you carve through native bush with Lake Wakatipu flashing between the trees below. If cross-country is more your style, the Queenstown Trail network stretches over 130 kilometres around the Wakatipu basin, connecting Queenstown, Arrowtown, and Gibbston Valley. Either way, this is mountain biking paradise.

name: Nevis Bungy Jump
short: Freefall 134 metres from New Zealand's highest bungy — the Nevis is pure adrenaline.
commentary: The Nevis Bungy is New Zealand's highest bungy jump at 134 metres, suspended above the Nevis River gorge. Operated by AJ Hackett, the jump pod is accessed via a cable car ride across the canyon. The 8.5-second freefall reaches speeds of around 130 km/h.

reviews.summary: The Nevis Bungy Jump adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Freefall 134 metres from New Zealand's highest bungy — the Nevis is pure adrenaline. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Nevis Bungy, New Zealand's tallest commercial bungy jump at a staggering 134 metres. You're about to step into a small glass-floored pod suspended by cables high above the Nevis River gorge. When you leap, you'll freefall for 8.5 seconds, hitting speeds of around 130 kilometres per hour before the cord catches you. The canyon walls will blur past as you plummet through 134 metres of pure vertical space. This is the jump that even seasoned thrill-seekers call the ultimate test of nerve. Take a deep breath — the Nevis doesn't do half measures.

name: NZONE Skydive Queenstown
short: Tandem skydive up to 15,000 feet over Queenstown's lakes and mountains.
commentary: NZONE Skydive operates tandem jumps from 9,000, 12,000, and 15,000 feet above Queenstown. From the highest altitude, you'll freefall for up to 60 seconds at 200 km/h with views stretching from Milford Sound to Mount Cook. The drop zone is at the Queenstown Airport end of the Frankton flats.

reviews.summary: The NZONE Skydive Queenstown adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Tandem skydive up to 15,000 feet over Queenstown's lakes and mountains. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at NZONE Skydive, one of the most scenic skydiving locations on the planet. In a few moments, you could be climbing to 15,000 feet in a plane, gazing down at Lake Wakatipu, the Remarkables, Cecil Peak, and Walter Peak laid out like a living map below. Then the door opens, and you freefall for up to 60 seconds at 200 kilometres per hour, the wind roaring past as Queenstown's mountains rush toward you. The parachute deploys, and suddenly everything is silent — just you, floating above one of the most dramatic alpine landscapes in the world. Over 30 years of operations and tens of thousands of jumps make this one of the most trusted skydiving outfits in New Zealand.

name: G Force Paragliding Queenstown
short: Tandem paraglide from Bob's Peak and soar above Lake Wakatipu.
commentary: G Force Paragliding offers tandem flights launching from Bob's Peak, with a flight time of 8 to 25 minutes depending on conditions. Pilots perform optional acrobatic manoeuvres on request. Landing is at the waterfront park in central Queenstown.

reviews.summary: The G Force Paragliding Queenstown adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Tandem paraglide from Bob's Peak and soar above Lake Wakatipu. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at one of the world's most spectacular paragliding launch sites. In moments, you and your tandem pilot will run a few steps off Bob's Peak and lift into the sky above Queenstown. Below you, Lake Wakatipu stretches in its distinctive lightning-bolt shape, flanked by the Remarkables and Cecil Peak. Your flight can last anywhere from 8 to 25 minutes depending on the thermals, and if you're feeling brave, ask your pilot for some acrobatic swoops and spirals. You'll eventually glide down to land gently in the park beside the lake, right in the heart of town. It's the most peaceful way to experience Queenstown's mountain scenery — until you ask for the tricks.

name: Pounamu Trail — Greenstone/Caples
short: The ancestral pounamu trail — Māori carried greenstone from the West Coast for centuries along this route.
commentary: The modern tramping track follows the same route as ancient pounamu carriers. Ngāi Tahu controlled the resource and the trails.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pounamu Trail — Greenstone/Caples as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The ancestral pounamu trail — Māori carried greenstone from the West Coast for centuries along this route. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Rees-Dart Track
short: A challenging 63 km loop through the Mt Aspiring wilderness, with a glacier valley and alpine pass crossing.
commentary: The Rees-Dart Track is a 63 km loop from Muddy Creek near Glenorchy, crossing Rees Saddle at 1,471 m between the Rees and Dart valleys. The track passes through remote alpine scenery, beech forest, and past the Dart Glacier. A side trip to Cascade Saddle offers one of the most spectacular views in New Zealand. It is considered a more challenging alternative to the Routeburn.

reviews.summary: The Rees-Dart Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A challenging 63 km loop through the Mt Aspiring wilderness, with a glacier valley and alpine pass crossing. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking the Rees-Dart Track, and this is where serious tramping begins. The 63-kilometre loop starts from near Glenorchy and takes you into country that feels truly remote — the Rees Valley rising to a 1,471-metre saddle crossing, then descending into the Dart Valley with its glacier and braided river. If you have the fitness and the weather, the side trip to Cascade Saddle is not optional — it is arguably the single most spectacular viewpoint accessible to trampers in New Zealand, looking down over the Dart Glacier and across to Mt Aspiring itself. The Rees-Dart does not have the manicured feel of the Great Walks; river crossings can be challenging after rain, the saddle is exposed to weather, and navigation requires experience. But that is exactly why the people who walk it remember it for the rest of their lives.

name: The Remarkables
short: A 22km ridgeline rising 2,300m above Lake Whakatipu — one of only two mountain ranges in the world that runs true north-south.
commentary: The Remarkables were uplifted along an active fault line — Double Cone, the highest peak, is 2,319 metres. In winter the ski field runs on 35% of the range; in summer the Sugar Loaf track takes experienced hikers to alpine tarns most people never see. The peak behind Frankton, locally known as The Chinamans Bluff, is sacred to Kāi Tahu.

reviews.summary: The Remarkables ski field delivers world-class terrain with the dramatic backdrop of Lake Wakatipu. Visitors praise the reliable snow, the variety of runs from beginner to expert, and the spectacular views. Summer visitors enjoy the alpine walks accessed from the ski field access road.

name: The Remarkables Ski Area
short: Ski or board the iconic Remarkables range with stunning Lake Wakatipu views.
commentary: The Remarkables ski area sits at 1,943 metres in the shadow-catching mountain range visible from Queenstown. It offers terrain for all levels including learner slopes, groomed runs, and expert chutes. The season typically runs June to October with reliable snow thanks to the south-facing aspect.

reviews.summary: The The Remarkables Ski Area adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ski or board the iconic Remarkables range with stunning Lake Wakatipu views. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at The Remarkables, the dramatic mountain range that forms Queenstown's iconic skyline. Named by early surveyors who were struck by how the peaks align almost perfectly north-south, The Remarkables offers skiing and snowboarding from 1,586 to 1,943 metres elevation. The south-facing slopes hold snow brilliantly, and the views across to Lake Wakatipu and Coronet Peak are extraordinary. Whether you're a beginner on the wide Shadow Basin runs or an expert dropping into the steep chutes of Homeward Bound, there's terrain here to match your ambition. The ski area has been a Queenstown institution since 1985, and the winding access road is an adventure in itself.

name: The Remarkables Access Road
short: Drive the dramatic switchback road climbing the Remarkables range above Queenstown.
commentary: The Remarkables ski field access road climbs from the Kawarau River bridge to 1,600 m in just 13 km via a series of tight switchbacks. The road offers increasingly dramatic views of the Wakatipu Basin, Queenstown, and surrounding mountains. The Remarkables range gets its name from being one of only two mountain ranges in the world that run directly north-south.

reviews.summary: The The Remarkables Access Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the dramatic switchback road climbing the Remarkables range above Queenstown. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving up the Remarkables, and each switchback lifts you further above the Wakatipu Basin until Queenstown looks like a toy town on the lakeshore far below. This road climbs 1,100 metres in just 13 kilometres, making it one of the steepest public roads in New Zealand. The Remarkables got their name from an early surveyor who noted that the range runs almost perfectly north-south — a rare alignment in mountain geography. At the ski field car park, even if there is no snow, the views are astounding: Lake Wakatipu curving through the basin, the Crown Range to the north, Cecil Peak to the west, and the vastness of Central Otago stretching east. In winter, this road requires chains and a strong stomach for exposed mountain driving.

name: Routeburn Track
short: A 33km Great Walk over the Harris Saddle — alpine tarns, red tussock basins, and the border between two national parks.
commentary: The Routeburn Track connects Glenorchy (near Queenstown) with the Milford Road (Fiordland National Park). 32.5km, three days, high alpine.

reviews.summary: Routeburn Track draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 33km Great Walk over the Harris Saddle — alpine tarns, red tussock basins, and the border between two national parks. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are about to walk the Routeburn Track, one of New Zealand's Great Walks and one of the finest short alpine tramps anywhere in the world. The track runs thirty two and a half kilometres between the Routeburn Shelter at the head of Lake Wakatipu near Glenorchy and the Divide on the Milford Road in Fiordland. Most walkers take three days. The route climbs from beech forest up through the Routeburn Flats, past Routeburn Falls, and over the Harris Saddle at around thirteen hundred metres, where on a clear day the view north into the Darran Mountains is almost beyond description. From the saddle the track drops slowly through tussock basins past Lake Mackenzie and finally down through silver beech forest to the Divide. Because the two ends of the track are four hours by road apart, most walkers book a shuttle or take one of the guided options that arrange transport. Huts must be booked well in advance, often months ahead, through Department of Conservation. Weather on the saddle can be extreme at any time of year. Do not attempt this track without proper equipment and a cautious plan. The reward is one of the iconic experiences of New Zealand tramping.

name: Routeburn Track Great Walk
short: A 32 km alpine crossing between Mt Aspiring and Fiordland through some of NZ's finest mountain scenery.
commentary: The Routeburn Track covers 32 km between the Routeburn Shelter (near Glenorchy) and The Divide on the Milford Road. The track crosses Harris Saddle at 1,255 m and passes Lake Harris, the Hollyford Face, and Routeburn Falls. It is often rated as one of the best multi-day hikes in the world for its combination of alpine scenery, forest, and mountain vistas.

reviews.summary: The Routeburn Track Great Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A 32 km alpine crossing between Mt Aspiring and Fiordland through some of NZ's finest mountain scenery. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are setting out on the Routeburn Track, and for the next 32 kilometres this trail will show you why people fly across the world to walk in New Zealand. The track connects the eastern side of the mountains near Glenorchy to the Milford Road at The Divide, crossing between Mt Aspiring and Fiordland National Parks. The first day takes you through beech forest along the Route Burn river to the spectacular Routeburn Falls. Day two is the revelation: you climb above the bushline to Harris Saddle at 1,255 metres and traverse the Hollyford Face, a narrow trail carved across a mountainside with the entire Hollyford Valley laid out below and the peaks of Darran Mountains beyond. On a clear day at the saddle, you can see all the way to Martins Bay and the Tasman Sea. This is walking at its absolute finest.

name: Shotover Jet & Canyon
short: The original jet boat ride — running the Shotover Canyons since 1970, closer to rock walls than feels legal.
commentary: Bill Hamilton invented the modern jet boat in Canterbury in the 1950s, specifically so South Island farmers could navigate the shallow braided rivers on their land. Shotover Jet opened in 1970 as the first commercial operator and still holds the exclusive concession for the Shotover Canyons. The drivers train for 400+ hours before they can carry passengers; the 360° spins in one metre of water are the headline.

reviews.summary: The Shotover Jet & Canyon adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. The original jet boat ride — running the Shotover Canyons since 1970, closer to rock walls than feels legal. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Shotover River White Water Rafting
short: Tackle grade 3-5 rapids through the dramatic Shotover River canyon.
commentary: White water rafting on the Shotover River features grade 3 to 5 rapids through a narrow canyon carved from schist rock. The 14-kilometre run passes through a 170-metre tunnel blasted during the gold rush era. Multiple operators run trips from September to May.

reviews.summary: The Shotover River White Water Rafting adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Tackle grade 3-5 rapids through the dramatic Shotover River canyon. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the put-in point for the Shotover River, one of New Zealand's most famous white water runs. Over the next 14 kilometres, your raft crew will battle through grade 3 to 5 rapids with names like Cascade, Squeeze, and Toilet — and yes, they earned those names. The canyon walls close in tight, carved from ancient schist rock, and at one point you'll shoot through a 170-metre tunnel blasted by gold miners in the 1860s. It's dark, it's loud, and the water boils around you. The Shotover was once called the richest river in the world for its gold deposits. Today its riches are pure adrenaline. Brace yourself, paddle hard, and whatever you do, don't let go.

name: Skippers Canyon Heritage Road
short: New Zealand's most dangerous road winds through a gold rush gorge so remote most rental cars are banned.
commentary: Skippers Canyon Road is a narrow, unsealed road carved into the cliff face above the Shotover River gorge, originally built by gold miners in the 1860s. It is so narrow and exposed that most rental car companies prohibit its use, and insurance is typically void. The canyon was one of the richest gold-producing areas in New Zealand, yielding an estimated 100+ tonnes of gold.

reviews.summary: The Skippers Canyon Heritage Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. New Zealand's most dangerous road winds through a gold rush gorge so remote most rental cars are banned. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving into Skippers Canyon, and your rental car company almost certainly does not want you here. This narrow, unsealed road was carved into the cliff face above the Shotover River in the 1860s, and in places it is barely wide enough for one vehicle, with a sheer drop to the river hundreds of metres below. There are no guardrails. In the gold rush, this canyon produced over 100 tonnes of gold, and thousands of miners — many of them Chinese — lived in the gorge in conditions of extraordinary hardship. The stone remains of their settlements are still visible: retaining walls, sluice channels, and the ruins of a township that once had its own school and post office. At the road's end, the Shotover River runs through a gorge so narrow you could almost throw a stone across it. This is New Zealand's wild past, barely tamed.

name: TSS Earnslaw
short: The coal-fired 1912 steamship that still crosses Lake Wakatipu daily — the Lady of the Lake.
commentary: Built 1912 in Dunedin. One of the few working coal-fired passenger steamships still in service. Operates daily cruises to Walter Peak Station.

reviews.summary: TSS Earnslaw draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The coal-fired 1912 steamship that still crosses Lake Wakatipu daily — the Lady of the Lake. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Steamer Wharf in Queenstown, and you are about to board the TSS Earnslaw, a twin screw coal-fired passenger steamship that has been crossing Lake Wakatipu since nineteen twelve. She was built in Dunedin, brought to Queenstown in pieces by rail, and reassembled on the shore. When she was launched, she was the pride of the Otago Central Railways fleet, and for decades she worked these waters carrying passengers, livestock, mail, and the wool clip from the high country stations around the lake. She has never stopped working. Today she still fires on coal, she is still stoked by hand by engineers who trained for the job, and you can walk down the companionways and stand a few feet from the great black boiler that drives her. Most passengers take the lunchtime cruise across the lake to Walter Peak High Country Farm, where a sheep dog demonstration and a farmhouse lunch round out the experience. In the evening the Earnslaw runs a shorter harbour cruise. However you sail her, stand on the open bow as she leaves the wharf, watch the black smoke curl from her funnel, and know that in a world of diesel engines and lithium batteries, this ship remains defiantly, beautifully, a Victorian machine.

name: Remarkables Via Ferrata
short: Climb iron rungs up the Remarkables range on New Zealand's highest via ferrata.
commentary: The Remarkables via ferrata is a guided climbing experience using iron rungs, ladders, and wire bridges bolted into the cliff face of the Remarkables mountain range. The route reaches over 1,800 metres elevation with views across the Wakatipu basin. No climbing experience is required.

reviews.summary: The Remarkables Via Ferrata adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Climb iron rungs up the Remarkables range on New Zealand's highest via ferrata. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the base of the Remarkables via ferrata, a climbing route bolted directly into the dramatic cliff faces of the mountain range that defines Queenstown's skyline. Via ferrata means 'iron road' in Italian, and the system of metal rungs, ladders, and wire bridges allows you to climb terrain that would otherwise require serious mountaineering skills. You'll ascend the rock face using your hands and feet on the iron holds, always clipped to a safety cable, climbing higher and higher until the Wakatipu basin spreads out below you like an aerial photograph. At over 1,800 metres, this is one of the highest via ferrata routes in New Zealand, and the exposure is exhilarating — sheer drops beneath your feet, rock walls on either side, and the vast Otago landscape unfolding in every direction. No climbing experience is needed, just a reasonable level of fitness and a willingness to trust the system.

name: Lake Wakatipu — The Sleeping Giant
short: The lake shaped like a sleeping giant — the burnt outline of the taniwha Matau.
commentary: The lake's curious seiche — a regular rise and fall of 12 centimetres — is said to be Matau's heart still beating.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Lake Wakatipu — The Sleeping Giant as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The lake shaped like a sleeping giant — the burnt outline of the taniwha Matau. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ziptrek Ecotours
short: Fly through native forest canopy on ziplines high above Queenstown.
commentary: Ziptrek Ecotours operates a series of ziplines through ancient native forest on Bob's Peak. The flagship Kereru tour features six ziplines including a 21-storey-high line. An eco-tourism pioneer, Ziptrek combines adrenaline with education about native bush ecology and conservation.

reviews.summary: The Ziptrek Ecotours adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Fly through native forest canopy on ziplines high above Queenstown. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Ziptrek Ecotours on Bob's Peak, where ancient beech and pine forest becomes your personal aerial playground. You'll clip into a harness and launch yourself along a series of ziplines that thread through the canopy high above Queenstown. The longest line stretches over 300 metres, and at its highest point you'll be soaring 21 storeys above the forest floor with Lake Wakatipu sparkling below. Between flights, your guides share fascinating insights about the native forest ecosystem, the bird life, and conservation efforts in the region. It's one of those rare adventures that feeds both your adrenaline addiction and your curiosity about the natural world.

name: Blue & Green Lakes / Tikitapu & Rotokākahi
short: Two small lakes separated by a narrow ridge — one turquoise, one green, side by side.
commentary: Tikitapu (the Blue Lake) and Rotokākahi (the Green Lake) lie side by side, separated by a narrow 100-metre ridge, on the scenic road to Lake Tarawera. The colour difference is from their different underlying sediments: Tikitapu has a rhyolite and pumice bed that scatters blue light; Rotokākahi has a shallower bottom with silt from a small catchment. Rotokākahi is privately owned by Tūhourangi-Ngāti Wāhiao and no swimming, boating or fishing is permitted — it is a wāhi tapu (sacred place). Tikitapu is the public swimming lake. A scenic ridge-walk lookout is signposted.

reviews.summary: Blue & Green Lakes / Tikitapu & Rotokākahi draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Two small lakes separated by a narrow ridge — one turquoise, one green, side by side. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Buried Village of Te Wairoa
short: The Te Arawa village of Te Wairoa — buried by 1886 Tarawera eruption ash, now excavated.
commentary: Before 10 June 1886, Te Wairoa was the tourist hub for European visitors to the Pink and White Terraces — Māori guides led canoe tours of Lake Rotomahana, and mud-brick hotels served silver-service dinners. The eruption buried the village under 1.5 metres of mud and ash within hours. 150 of the 153 villagers died or were unaccounted for. Excavations began in 1931 and are still ongoing. The site today includes the excavated whare and hotel foundations, a small museum with survivor accounts, and a walk through bush to Wairere Falls.

reviews.summary: Buried Village of Te Wairoa draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The Te Arawa village of Te Wairoa — buried by 1886 Tarawera eruption ash, now excavated. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Mount Tarawera
short: The 1,111 m volcano that split open in 1886 — viewed from the lakeshore, or (with iwi permission) walked along the 17 km rift.
commentary: Mount Tarawera is sacred to Tūhourangi-Ngāti Wāhiao. At 2:30 am on 10 June 1886 the whole mountain split along a 17 km rift, erupting for six hours — the most violent eruption in New Zealand since European contact. What you see from the lakeshore is the scarred crater rim; the rift is a deep, 17 km gash across the summit. Guided 4WD tours (Mt Tarawera NZ, Kaitiaki Adventures) take visitors to the summit and along the rim by arrangement with the iwi owners. Helicopter flights also land on the summit. No independent access — the mountain is private and sacred.

reviews.summary: Mount Tarawera draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The 1,111 m volcano that split open in 1886 — viewed from the lakeshore, or (with iwi permission) walked along the 17 km rift. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Ōkere Falls / Kaituna River
short: Home to Tutea's Falls — at 7 metres, the highest commercially-rafted waterfall in the world.
commentary: The Kaituna River drains Lake Rotoiti out to the Bay of Plenty, falling through a short steep gorge of three named waterfalls. Tutea's Falls, the tallest at 7 metres, is the highest commercially-rafted waterfall in the world — and the whole of Kaituna is Grade 5 white water. Rafting operators run year-round. Non-rafters can walk the short track to viewing platforms overlooking all three falls and Tutea's Cave, a refuge for local hapū during Musket War–era attacks in the 1820s.

reviews.summary: The Ōkere Falls / Kaituna River adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Home to Tutea's Falls — at 7 metres, the highest commercially-rafted waterfall in the world. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

name: Rainbow Mountain / Maungakākaramea
short: An eroded rhyolite dome streaked red, orange, white and black — a short walk past bright crater lakes to a 360° summit.
commentary: Maungakākaramea — 'the mountain of coloured earth' — is a partially eroded rhyolite dome whose exposed hydrothermally-altered rock shows in bands of red, orange, yellow, white and black. A track leads past two small crater lakes (one bright green, one turquoise) to the 743 m summit in about 2 hours return. On a clear day the view includes Lake Taupō, Ruapehu, Ngāuruhoe and Tongariro. Access is from a signposted carpark on SH5 between Waiotapu and Taupō.

reviews.summary: Rainbow Mountain / Maungakākaramea draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. An eroded rhyolite dome streaked red, orange, white and black — a short walk past bright crater lakes to a 360° summit. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Redwoods / Whakarewarewa Forest
short: A 5,600-hectare Californian redwood forest right next to the city — the premier mountain-bike destination in the North Island.
commentary: The Whakarewarewa Forest was planted from 1899 onwards with dozens of exotic tree species to test which would grow best in New Zealand. The Californian coastal redwoods in the small 'Redwood Grove' near the entrance won: they are now over 70 metres tall. The wider forest has 180+ km of mountain-biking trails (genuine world-class), a family-friendly Treewalk through the canopy (open day and evening with Hundertwasser-style night lighting), and free walking tracks. Entry to the grove and walks is free; Treewalk is ticketed.

reviews.summary: Redwoods / Whakarewarewa Forest draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 5,600-hectare Californian redwood forest right next to the city — the premier mountain-bike destination in the North Island. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Te Wairoa Buried Village

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Te Wairoa Buried Village is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You are at Te Wairoa, the village buried by the Tarawera eruption of eighteen eighty six. Before that night this was a thriving settlement of Tūhourangi people and a small number of European hoteliers and guides who served visitors travelling to see the famed Pink and White Terraces. When the mountain erupted, hot mud and ash up to two metres deep crushed and entombed the village. Some sheltered in a sturdy whare and survived, including the elderly tohunga Tūhoto Ariki, who was dug out alive after four days. Many others did not survive. Decades later, archaeologists carefully excavated parts of the site, revealing the flour mill, the schoolhouse, the blacksmith's, and the homes of villagers. Walking the path here is a sombre experience. You see ovens still warm-looking with ash, doorways framed in earth, and a small museum holding photographs taken before that night. The waterfall at the end of the trail offers a moment of quiet. Te Wairoa is a memorial as much as a village, a place where you walk softly, listen carefully, and remember.

name: Government Gardens and Bath House

reviews.summary: Government Gardens and Bath House draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are walking through the Government Gardens, a large lakeside park gifted by Ngāti Whakaue to the Crown in eighteen eighty as a place of healing for all people. Within them stands the elaborate Bath House, an Edwardian half-timbered building that opened in nineteen oh eight and was once the grandest spa complex in the southern hemisphere. The government promoted Rotorua as a health resort, and visitors travelled from across the British Empire to soak in the mineral pools, hoping to cure rheumatism, gout, and a long list of other complaints. The grounds were laid out with formal lawns, rose gardens, and croquet greens, deliberately European in feeling. The Bath House now houses the Rotorua Museum, currently closed for earthquake strengthening, but its silhouette remains the most photographed building in the city. Behind the genteel surface lies the gift of the people of Te Arawa, whose hospitality and willingness to share their land made the spa town possible. Pause and notice both stories, the Edwardian fantasy and the much older generosity that underpinned it.

name: Mokoia Island and Hinemoa

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Mokoia Island and Hinemoa as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are looking out at Mokoia Island, the green dome rising from the centre of Lake Rotorua. This island is home to one of Aotearoa's most beloved love stories. The chief's daughter Hinemoa lived on the lake's edge, and her people opposed her love for Tūtānekai, who lived on Mokoia. Her family beached all the canoes so she could not cross. But each evening Tūtānekai played his flute on the island, and the music carried across the water. One night Hinemoa tied empty calabashes to her body for floats and swam through cold dark water guided by his music. She arrived chilled and exhausted, and warmed herself in a hot spring on the island that still bears her name, Hinemoa's Pool. Tūtānekai's servant came to draw water, and Hinemoa, hidden in the steam, broke his calabashes one by one until he ran to fetch his master. Their love story was finally accepted by both families. Mokoia today is jointly managed for conservation. From the lakeshore you can still see the island where bravery met music.

name: Ohinemutu Village and St Faith's

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ohinemutu Village and St Faith's as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are walking into Ohinemutu, the lakeside Te Arawa village that pre-dates Rotorua town by centuries. Steam rises through the cracks in the pavement and from the warm pools where families have cooked, washed, and bathed for generations. At the heart of the village stands Tama-te-Kapua, a magnificently carved meeting house named for the captain of the Te Arawa waka. Beside it is St Faith's Anglican Church, an extraordinary fusion of Māori and English forms. Inside, an etched window shows Christ in a feathered cloak appearing to walk on the waters of Lake Rotorua, and tukutuku panels and carvings line the walls. This is a living village. The marae is in daily use and the cemetery beside the church holds many of the rangatira who shaped Te Arawa's modern history. Walk gently, do not enter the wharenui without invitation, and respect the small signs asking for quiet. Ohinemutu shows what a Māori village looked like, and looks like, in a country where so much has been lost or moved aside.

name: Pukeroa Pā Site

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pukeroa Pā Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are on Pukeroa, the low hill that rises just behind Ohinemutu and looks out across Lake Rotorua. This was once the principal pā of Ngāti Whakaue, a defended settlement with terraces, palisades, and storage pits laid out across its slopes. From here the people watched canoes coming across the lake and could see far enough to spot any threat from rival hapū. In the nineteenth century the hospital was built on top of the hill, and today modern medical buildings cover much of the old fortification, but pou and signs mark its significance. Pukeroa is more than a hill, it is a living title held by the descendants of the chiefs who once stood at its summit. The land was leased to the Crown in the nineteenth century, and its return to Ngāti Whakaue ownership has been a long and ongoing story. Stand at the lookout, picture the wooden palisades and the smoke of cooking fires, and remember that beneath the hospital's foundations lies the heart of an iwi.

name: Mount Tarawera Eruption Site

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Mount Tarawera Eruption Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are looking at Mount Tarawera, a dark broken-backed mountain split by a long jagged rift. On the night of the tenth of June, eighteen eighty six, this peaceful peak tore itself apart in one of New Zealand's deadliest volcanic eruptions. A line of vents ripped open the summit, hurling ash and scoria for kilometres and burying the villages of Te Wairoa, Te Ariki, and Moura. Around one hundred and twenty people died, most of them Tūhourangi and Ngāti Rangitihi. The world-famous Pink and White Terraces, vast natural staircases of silica that drew European tourists by paddle steamer, were destroyed in minutes. Days before the eruption, witnesses on Lake Tarawera reported seeing a phantom war canoe gliding through the mist, an omen later understood as a warning from the ancestors. The eruption reshaped the entire landscape and changed Te Arawa's relationship with this place forever. Today the mountain is sacred wāhi tapu. From the lookout you can trace the rift and the dark scoria flows, and feel how recent and how violent this story still is.

name: Te Arawa Waka Arrival (Maketū to Rotorua)

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Arawa Waka Arrival (Maketū to Rotorua) as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are in the rohe of Te Arawa, descended from the great waka that made landfall at Maketū around six hundred years ago, captained by Tamatekapua and navigated by the tohunga Ngātoroirangi. From the coast, the people moved inland, claiming the lakes and forests of the central plateau. Ngātoroirangi, exploring the high country, found himself caught in a snowstorm on the slopes of Mount Tongariro and called to his sisters in distant Hawaiki for fire. They sent it through underground channels, and where the fire surfaced, the geothermal wonders of the central North Island appeared, the boiling pools of Rotorua, the steam vents of Tokaanu, and the volcanoes themselves. This is why the lands of Te Arawa, from Maketū to Tongariro, share a single fiery thread. Every geyser, every silica terrace, every warm spring is part of one story of a navigator who could not be allowed to freeze. As you walk this region, remember that the heat under your feet has a name, a family, and a journey behind it.

name: Te Pohue Pā and Te Arawa Confederation

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Pohue Pā and Te Arawa Confederation as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are in the heart of the Te Arawa rohe, the homelands of a great confederation of iwi descended from the Te Arawa waka. The confederation includes Ngāti Whakaue, Tūhourangi, Ngāti Pikiao, Ngāti Rangiwewehi, Ngāti Rangitihi, Ngāti Tarawhai, Ngāti Tūwharetoa ki Kawerau, and Tūhoe relatives, among others, each with their own takiwā around the central lakes. Their unity was tested in the New Zealand Wars when Te Arawa chose to support the Crown rather than join the Kīngitanga, fighting at Maketū and along the eastern lakes against forces moving south through the Bay of Plenty. That decision shaped land outcomes for generations. Today Te Arawa Lakes Trust holds the lake beds returned by Treaty settlement, and Te Arawa identity is strong in language, kapa haka, and tribal radio. As you travel this region from the geothermal Whakarewarewa to the forests of Te Urewera, remember that you are crossing the territories of many hapū, each with its own marae and its own stories, all linked by one ancestral canoe.

name: Lake Rotomahana and the Pink and White Terraces

reviews.summary: Lake Rotomahana and the Pink and White Terraces draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are at Lake Rotomahana, a large warm lake that did not exist in its present form before eighteen eighty six. Before the Tarawera eruption, two small lakes lay here, with the famous Pink and White Terraces cascading down their shores. The terraces were vast staircases of silica laid down by hot springs over centuries, the white terrace at one end and the smaller pink terrace at the other. They were considered the eighth wonder of the world, and travellers from London and New York paid handsomely to be guided here by Tūhourangi from Te Wairoa. On the night the mountain erupted, the terraces vanished, blasted to pieces or buried beneath ash and a vastly enlarged Rotomahana. For more than a century researchers debated whether any fragments remained. Recent expeditions have searched the lake bed with sonar and found tantalising clues. Today you can take a boat across the new Rotomahana, see steaming cliffs and bird life, and reflect on a wonder lost. The terraces are gone but they live on in paintings, photographs, and the grief of the people who guided visitors to them.

name: Whakarewarewa Living Māori Village

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Whakarewarewa Living Māori Village as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are at Whakarewarewa, the living thermal village where Tūhourangi and Ngāti Wāhiao have lived for generations. After the Tarawera eruption of eighteen eighty six destroyed Te Wairoa, displaced Tūhourangi families were welcomed here by Ngāti Wāhiao, and together they built a community around the geysers and boiling pools. Children of the village still dive for coins in the warm streams, and food is cooked in steam boxes set into the ground. The village's full name is Te Whakarewarewatanga o te Ope Taua a Wahiao, the gathering place of the war party of Wahiao. Pohutu Geyser, one of the most reliable in the country, erupts a column of hot water many times a day from the village's grounds. Generations of guides, including the legendary guides Maggie Papakura and Rangitiaria Dennan, led visitors through these landscapes and shaped the way the world saw Māori hospitality. As you walk, remember that this is home, not a stage. The kūmara cooked in the steam, the carved gateway, and the guides who tell their own stories all carry centuries of resilience.

name: Rotorua Canopy Tour Zipline
short: Zipline through 500-year-old native forest on New Zealand's original canopy tour.
commentary: Rotorua Canopy Tours is New Zealand's original zipline experience, set in a 500-year-old native forest. The course includes six ziplines, two swing bridges, and 22 platforms among ancient rimu, tawa, and kamahi trees. A portion of proceeds funds pest trapping in the forest.

reviews.summary: The Rotorua Canopy Tour Zipline adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Zipline through 500-year-old native forest on New Zealand's original canopy tour. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Rotorua Canopy Tours, where you are about to fly through the canopy of a 500-year-old native forest on New Zealand's original zipline experience. Your guides will clip you into the safety system and lead you across swing bridges to platforms built high in ancient trees, some of which were seedlings when European explorers had not yet left their shores. Six ziplines carry you between the treetops, the longest spanning over 220 metres, with the forest floor 22 metres below. Between zips, your guides share their deep knowledge of the ecology around you, pointing out birds like tui, kereru, and if you are very lucky, a North Island robin. This is conservation tourism at its best, a portion of every ticket funds a predator trapping network that protects the forest and its birdlife. You will hear the difference, this bush is alive with birdsong.

name: Blue and Green Lakes Cycle Loop
short: Cycle between Rotorua's famous Blue and Green Lakes through native bush on easy lakeside trails.
commentary: The Blue Lake (Tikitapu) and Green Lake (Rotokakahi) sit side by side separated by a narrow bush-covered isthmus. The colour difference is striking — the blue lake's clarity contrasts with the green lake's algae-tinted waters. The 12 km loop trail is part of Rotorua's extensive cycling network.

reviews.summary: The Blue and Green Lakes Cycle Loop route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cycle between Rotorua's famous Blue and Green Lakes through native bush on easy lakeside trails. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are cycling the loop between Rotorua's Blue and Green Lakes, two bodies of water that sit side by side yet look remarkably different. Tikitapu, the Blue Lake, is a deep, clear blue thanks to the purity of its water — it has no significant inflows. Rotokakahi, the Green Lake, is tinted by algae and appears vivid emerald green. Where the two lakes nearly touch, a narrow bush-clad isthmus separates them, and the colour contrast when you look from one to the other is startling. The 12-kilometre trail is flat and well-formed, winding through native bush and past picnic spots. The Green Lake is tapu — sacred — to the local iwi and swimming is not permitted, but the Blue Lake has a lovely swimming beach. This ride is perfect for families or anyone wanting an easy, scenic outing. On a calm day, the reflections of the surrounding bush in both lakes are mirror-perfect.

name: Te Wairoa — The Buried Village
short: A Māori village buried by the 1886 Tarawera eruption — NZ's Pompeii, now partly excavated.
commentary: On 10 June 1886, Mount Tarawera erupted, burying Te Wairoa under metres of mud and ash. Over 120 people were killed. The tohunga Tūhoto Ariki, who warned of the eruption, was found alive four days later.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Wairoa — The Buried Village as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A Māori village buried by the 1886 Tarawera eruption — NZ's Pompeii, now partly excavated. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Government Gardens & Bath House
short: Rotorua's Edwardian heart — manicured gardens, the Tudor Tower bath house, and a steaming lake at your back.
commentary: Government Gardens were laid out in 1903 around the Bath House (now the Rotorua Museum), built as a great curative spa to make Rotorua the South Pacific Brighton.

reviews.summary: Government Gardens & Bath House draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Rotorua's Edwardian heart — manicured gardens, the Tudor Tower bath house, and a steaming lake at your back. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are walking through the Government Gardens, the formal Edwardian heart of Rotorua. The land you are walking on was gifted to the Crown by the Ngāti Whakaue chief Te Kiharoa in eighteen eighty, on the condition that it be used for the people. In nineteen oh three the gardens were laid out in their current form around the great Tudor revival bath house at their centre, a building so unlike anything else in New Zealand that you will probably stop to take a photograph before you understand what you are looking at. The bath house was the government's bid to turn Rotorua into the great spa town of the South Pacific. Wealthy invalids came from around the world to bathe in mineral water that, depending on the pool, could be hot, cold, alkaline, or pleasantly fizzy with carbon dioxide. The building has since been a museum and is currently undergoing earthquake strengthening, but the gardens around it are still beautifully kept, complete with manicured lawns, croquet greens, lake views, and the unmistakable, faintly eggy smell of geothermal Rotorua on a still day. Sit on a bench. Watch the locals lawn bowl. Smell the sulphur. This is the city's drawing room.

name: Kuirau Park
short: A free geothermal park named for the maiden Kuirau who was pulled into a boiling pool by a taniwha.
commentary: Kuirau Park features boiling mud pools, steaming vents, and a free public foot bath — all in the middle of town. One of the few places in the world where geothermal activity occurs in an urban park.

reviews.summary: Kuirau Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A free geothermal park named for the maiden Kuirau who was pulled into a boiling pool by a taniwha. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Mokoia Island, Lake Rotorua
short: The island of the Hinemoa and Tūtānekai love story — a fortified pā at the heart of Te Arawa identity.
commentary: Mokoia Island is the setting for the famous love story of Hinemoa and Tūtānekai. But it was also a major fortified pā attacked during the Musket Wars in 1823. Access is by boat.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Mokoia Island, Lake Rotorua as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The island of the Hinemoa and Tūtānekai love story — a fortified pā at the heart of Te Arawa identity. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Whare Taonga o Te Arawa — Rotorua Museum
short: The former Tudor-style bathhouse housing Te Arawa taonga — telling the story of the people and the land.
