Cruise beneath towering cliffs and waterfalls in the eighth wonder of the world. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Milford Sound, Piopiotahi, widely regarded as the most stunning natural attraction in New Zealand. As your cruise vessel glides down the 16-kilometre fiord, the scale of this place will take your breath away. Mitre Peak rises 1,692 metres directly from the dark water — that's five times the height of the Empire State Building. Waterfalls plunge from hanging valleys hundreds of metres above, and your captain will nose the boat right under Stirling Falls, close enough to feel the spray on your face. The fiord receives around seven metres of rainfall per year, and after heavy rain, temporary waterfalls cascade from every cliff face in their thousands. Watch the rocks at the water's edge for New Zealand fur seals, and keep your eyes on the surface for bottlenose dolphins. Rudyard Kipling wasn't exaggerating when he called this the eighth wonder of the world.

name: Motupōhue / Bluff Hill
short: The southernmost point of the South Island — a Ngāi Tahu landmark with pā sites and views to Rakiura.
commentary: The hill was a pā site and lookout. Bluff oysters, pāua, and access to the tītī islands provided rich food resources. Tītī harvesting rights are Treaty-protected.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Motupōhue / Bluff Hill as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The southernmost point of the South Island — a Ngāi Tahu landmark with pā sites and views to Rakiura. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Murihiku Marae, Invercargill
short: One of the southernmost marae in the world — representing Ngāi Tahu at the bottom of the world.
commentary: Murihiku means 'the tail end of the land'. The marae embodies the enduring Ngāi Tahu presence in the deep south.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Murihiku Marae, Invercargill as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of the southernmost marae in the world — representing Ngāi Tahu at the bottom of the world. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ōmaui Track — Southern Coastal Heritage
short: A coastal walking track near Bluff passing archaeological sites — centuries of Māori coastal settlement.
commentary: Shell middens, stone tool-working areas, and camp sites. Views across Foveaux Strait to Stewart Island.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ōmaui Track — Southern Coastal Heritage as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A coastal walking track near Bluff passing archaeological sites — centuries of Māori coastal settlement. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Oreti Beach Drive
short: Drive along the vast Oreti Beach where Burt Munro tested his famous Indian motorcycle.
commentary: Oreti Beach stretches 26 km along the southern coast west of Invercargill. It is one of New Zealand's few beaches where vehicles are permitted. Burt Munro, the subject of the film 'The World's Fastest Indian,' used Oreti Beach to test his modified 1920 Indian Scout motorcycle before setting world speed records at Bonneville. The beach is also a traditional toheroa shellfish gathering site.

reviews.summary: The Oreti Beach Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive along the vast Oreti Beach where Burt Munro tested his famous Indian motorcycle. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving onto Oreti Beach, and the sand stretches so far in both directions that the ends disappear into sea mist. This 26-kilometre beach is one of the few in New Zealand where you can drive a car on the sand, and it has a special place in Kiwi mythology. This is where Burt Munro — the Invercargill local immortalised in 'The World's Fastest Indian' — tested his heavily modified 1920 Indian Scout motorcycle, coaxing impossible speeds from an engine he rebuilt in his garden shed over 40 years. Munro went on to set world speed records at Bonneville that still stand today. The beach itself is wild and windswept, facing the Southern Ocean with nothing between you and Antarctica but grey sea. In the right conditions, surfers ride the break at the western end, and local Maori have gathered toheroa shellfish here for centuries.

name: Piopiotahi / Milford Sound
short: Named for the piopio bird — the goddess Hinenuitepo released sandflies here so people wouldn't stay forever.
commentary: The fiords were carved by the tū god Tū-te-raki-whanoa. He made each more beautiful than the last. Piopiotahi was so beautiful that sandflies were released to drive humans away.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Piopiotahi / Milford Sound as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Named for the piopio bird — the goddess Hinenuitepo released sandflies here so people wouldn't stay forever. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Rakiura / Stewart Island — The Glowing Skies
short: Rakiura means 'glowing skies' — named for the aurora australis. Stewart Island is the anchor stone of Māui's canoe.
commentary: In Māori cosmology, the South Island is Te Waka a Māui and Stewart Island is the anchor stone. Ngāi Tahu tītī harvesting continues today.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Rakiura / Stewart Island — The Glowing Skies as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Rakiura means 'glowing skies' — named for the aurora australis. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Aparima / Riverton — Earliest Southern Settlement
short: Māori occupied this area for centuries before Europeans — drawn by rich food resources.
commentary: Te Hikoi Southern Journey Museum tells the story of both Māori and European settlement. The town retains a distinctive bicultural character.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Aparima / Riverton — Earliest Southern Settlement as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Māori occupied this area for centuries before Europeans — drawn by rich food resources. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Riverton / Aparima Heritage Walk
short: Explore one of NZ's oldest European settlements, a whaling town where Maori and Pakeha history intertwine.
commentary: Riverton / Aparima, established in 1836, is one of the earliest permanent European settlements in New Zealand. The town developed around shore whaling and sealing operations and had a notably mixed Maori-European community from its earliest days. Te Hikoi Museum tells the intertwined stories of tangata whenua and early settlers.

reviews.summary: The Riverton / Aparima Heritage Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Explore one of NZ's oldest European settlements, a whaling town where Maori and Pakeha history intertwine. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking through Riverton, and this quiet seaside town at the mouth of the Aparima River holds a claim that few New Zealand settlements can match — it has been continuously inhabited by Europeans since 1836, making it one of the oldest Pakeha settlements in the country. But the story here is not just European. Riverton developed as a place where Maori and Pakeha lived and worked together from the beginning, intermarrying and trading in a way that was unusual for the era. The shore whalers who set up here needed Maori knowledge and cooperation, and the result was a community of mixed heritage. Te Hikoi Museum on the main street tells this story beautifully. Walk along the estuary where the whaling boats once launched and you will find a town that wears its age gracefully.

name: Riverton Surfing
short: Surf the powerful Southern Ocean swells at New Zealand's deep south beaches.
commentary: Riverton and nearby Colac Bay offer consistent surf breaks powered by Southern Ocean swells. The beach breaks suit intermediate to experienced surfers, with occasional world-class conditions. A thick wetsuit is essential in these chilly waters. The small town of Riverton is one of New Zealand's oldest settlements.

reviews.summary: The Riverton Surfing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Surf the powerful Southern Ocean swells at New Zealand's deep south beaches. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the wild shores of Riverton, about as far south as surfing gets in New Zealand. The Southern Ocean delivers powerful, consistent swells that break along the sandy beaches of Riverton and nearby Colac Bay, producing waves that can rival anything further north. The water is cold — properly cold — so a good thick wetsuit is non-negotiable, but that's a small price for the waves and the solitude you'll find here. You might share the break with one or two locals, but crowd pressure is virtually unknown. The small town of Riverton, known as Aparima, is one of the oldest European settlements in New Zealand, founded by whalers and sealers in the early 1800s. After your surf, the local fish and chips are legendary. This is raw, authentic, Southern Ocean surfing at the bottom of the world.

name: Milford Sound Scuba Diving
short: Dive Milford Sound's unique freshwater layer to discover deep-water species in the shallows.
commentary: Milford Sound diving is unique due to a permanent layer of tannin-stained freshwater sitting atop the saltwater, blocking sunlight and allowing deep-water species like black coral to grow at remarkably shallow depths. Guided dives explore cliff walls encrusted with rare black and red corals, anemones, and unusual fish species.

reviews.summary: The Milford Sound Scuba Diving adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Dive Milford Sound's unique freshwater layer to discover deep-water species in the shallows. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at one of the most extraordinary dive sites in the world. Milford Sound receives an average of seven metres of rainfall per year, creating a permanent layer of dark, tannin-stained freshwater that sits on top of the saltwater. This freshwater layer blocks sunlight, tricking deep-water marine organisms into growing at incredibly shallow depths. The result is a diver's wonderland — black coral trees that normally grow at 100 metres depth thrive here at just 15 metres. The cliff walls are encrusted with delicate red coral, jewel anemones, brachiopods, and other creatures that most divers only ever see on deep technical dives. As you descend through the freshwater layer into the clear saltwater below, it's like passing through a curtain into another world. Milford Sound's underwater environment has been compared to diving in the deep sea without the depth. It's genuinely unique — there is nowhere else on earth quite like this.

name: Slope Point
short: The southernmost point of the South Island mainland — a bleak cliff and a famous row of wind-sculpted macrocarpa trees.
commentary: Slope Point, in the Catlins, is the southernmost tip of the South Island. Accessed by a 20 minute walk across farmland.

reviews.summary: Slope Point draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The southernmost point of the South Island mainland — a bleak cliff and a famous row of wind-sculpted macrocarpa trees. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have come to Slope Point, the southernmost tip of the South Island mainland, and you are about to understand why the southern trees grow sideways. The access track crosses a working sheep paddock, about twenty minutes each way, and is closed during spring lambing. At the end of the walk you reach a low clifftop above a grey Southern Ocean that continues, uninterrupted, all the way to Antarctica. There is nothing between you and the ice. A yellow sign on a post marks the point with the distances to the equator and to the South Pole. The wind here is the wind of the Roaring Forties, and it never really stops. On your way to the point you will pass the famously leaning row of macrocarpa trees, planted as a shelter belt decades ago, now bent almost flat to the ground by the constant prevailing wind. The trees are one of the most photographed sights in New Zealand, and the photograph does not quite prepare you for the reality of standing beside them. Bring a warm coat even on a summer day. This is the edge of a continent. Stand for a few minutes. Then turn around and go home.

name: Southern Scenic Route
short: A 610 km scenic route linking Queenstown to Dunedin via Te Anau, Invercargill, and the Catlins.
commentary: The Southern Scenic Route covers approximately 610 km from Queenstown to Dunedin via Te Anau, Riverton, Invercargill, and the Catlins coast. It is one of New Zealand's designated scenic routes and passes through some of the least-visited parts of the South Island. Key stops include the old whaling town of Riverton (est. 1836), Colac Bay, and Fortrose.

reviews.summary: The Southern Scenic Route route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A 610 km scenic route linking Queenstown to Dunedin via Te Anau, Invercargill, and the Catlins. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the Southern Scenic Route, and this 610-kilometre road takes you through the deep south of New Zealand — a region most tourists miss entirely. From Queenstown, the route heads to Te Anau and then turns south through farmland to Riverton, one of the oldest European settlements in New Zealand, established in 1836 as a shore whaling station. The road follows the southern coast through Colac Bay, where surfers ride breaks that arrive unimpeded from the Southern Ocean, and on through Invercargill with its broad, wind-swept streets. East of the city, the route enters the Catlins, where wild coastline, native forest, and wildlife replace the farmland. This is driving for people who want to see the real New Zealand — no adventure tourism, no boutique hotels, just land and sea and the occasional farmhouse smoke rising against a grey sky.

name: Rakiura / Stewart Island Nature Experience
short: Spot wild kiwi on their beach at New Zealand's third island — Rakiura.
commentary: Rakiura / Stewart Island is New Zealand's third largest island and one of the best places to see wild kiwi in their natural habitat. Guided evening walks on Ocean Beach offer reliable kiwi sightings. The island has 280 km of walking tracks, pristine beaches, and 85% of its land is national park.

reviews.summary: The Rakiura / Stewart Island Nature Experience adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Spot wild kiwi on their beach at New Zealand's third island — Rakiura. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Rakiura, Stewart Island — New Zealand's third and most remote island, where the native bush is so dense and the human population so small that the kiwi outnumber the people. This is one of the very few places in New Zealand where you can reliably see wild kiwi in their natural habitat, feeding on the beaches at dusk. Guided walks to Ocean Beach time the visit for when the Southern Tokoeka kiwi emerge from the bush to forage in the sand. Watching a wild kiwi probe the beach with its long bill, utterly unconcerned by your presence, is one of the most special wildlife experiences in the country. Eighty-five percent of the island is national park, and its 280 kilometres of walking tracks explore ancient forests, deserted beaches, and coastline that faces nothing but the Southern Ocean all the way to Antarctica.

name: Rakiura Track (Stewart Island)
short: Walk the Great Walk on New Zealand's third island — a 32 km loop through primeval bush and wild coast.
commentary: The Rakiura Track is a 32 km, 3-day Great Walk loop on Stewart Island / Rakiura, starting and finishing in Oban. The track passes through dense podocarp and coastal forest, along sheltered shorelines, and through some of the most pristine bush remaining in New Zealand. Stewart Island has a population of about 400 people and is one of the best places to see kiwi in the wild.

reviews.summary: The Rakiura Track (Stewart Island) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk the Great Walk on New Zealand's third island — a 32 km loop through primeval bush and wild coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are stepping onto the Rakiura Track on Stewart Island, New Zealand's third island, and the bush here is as close to primeval as you will find anywhere in the country. The 32-kilometre loop takes three days and circles through dense podocarp forest — rimu, miro, and kamahi trees draped in mosses and ferns so thick the forest floor is permanently soft underfoot. Stewart Island has just 400 human residents but uncountable kiwi, and this is one of the very best places in New Zealand to see one in the wild. Join a guided evening walk on Ocean Beach: as darkness falls and your eyes adjust, the unmistakable silhouette of a kiwi probing the sand with its long bill appears — a moment that stays with you. The track also follows the coastline of Paterson Inlet, where the water is glass-calm and the only sounds are bellbirds and the lap of the tide.

name: Takitimu Mountains Drive
short: Drive beneath the Takitimu Mountains, a remote range sacred to Ngai Tahu on the Fiordland fringe.
commentary: The Takitimu Mountains are a remote range rising to 1,580 m between Te Anau and Riverton. The range is sacred to Ngai Tahu, who identify it as the upturned hull of the ancestral waka Takitimu. The back roads beneath the range pass through rolling farmland with few other vehicles, offering views of a mountain range that most visitors never see.

reviews.summary: The Takitimu Mountains Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive beneath the Takitimu Mountains, a remote range sacred to Ngai Tahu on the Fiordland fringe. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the back roads beneath the Takitimu Mountains, and chances are you have never heard of this range. Most visitors to Southland are focused on Fiordland to the west, but the Takitimu rises to 1,580 metres from the farmland east of Te Anau, dramatic and largely unvisited. For Ngai Tahu, these mountains are the upturned hull of the ancestral waka Takitimu, one of the great canoes that brought Maori to New Zealand. The ridge does indeed look like an overturned canoe from certain angles. The roads around the base are quiet — mostly gravel, mostly empty — and the farmland has a quality of deep, settled peace that feels rare in a world that moves fast. If you want to see Southland as locals know it, away from every tourist trail, this is where you drive.

name: Te Anau — Gateway to Fiordland
short: Te Anau is the gateway to fiords carved by the gods — the lake was an important Ngāi Tahu travel route.
commentary: Lake Te Anau was part of the network of routes used by Ngāi Tahu to reach the fiords and their pounamu resources. The name may refer to 'the cave of swirling water', perhaps the Te Anau Caves with their glowworms.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Anau — Gateway to Fiordland as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Te Anau is the gateway to fiords carved by the gods — the lake was an important Ngāi Tahu travel route. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Anau Glow Worm Caves
short: Boat into ancient caves lit by thousands of glow-worms on Lake Te Anau.
commentary: The Te Anau Glow Worm Caves are accessed by a short boat ride across Lake Te Anau. Rediscovered in 1948, the caves feature rushing underground streams and a grotto illuminated by thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa glow-worms. The cave system is geologically young, still being actively carved by water.

reviews.summary: The Te Anau Glow Worm Caves adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Boat into ancient caves lit by thousands of glow-worms on Lake Te Anau. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the shores of Lake Te Anau, where a boat is waiting to carry you across the lake to one of New Zealand's most magical underground experiences. The Te Anau Glow Worm Caves were rediscovered in 1948 by local explorer Lawson Burrows, though Maori knew of them centuries earlier. You'll walk through caverns carved by rushing underground streams, past whirlpools and waterfalls, deeper and deeper into the limestone. Then the lights go out, and you drift by boat into the glow-worm grotto. Thousands of tiny Arachnocampa luminosa larvae light up the cave ceiling like a constellation, their blue-green glow reflecting off the still water. The silence, the darkness, and the living lights create an atmosphere that is genuinely otherworldly. These caves are still geologically active — the water is still carving, still creating — making them a living, breathing wonder.

name: Fiordland Horse Trekking
short: Ride horses through Fiordland farmland with mountain and lake views.
commentary: Horse trekking near Te Anau takes riders through high-country farmland and native bush with views of Lake Te Anau and the Fiordland mountains. Rides suit all experience levels, from gentle walks to longer backcountry treks. The area is home to working sheep and deer stations.

reviews.summary: The Fiordland Horse Trekking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride horses through Fiordland farmland with mountain and lake views. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the base for horse trekking in the Fiordland region, where working farmland meets true wilderness. Your horse will carry you through open tussock pastures with views across Lake Te Anau to the snow-capped peaks of Fiordland National Park. The high-country stations around Te Anau run sheep and deer across some of the most beautiful farmland in New Zealand, and riding through it gives you a genuine connection to the rural character of the deep south. The horses are calm and well-trained, so beginners are welcome, while more experienced riders can join longer treks that venture into native bush and river flats. The light in Fiordland has a quality all its own — clear, golden, and somehow deeper than anywhere else. On horseback, moving at the landscape's own pace, you absorb it all.

name: Te Anau to Manapouri Lakeshore Drive
short: A short lakeshore drive between two Fiordland gateway towns with mountain views across the water.
commentary: The 20 km road between Te Anau and Manapouri follows the shore of Lake Te Anau, New Zealand's second-largest lake, with views across the water to the Fiordland mountains. Te Anau is the gateway to Milford Sound and the Milford Track, while Manapouri is the departure point for Doubtful Sound cruises.

reviews.summary: The Te Anau to Manapouri Lakeshore Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A short lakeshore drive between two Fiordland gateway towns with mountain views across the water. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the short road between Te Anau and Manapouri, and do not rush this one. Lake Te Anau stretches to your left, the second-largest lake in New Zealand, its far shore backed by mountains that disappear into cloud. On a clear day, the peaks are reflected so perfectly in the lake that you cannot tell where the water ends and the sky begins. Te Anau itself is the gateway town for Fiordland — Milford Sound, the Milford Track, the Kepler Track all start from here. Manapouri, 20 kilometres south, is smaller and quieter, the departure point for Doubtful Sound. Between the two, this stretch of road gives you a gentle introduction to the scale of Fiordland, a national park so vast and wild that significant portions of it have never been explored on foot.

name: Tiwai Point Archaeological Sites
short: Moa-hunting camp sites from the earliest period of human occupation at the mouth of Bluff Harbour.
commentary: The finds include moa bone fragments, stone tools, and remnants of camps used by people hunting the giant birds.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Tiwai Point Archaeological Sites as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Moa-hunting camp sites from the earliest period of human occupation at the mouth of Bluff Harbour. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Hump Ridge Track
short: Walk a 61 km loop from coast to alpine ridge, crossing the spectacular Percy Burn Viaduct.
commentary: The Hump Ridge Track is a 61 km, 3-day loop in the Hump Ridge area west of Tuatapere. The track climbs from Te Waewae Bay coastline to alpine ridges at over 900 m, passing through native forest and crossing the Percy Burn Viaduct — one of the largest remaining wooden rail viaducts in the world. It became New Zealand's newest Great Walk in 2023.

reviews.summary: The Hump Ridge Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk a 61 km loop from coast to alpine ridge, crossing the spectacular Percy Burn Viaduct. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are starting the Hump Ridge Track at Rarakau on Te Waewae Bay, and over the next three days you will climb from wild southern coast to alpine ridge and back. The track became New Zealand's newest Great Walk in 2023, and it deserves the title. The first day follows the coast, where the Southern Ocean swells crash onto rocky platforms, before climbing steeply through podocarp forest to the first hut. The highlight of day two is the Percy Burn Viaduct — a massive wooden rail viaduct 36 metres high, built in 1930 for the timber industry and now one of the largest surviving wooden viaducts in the world. Walking across it, with the forest canopy at your feet and the valley falling away, is unforgettable. The alpine section of the ridge offers views south to Stewart Island and north to the Fiordland mountains.

name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara
short: A predator-free island in Paterson Inlet — a short water taxi from Stewart Island and alive with birds.
commentary: 266-hectare predator-free sanctuary off Stewart Island. Home to South Island saddleback, yellowhead, weka, kiwi, and many other species.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. A predator-free island in Paterson Inlet — a short water taxi from Stewart Island and alive with birds. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

audio_story: You have caught the water taxi across Paterson Inlet from Stewart Island to Ulva Island, known to Māori as Te Wharawhara. Ulva is two hundred and sixty six hectares of ancient rimu, rātā and kāmahi forest, and it is, crucially, entirely free of rats, stoats, possums and cats. That small miracle has been achieved over decades of patient Department of Conservation work, and the result is one of the most extraordinary experiences in the country. Within minutes of stepping off the jetty, you will be surrounded by native birds that are rare or absent almost everywhere else. The South Island saddleback and the yellowhead, both nearly extinct a generation ago, are common here. Weka patter along the tracks, unconcerned by humans. Kākā screech in the canopy. Bellbirds and tūī fill the understory with song. There is a good chance of seeing a Stewart Island tokoeka kiwi in daylight, because the species here is partly diurnal. A network of well formed walking tracks loops across the island, usually taking three to four hours at a slow pace. Bring lunch. Take nothing but photographs. Brush your boots for any hidden seeds before you step ashore.

name: Waiau River Scenic Road
short: Drive along the Waiau River from Lake Manapouri through remote farmland toward the coast.
commentary: The road from Manapouri follows the Waiau River south through remote Southland farmland to Tuatapere. The Waiau drains both Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapouri and is one of the most powerful rivers in Southland. The drive passes through a landscape of rolling green hills backed by the Takitimu Mountains and offers a glimpse of deep rural Southland.

reviews.summary: The Waiau River Scenic Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive along the Waiau River from Lake Manapouri through remote farmland toward the coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving south along the Waiau River from Manapouri, and the road here takes you through Southland as few tourists see it. The Waiau is a big river — it drains both Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapouri — and its green water moves with a steady, muscular current past farmland so quiet you can hear the birds from the car. The road follows the river valley between low hills, with the Takitimu Mountains occasionally appearing to the east. There are no towns for long stretches, just farms, sheep, and the kind of silence that takes getting used to. At Clifden, the historic suspension bridge — built in 1899 and now retired from vehicle use — is worth a stop. You can walk across it and feel it sway, imagining the horse-drawn carts that once crossed here when this was the main road south.

name: Waipapa Point Lighthouse
short: A wooden 1884 lighthouse built after the Tararua wreck — New Zealand's worst civilian maritime disaster.
commentary: Built 1884 after the 1881 wreck of the SS Tararua, in which 131 people died. The lighthouse area is also a popular sea lion haul-out.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waipapa Point Lighthouse — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A wooden 1884 lighthouse built after the Tararua wreck — New Zealand's worst civilian maritime disaster. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have arrived at Waipapa Point Lighthouse, a small white wooden tower on a low windswept headland at the south eastern corner of Southland. The lighthouse looks modest, almost apologetic. The reason it exists is anything but. On the twenty ninth of April eighteen eighty one, the steamship SS Tararua struck a reef just offshore here in the early hours of the morning. The ship broke up within a day. One hundred and thirty one people died, including women, children, and most of the crew. Only twenty survived. It remains the worst civilian maritime disaster in New Zealand's history. In the inquiry that followed, Waipapa Point was finally lit, and in eighteen eighty four the lighthouse you are looking at was built, one of the last remaining wooden lighthouses in the country. Today the keeper's cottage is gone, the light is automated, and the headland is a quiet sheep paddock. You may well find a sea lion sleeping on the short access path or on the rocks below. Give them a very wide berth, at least twenty metres, and never get between a sea lion and the sea. Stand at the lighthouse, look out at the reef, and consider the names on the memorial stone.

name: Waipapa Point — Shipwreck & Settlement
short: The southernmost lighthouse in NZ — built after the Tararua shipwreck on a coast long known to Māori.
commentary: The coast around Waipapa Point was used by Māori for food gathering long before the 1881 Tararua shipwreck prompted the lighthouse. The area is rich in coastal archaeological sites.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Waipapa Point — Shipwreck & Settlement as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The southernmost lighthouse in NZ — built after the Tararua shipwreck on a coast long known to Māori. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Whenua Hou / Codfish Island
short: A predator-free kākāpō sanctuary — co-managed by Ngāi Tahu and DOC.
commentary: Whenua Hou is one of the primary breeding sites for kākāpō. For Ngāi Tahu, the bird is a taonga species and their involvement in recovery is a deep cultural commitment.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Whenua Hou / Codfish Island earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. A predator-free kākāpō sanctuary — co-managed by Ngāi Tahu and DOC. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

name: Stewart Island / Rakiura
short: New Zealand's third island — 85% national park, home to wild kiwi that walk the beaches in daylight.
commentary: Rakiura — 'the glowing skies' — is the third and southernmost of New Zealand's main islands, 30 km across Foveaux Strait from Bluff. Population ~400, concentrated in the single village of Oban / Halfmoon Bay. 85% of the island is Rakiura National Park (NZ's youngest, 2002). Rakiura is the easiest place in NZ to see wild kiwi — the southern brown kiwi (tokoeka) emerges in daylight on some beaches, because there are few predators. The 32 km Rakiura Track Great Walk (3 days) is the best introduction. Access via 1-hour ferry from Bluff, or 20-minute flight from Invercargill. Aurora Australis visible on clear winter nights — this is one of NZ's few reliable aurora-viewing spots.

reviews.summary: Stewart Island / Rakiura draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. New Zealand's third island — 85% national park, home to wild kiwi that walk the beaches in daylight. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Te Anau
short: The gateway to Fiordland — on the shore of New Zealand's second-largest lake, with its own glow-worm caves.
commentary: Te Anau sits on the eastern shore of 344 km² Lake Te Anau, the country's second-largest lake (Taupō is larger) and its deepest (417 m). The town is Fiordland's service base — petrol, coaches to Milford, the only supermarket for 200 km. The Te Anau Glow-worm Caves — on the far side of the lake, reached by boat — are living, still-being-formed limestone caves whose ceilings are dotted with thousands of glow-worm larvae (*Arachnocampa luminosa*). Guided boat-and-walk tour, 2.25 hours. Also in town: the DOC Great Walks Centre (Kepler starts here); the endangered takahē bird live at the nearby wildlife park. Don't rush through.

reviews.summary: Te Anau draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The gateway to Fiordland — on the shore of New Zealand's second-largest lake, with its own glow-worm caves. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: The Chasm
short: A 20-minute loop walk through rainforest to a deep, sculpted waterfall pothole.
commentary: The Chasm is a roadside stop on State Highway 94 about 10 km before Milford Sound. A 20-minute loop through silver beech and moss-covered rainforest leads to a series of bridges across the Cleddau River, which has carved a network of plunge pools and potholes into the granite. The rock is sculpted into abstract shapes by centuries of water and gravel working round and round; the main cascade is ~22 m. One of the best short walks in Fiordland and a perfect leg-stretcher between Te Anau and Milford. Stop on the way into Milford, not on the way out, when light is better.

reviews.summary: The Chasm draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 20-minute loop walk through rainforest to a deep, sculpted waterfall pothole. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Tuatapere & Hump Ridge Track
short: The 'Sausage Capital of New Zealand' and the start of the 61 km Hump Ridge Track — the country's 11th Great Walk.
commentary: Tuatapere is a one-street milling town on the Waiau River in Western Southland. Its self-appointed title — 'Sausage Capital of NZ' — dates from the mid-20th century when the town's butchers competed for the country's best sausage prize. The town is also the start of the 61 km Hump Ridge Track, a loop through coastal Waitutu Forest, over the subalpine Hump Ridge (1,000 m, exposed), and back along the Fiordland south coast. Features: two historic 1925 timber viaducts, the sub-alpine tops, sea-cliff views across Te Waewae Bay. Upgraded to Great Walk status in 2024 — the country's 11th and one of its newest. 3 days of walking.

reviews.summary: Tuatapere & Hump Ridge Track draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The 'Sausage Capital of New Zealand' and the start of the 61 km Hump Ridge Track — the country's 11th Great Walk. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: East Cape Lighthouse / Whangaokeno
short: The easternmost lighthouse in the world — ~700 steps up a hill, and most of the year, the first place on Earth the sun touches.
commentary: The original light was built in 1900 on East Island (Whangaokeno), a small unstable rocky island offshore — landslides threatened the keepers' cottages and the whole operation was moved to this mainland hilltop in 1922. Three lighthouse keepers lived here until it was automated in 1985. The ~700-step climb is steep but short. On a clear winter dawn this is the first land on the planet to see the sun — although Kiribati's 1995 dateline shift technically gave Caroline Island the first new day. Geography is on our side; calendars aren't. Bring a torch if you're going for dawn, and more layers than you think.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at East Cape Lighthouse / Whangaokeno — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The easternmost lighthouse in the world — ~700 steps up a hill, and most of the year, the first place on Earth the sun touches. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Eastwoodhill Arboretum
short: The National Arboretum of New Zealand — one man's 80-year obsession, now 135 hectares of Northern Hemisphere trees.
commentary: William Douglas Cook bought this Ngātapa farm in 1910 and spent the rest of his life — until his death in 1967 — planting it. His logic: Europe's forests were being destroyed by war and industrial logging, and someone needed to preserve them. He imported thousands of species of trees, shrubs and climbers, laid out 25 km of paths, and built the extraordinary spring-flowering oak and magnolia collections for which Eastwoodhill is now famous. In 1975 Parliament declared it the National Arboretum of New Zealand — the country's largest collection of Northern Hemisphere woody plants, now ~135 ha with ~4,000 species. Best in spring (cherries, magnolias) and autumn (maples, oaks). 45 minutes west of Gisborne. Entry fee.

reviews.summary: Eastwoodhill Arboretum draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The National Arboretum of New Zealand — one man's 80-year obsession, now 135 hectares of Northern Hemisphere trees. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Morere Hot Springs
short: Hot saline pools in the middle of a nikau-palm rainforest — steamy water that started the journey as ancient seawater, now warmed underground.
commentary: Morere sits in a 364 ha scenic reserve on SH2 between Wairoa and Gisborne, protecting a rare coastal stand of nikau-palm rainforest. The pools are filled from a hot spring whose water originated as 10-million-year-old seawater trapped underground, now heated to 60 °C — it still tastes faintly salty. The reserve has four public pools (two cool, two hot) and private tubs for hire; the hottest pools sit right under the nikau canopy. A short forest walk through the reserve passes giant puriri and towering tawa. Small fee. 1 hour south of Gisborne, a useful stop on the SH2 run to Napier. Cafe at the entrance.

reviews.summary: Morere Hot Springs draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Hot saline pools in the middle of a nikau-palm rainforest — steamy water that started the journey as ancient seawater, now warmed underground. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Mt Hikurangi
short: The first point on the North Island to see each sunrise — the sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou, with nine massive carved pou near the summit.
commentary: Mt Hikurangi (1,752 m) is the highest non-volcanic mountain on the North Island and the first point on the mainland to receive each new day's light. For Ngāti Porou the mountain is an ancestor: in their tradition it is the prow of Māui's waka, which fished up the North Island. On the ridges below the summit stand nine 2-metre-tall carved wooden pou depicting Māui and his whānau — installed in 1999. Access is across Ngāti Porou farmland; a 4WD guided tour from Pakihiroa Station to the summit (or to the carvings, much lower) is the standard way. Unguided hiking requires a permit through Te Rūnanganui o Ngāti Porou. Weather is brutal — check forecasts, dress for worse than you expect.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Mt Hikurangi as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The first point on the North Island to see each sunrise — the sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou, with nine massive carved pou near the summit. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing Site
short: Where Cook landed on 9 October 1769, where Te Maro of Ngāti Oneone was killed, and where Māia had already built a pā generations earlier.
commentary: Cook's first New Zealand landfall was on 9 October 1769, at the mouth of the Tūranganui River. Nine Māori were killed across the first two days of contact, the first of them the Ngāti Oneone rangatira Te Maro. Cook, unable to secure supplies, named the place 'Poverty Bay' and sailed south. The older name — Tūranganui-a-Kiwa, 'the great standing place of Kīwa' — records the much earlier arrival of the Horouta waka captained by Kīwa, and of Māia, who built a pā here called Puhi Kai Iti. The 1906 Cook obelisk is still on site; since Tuia 250 (2019) the reserve has been reinterpreted with iwi-carved pou and signage that tells both stories. A short climb up Titirangi / Kaiti Hill brings you to the Ruatanuika lookout and a memorial to Te Maro.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Puhi Kai Iti / Cook Landing Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Cook landed on 9 October 1769, where Te Maro of Ngāti Oneone was killed, and where Māia had already built a pā generations earlier. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Rere Falls & Rere Rockslide
short: A 10-metre wide waterfall, and a 60-metre natural rock waterslide downstream — both free, both on the same river.
commentary: The Wharekopae River west of Gisborne gives you two remarkable free swim spots within 5 km of each other. Rere Falls is a 10 m wide, 5 m high curtain waterfall — you can walk behind it. A few minutes further upriver is the Rere Rockslide: a 60 m long smooth rock slab angled gently enough that you can slide down it on a boogie board, an inflatable, or just in shorts, into a deep pool at the bottom. Locals have been using it for generations. No charge, no lifeguard, no facilities — bring a boogie board or thick shorts (nothing white, nothing loose), a towel, and check the flow is safe. 45 minutes west of Gisborne on the way to Eastwoodhill.

reviews.summary: Rere Falls & Rere Rockslide draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 10-metre wide waterfall, and a 60-metre natural rock waterslide downstream — both free, both on the same river. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Sir Apirana Ngata Memorial, Waiomatatini
short: Memorial to Sir Apirana Ngata — the statesman who championed Māori language, culture, and land development.
commentary: Sir Apirana Ngata (1874–1950) was the greatest Māori political leader of the 20th century. He revived Māori arts, established Māori land development schemes, and championed te reo Māori. His face appears on NZ's $50 note.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Sir Apirana Ngata Memorial, Waiomatatini as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Memorial to Sir Apirana Ngata — the statesman who championed Māori language, culture, and land development. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: East Cape Lighthouse
short: The easternmost point of mainland NZ — 700 steps to the first sunrise, on Ngāti Porou ancestral land.
commentary: The lighthouse stands where the first rays of each day's sun touch the land. The 700-step climb rewards with vast ocean views. The East Coast is one of NZ's most culturally rich Māori regions.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe East Cape Lighthouse as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The easternmost point of mainland NZ — 700 steps to the first sunrise, on Ngāti Porou ancestral land. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Wainui Beach Surfing
short: Surf the consistent beach breaks at Gisborne's premier surfing beach.
commentary: Wainui Beach is Gisborne's main surfing beach, a long, exposed stretch that picks up swell from multiple directions. The beach has a surf club and multiple peaks spread along its length. Gisborne's position on the East Cape means it catches swell when other regions are flat.

reviews.summary: The Wainui Beach Surfing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Surf the consistent beach breaks at Gisborne's premier surfing beach. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Wainui Beach, the surfing heartland of Gisborne and one of the most consistent breaks on the East Coast. This long stretch of beach picks up swell from the north, east, and south, meaning there are waves here more often than not. Multiple peaks along the beach spread the crowd, and on a solid swell, the waves here can rival anything in the country. Gisborne sits further east than any other city in the world, and that exposure means it catches swells that miss the rest of the North Island entirely. The Wainui Surf Club has been nurturing young surfers here for decades, and many of New Zealand's top competitive surfers have trained on these waves. Whether you are a beginner looking for a gentle whitewater ride or an experienced surfer hunting barrels, Wainui has a peak for you. The sunrises from the water here are the first in the world.

name: Gisborne Deep Sea Fishing Charter
short: Fish for marlin, tuna, and mahi mahi on the productive Gisborne coast.
commentary: Gisborne's offshore waters offer excellent big game fishing, with blue and striped marlin, yellowfin tuna, and mahi mahi available in the warmer months. The continental shelf sits relatively close to shore, making for shorter runs to productive deep water.

reviews.summary: The Gisborne Deep Sea Fishing Charter adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Fish for marlin, tuna, and mahi mahi on the productive Gisborne coast. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Gisborne's inner harbour, where a charter boat is rigged and ready for a day on one of the North Island's most productive fishing grounds. The beauty of Gisborne's location is that the continental shelf sits remarkably close to shore, so you do not spend hours getting to the action. Within 30 to 40 minutes of leaving the harbour, you could be over deep water where blue and striped marlin cruise from January through April. Yellowfin tuna, mahi mahi, and albacore are also on the menu, along with bottom species like hapuku and bluenose for those who prefer to fish deep. Your skipper knows these waters intimately, reading the temperature breaks and current lines where the fish congregate. The East Cape coastline is stunning from the water, and there is nothing quite like fighting a big fish with the first sunrise in the world at your back.

name: Gisborne Historical Walking Trail
short: Walk through Gisborne's history from Cook's 1769 landing site to the meeting of three rivers.
commentary: Gisborne stands at the site where Captain Cook first set foot on New Zealand on 9 October 1769. The historical trail passes Kaiti Hill with its Cook statue, the Titirangi reserve, and the confluence of the Waimata, Taruheru, and Turanganui rivers. The city is also significant as a centre of Maori culture and identity.

reviews.summary: The Gisborne Historical Walking Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk through Gisborne's history from Cook's 1769 landing site to the meeting of three rivers. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking through Gisborne, the first place Captain James Cook set foot in New Zealand on 9 October 1769. Climb Kaiti Hill — Titirangi — for the panoramic view Cook himself would have seen from the Endeavour: a wide bay backed by fertile plains, with three rivers converging at their mouth. The landing did not go well — a misunderstanding led to violence, and Cook named the area Poverty Bay because he could not obtain the supplies he needed. The name has been a source of grievance ever since, and there are ongoing discussions about reverting to the Maori name Turanganui-a-Kiwa. At the base of the hill, the Tairawhiti Museum tells the complex story of both Maori and Pakeha history in the region. Gisborne is the first city in the world to see the sunrise each day, and on New Year's Eve, thousands gather here to welcome the new year before anyone else on the planet. Walk along the riverbank to where the three rivers meet — it is a beautiful, contemplative spot in the heart of town.

name: Mount Hikurangi
short: Sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou — the first place on mainland NZ to see the sunrise.
commentary: Mount Hikurangi (1,752m) is where Māui's waka was turned to stone. Whakairo on the summit represent the crew. Access requires permission from Ngāti Porou — the pre-dawn climb to catch first light is unforgettable.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Mount Hikurangi as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Sacred mountain of Ngāti Porou — the first place on mainland NZ to see the sunrise. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Sir Āpirana Ngata Memorial, Waiomatatini
short: The home marae and memorial of Sir Āpirana Ngata — Ngāti Porou leader, first Māori university graduate, architect of twentieth-century Māori revival.
commentary: Sir Āpirana Turupa Ngata (1874–1950), Ngāti Porou, was the first Māori to graduate from a NZ university and served as an MP for 38 years. His home marae is at Waiomatatini.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Sir Āpirana Ngata Memorial, Waiomatatini as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The home marae and memorial of Sir Āpirana Ngata — Ngāti Porou leader, first Māori university graduate, architect of twentieth-century Māori revival. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are in the Waiapu Valley, the heartland of Ngāti Porou, and near a marae called Waiomatatini where one of the most influential Māori leaders of the twentieth century is buried. Sir Āpirana Turupa Ngata was born here in eighteen seventy four. He was the first Māori to gain a university degree in New Zealand, earning a bachelor of arts and then a law degree at Canterbury College. He went on to represent the Eastern Māori electorate in Parliament for thirty eight years, longer than almost anyone in New Zealand political history. Ngata is remembered for two great projects. The first was his effort to rebuild the Māori economy through land consolidation and farm development schemes that helped Ngāti Porou and other iwi retain control of what remained of their ancestral lands. The second was the cultural revival. Ngata personally organised the carving and building of dozens of wharenui across the country in the nineteen twenties and thirties, rescuing traditional arts that were on the edge of being lost. He died in nineteen fifty and is buried here in his home valley. Pass Waiomatatini with respect. Without Āpirana Ngata, much less of what is now called Māori culture would have survived the twentieth century intact.

name: Titirangi / Kaiti Hill Summit
short: Ancestral pā of Ngāti Oneone above Gisborne — the best interpretive view of Cooks landing, Young Nicks Head, and the first encounter.
commentary: Titirangi (Kaiti Hill) was a major pā of Ngāti Oneone. The summit lookout at Ruatanuika interprets the Cook arrival and the death of Te Maro. 15-minute walk from the carpark.

reviews.summary: Titirangi / Kaiti Hill Summit draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Ancestral pā of Ngāti Oneone above Gisborne — the best interpretive view of Cooks landing, Young Nicks Head, and the first encounter. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are climbing Titirangi, also known as Kaiti Hill, the steep headland that guards the northern entrance to Poverty Bay. Before colonisation this was one of the great pā of Ngāti Oneone, a terraced hill fort with a commanding view over the whole of Tūranganui a Kiwa. From the summit lookout at Ruatanuika you can see the entire landscape of first encounter. Below you, at the river mouth, is Puhi Kai Iti, where Cook came ashore in seventeen sixty nine and where Te Maro of Ngāti Oneone was shot dead within hours of arrival. Across the bay, the pale cliff of Young Nicks Head, Te Kurī a Pāoa, is the promontory Cooks surgeons boy first sighted from the rigging of the Endeavour. Out to sea, somewhere in the open water, the Endeavour herself lay at anchor. Closer to you, the streets of modern Gisborne lay out on the flat behind the beach, the apartment blocks and sports fields sitting on the old kumara gardens of the iwi. Since Tuia two hundred and fifty in twenty nineteen, the summit has been reinterpreted with pou carved by iwi artists, telling the story from both sides. A memorial to Te Maro stands at the very top. The walk up takes fifteen minutes. The view is one of the most historically dense in the country.

name: Manutuke Churches — Toko Toru Tapu & Te Mana o Turanga
short: Two elaborately carved Māori churches — among the finest fusions of Māori and Christian architecture in New Zealand.
commentary: Toko Toru Tapu (Holy Trinity) was built 1913, carved by masters of Rongowhakaata. The older Te Mana o Turanga whare nearby dates from 1883.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Manutuke Churches — Toko Toru Tapu & Te Mana o Turanga as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Two elaborately carved Māori churches — among the finest fusions of Māori and Christian architecture in New Zealand. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You have arrived at Manutuke, a small settlement south of Gisborne that contains one of the most remarkable concentrations of Māori carving in the country. The Anglican church of Toko Toru Tapu, the Holy Trinity, was completed in nineteen thirteen. Unlike almost any other church in New Zealand, its interior was entirely carved in the traditional manner by master carvers of Rongowhakaata, with painted tukutuku panels, woven kōwhaiwhai rafters, and poupou of ancestors standing along the walls. It is a Christian church, but one in which the architecture speaks in the visual grammar of the wharenui. Close by stands the wharenui Te Mana o Turanga, built in eighteen eighty three, which is considered one of the finest nineteenth century carved houses to survive in Aotearoa. The house is a living building of Rongowhakaata, not open for casual visits, but can sometimes be arranged through the iwi for interested visitors. Toko Toru Tapu is generally open to respectful visitors. Step quietly inside, take your hat off, and sit for a few minutes in the pews. You are sitting inside a work of art that took generations of skill to bring into being, and that continues to serve the community it was built for.

name: Matawhero
short: Site of the November 1868 attack by Te Kooti and his Ringatū followers — a night that left around 60 dead and still shadows the district.
commentary: On 10 November 1868, after escaping the Chathams, Te Kooti led a raid on Matawhero. Around 60 people were killed, including settlers and Māori not aligned with Ringatū. The church at Matawhero (1862) survives.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Matawhero as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Site of the November 1868 attack by Te Kooti and his Ringatū followers — a night that left around 60 dead and still shadows the district. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are at Matawhero, a small rural settlement a few kilometres west of Gisborne. On the night of the tenth of November, eighteen sixty eight, Te Kooti and a party of his Ringatū followers, recently escaped from imprisonment on the Chatham Islands, descended on this district. Over the course of that night and the following day, around sixty people were killed. The dead included colonial settler families, Māori who were not aligned with Te Kooti, and at least one magistrate whom Te Kooti held personally responsible for his unjust imprisonment. It is remembered in local settler history as the Matawhero Massacre, though the fuller picture includes the years of wrongful detention that preceded it and the cycles of colonial violence that followed. The small wooden Matawhero Church, built in eighteen sixty two, was the only structure the raiders deliberately spared on that night and still stands on its original site today. It is one of the oldest Anglican churches in the country. Stop at the church, read the memorial to those who died, and sit for a moment in the quiet of the old wooden interior. This is painful ground, carefully tended.

name: Māhia Peninsula — Rongomaiwahine Rohe
short: The ancestral rohe of Rongomaiwahine — the tūpuna wahine after whom the iwi of Māhia is named.
commentary: Māhia is the home of Rongomaiwahine iwi, named for a famous ancestress who married Kahungunu and then left him. The peninsula has been occupied for over 700 years.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Māhia Peninsula — Rongomaiwahine Rohe as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The ancestral rohe of Rongomaiwahine — the tūpuna wahine after whom the iwi of Māhia is named. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are driving onto the Māhia Peninsula, a rugged thumb of land jutting into the Pacific between Poverty Bay and Hawkes Bay. This is the rohe of Rongomaiwahine, an iwi named for one of the most famous ancestresses in Māori tradition. Rongomaiwahine was the senior wife of the great rangatira Kahungunu, but she is said to have left him because of his womanising and returned here, to her own lands, where she raised their children and maintained her mana in her own right. Her descendants have held this peninsula ever since. Māhia has been settled for over seven hundred years. There are pā sites on almost every headland, the remnants of an unbroken occupation by people who lived from the sea and from the kumara gardens of the sheltered eastern shore. In the twentieth century the peninsula became quietly famous for its surf beaches and its near total resistance to the worst kinds of tourism development. More recently, at the very southern tip of the peninsula, the Rocket Lab launch complex has put Māhia on maps of a different kind, firing small rockets to orbit from what is still essentially farmland. Old and new, in a single landscape.

name: Rongopai Wharenui, Patutahi
short: A remarkable painted wharenui built in 1887 to welcome Te Kooti home after his pardon — unique in NZ Māori architecture.
commentary: Rongopai was built 1887 by Te Whānau a Kai to welcome Te Kooti home to Tairāwhiti. When his return was blocked, the elaborate painted interior became a unique art treasure. Access only by arrangement with Te Whānau a Kai.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Rongopai Wharenui, Patutahi as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A remarkable painted wharenui built in 1887 to welcome Te Kooti home after his pardon — unique in NZ Māori architecture. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are near Patutahi, a small settlement west of Gisborne, and nearby stands a wharenui called Rongopai that is unlike any other in the country. It was built in eighteen eighty seven by the people of Te Whānau a Kai, specifically to welcome Te Kooti Arikirangi home to the East Coast after his pardon four years earlier. The interior of the whare is painted rather than carved, in bold figurative imagery that includes biblical figures, historical ancestors, flowering plants, and vivid colours. It was an experimental response to the loss of traditional carving materials and carvers during the wars, and it produced one of the most original interiors in Māori art history. Te Kooti, in the end, was not able to return. The colonial government continued to pressure him to stay away from Tairāwhiti, and he died in Ōhiwa in eighteen ninety three without ever seeing Rongopai. The whare stood empty of its intended welcome, but it remains a sacred house for Te Whānau a Kai today. The wharenui is not open to casual visitors. Access is only possible by arrangement with the iwi. Pass the marae with respect. The painted walls inside are one of the great secret treasures of Aotearoa.

name: Te Araroa & Hinerupe Marae
short: The village closest to the East Cape — and home to a 600-year-old pōhutukawa and the historic Hinerupe Marae.
commentary: Te Araroa sits on the broad curve of Kawakawa Bay near the East Cape. The giant pōhutukawa called Te Waha-o-Rerekohu in the school grounds is reputed to be over 600 years old.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Araroa & Hinerupe Marae as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The village closest to the East Cape — and home to a 600-year-old pōhutukawa and the historic Hinerupe Marae. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You have arrived at Te Araroa, the last real settlement before the East Cape lighthouse and the easternmost inhabited point of the New Zealand mainland. The village wraps around the broad curve of Kawakawa Bay, sheltered by high cliffs on both sides. At the local school grounds stands one of the most venerable trees in the country, a pōhutukawa called Te Waha o Rerekohu, reputed to be over six hundred years old. Beneath its enormous canopy generations of local children have played, waited for buses, and grown up. Nearby stands Hinerupe Marae, the principal marae of Te Whānau a Hinerupe, the hapū of Ngāti Porou who hold this land. The carvings on the wharenui trace the descent of the hapū from Porourangi and onward to the ancestors specific to this valley. This is a tight knit community. It is one of the few parts of the country where the first language you hear at the school gate is still likely to be te reo Māori, spoken naturally between grandparents and children. Buy something at the store. Fill up with fuel before you push on to the cape itself. And if you are lucky enough to pass through when a hui is being held, keep your distance and your voice low.

name: Te Kooti Arikirangi Birthplace Area — Te Karaka
short: Near where Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Tūruki was born around 1832 — founder of the Ringatū faith and one of the most controversial figures of New Zealand history.
commentary: Te Kooti was born near Te Karaka to a Rongowhakaata father. Wrongly imprisoned on the Chathams in 1866, he led a famous escape in 1868 and founded the Ringatū Church.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Kooti Arikirangi Birthplace Area — Te Karaka as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Near where Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Tūruki was born around 1832 — founder of the Ringatū faith and one of the most controversial figures of New Zealand history. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are travelling through the flat farming country of Te Karaka, a small settlement in the Waipaoa Valley north of Gisborne. Somewhere close to here, around the year eighteen thirty two, a child was born to a Rongowhakaata family who would grow up to become one of the most extraordinary and divisive figures in New Zealand history. His name was Te Kooti Arikirangi Te Tūruki. As a young man Te Kooti was arrested by the colonial government, without trial, on the charge of spying for Māori forces. He protested his innocence but was deported with hundreds of other prisoners to the Chatham Islands in eighteen sixty six. On the islands, during his two years of imprisonment, he experienced a series of visions and began preaching a new faith founded on the Old Testament, which became known as Ringatū, meaning the upraised hand. In eighteen sixty eight Te Kooti and his followers seized a schooner and escaped back to the mainland, landing on the Poverty Bay coast. What followed was four years of war across the central North Island. Te Kooti was never captured. He was pardoned in eighteen eighty three. The Ringatū Church he founded remains a living faith today. Pass this land with quiet attention.

name: Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae, Kaiti
short: One of the largest wharenui in New Zealand — home of Ngāti Oneone and Te Aitanga-a-Māhaki. Viewed respectfully from the road.
commentary: Te Poho-o-Rawiri is the principal marae of Ngāti Oneone, built in 1929 after the previous wharenui burned. At 28m long it is among the largest wharenui in Aotearoa.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae, Kaiti as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of the largest wharenui in New Zealand — home of Ngāti Oneone and Te Aitanga-a-Māhaki. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are passing Te Poho o Rāwiri, one of the great wharenui of Aotearoa. This is the principal marae of Ngāti Oneone, the iwi of the Kaiti foreshore, and the place where Cook himself first encountered tangata whenua in seventeen sixty nine. The present wharenui was built in nineteen twenty nine to replace an earlier building that had burned. At twenty eight metres long it is among the largest wharenui in the country. The elaborately carved interior includes panels that tell the story of Kiwa, the captain of the Horouta waka who gave the traditional name of this region, Tūranganui a Kiwa. The house is not open to casual tourists. It is a working marae, a living building, used for tangihanga, weddings, hui, and the tangible business of Ngāti Oneone as an iwi. From the road you can see the grand bargeboards and the carved amo of the porch. Pause for a moment to acknowledge the mana of this place, and then continue. If you have genuine business to bring to the marae, it must be arranged formally with the iwi in advance. Behind the marae rises Titirangi, the sacred hill, whose summit you can visit by a short walk and where the wider story of this coastline is told.

name: Cooks Cove, Tolaga Bay
short: Where Cook and Banks came ashore for six days in October 1769 — the first peaceable encounter of the Endeavour voyage.
commentary: After the violent encounter at Gisborne, Cook anchored at Tolaga Bay from 23 to 29 October 1769 and traded peacefully with Te Aitanga-a-Hauiti. The Cooks Cove walkway (2 hours return) leads to the landing.

reviews.summary: Cooks Cove, Tolaga Bay draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Where Cook and Banks came ashore for six days in October 1769 — the first peaceable encounter of the Endeavour voyage. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are at Cooks Cove, the small sheltered bay at the southern end of Tolaga Bay. After the violent first encounters at Tūranganui a Kiwa further south, Cook sailed the Endeavour north and anchored here on the twenty third of October, seventeen sixty nine. He and his crew stayed for six days. The encounter with the local iwi, Te Aitanga a Hauiti, was peaceful. Water was taken on from the cove stream, the botanists Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander collected plant specimens from the coastal forest, and the astronomer Charles Green made observations from shore. In return Cook gave cloth, nails and iron tools. It was the first peaceful encounter of the Endeavour voyage with Māori, and some of the most detailed journal descriptions of early Māori life in Tairāwhiti come from the week spent here. The walkway to the cove from Tolaga Bay beach takes about two hours return. The path climbs through farm paddocks, drops into the sheltered cove, and finishes at a small beach where a natural rock arch called the Hole in the Wall is visible just offshore. The old watering hole is still there. The landscape is almost unchanged. Stand at the tideline and imagine the Endeavour at anchor in the bay.

name: Waerenga-a-Hika Battle Site
short: Where a Pai Mārire pā was besieged and taken in November 1865 — the battle that led to Te Kootis unjust arrest.
commentary: In November 1865 colonial forces and pro-government Māori besieged the Pai Mārire (Hauhau) pā at Waerenga-a-Hika. After a week it fell. Prisoners including Te Kooti were sent to the Chathams.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Waerenga-a-Hika Battle Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where a Pai Mārire pā was besieged and taken in November 1865 — the battle that led to Te Kootis unjust arrest. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are at Waerenga a Hika, the site of a week long siege in November eighteen sixty five that few people today remember but whose consequences rippled across the next decade of New Zealand history. The followers of the Pai Mārire faith, a syncretic Māori religious movement born in Taranaki, had built a fortified pā on this ground. A force of colonial militia and pro government Māori surrounded the pā on the seventeenth of November. For seven days the defenders held out under fire. By the twenty fourth of November, short of water and with their earthworks crumbling, the defenders surrendered. Around seventy one Māori were killed in the fighting. Several hundred prisoners were taken, and in the chaotic aftermath a number of men who had taken no part in the fighting were also arrested, including a Rongowhakaata rangatira named Te Kooti Arikirangi. All were shipped to the Chatham Islands for indefinite imprisonment without trial. It was on the Chathams that Te Kooti founded the Ringatū faith. Three years later his dramatic escape and the violence that followed changed the East Coast forever. Everything that came later, began here, on this quiet paddock.

name: Waiapu Valley & Ngāti Porou Cathedral
short: The heartland of Ngāti Porou — and the site of St Marys Tikitiki, the most extraordinary Māori church in Aotearoa.
commentary: The Waiapu Valley on the northern East Coast is the ancestral heartland of Ngāti Porou. St Marys Church, Tikitiki (1924–26), with its entirely carved and painted interior, was built as a memorial to Ngāti Porou who died in WWI.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waiapu Valley & Ngāti Porou Cathedral — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The heartland of Ngāti Porou — and the site of St Marys Tikitiki, the most extraordinary Māori church in Aotearoa. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are driving through the Waiapu Valley, the ancestral heartland of Ngāti Porou, the iwi of the northern East Coast. Every marae you pass here, every small church and every carved gateway, belongs to hapū of Ngāti Porou who trace their descent from Porourangi, the eponymous ancestor who lived on these rivers perhaps thirty generations ago. At the village of Tikitiki you will find St Marys Anglican Church. From outside it looks modest. Inside, it is extraordinary. Built between nineteen twenty four and nineteen twenty six as a memorial to the Ngāti Porou soldiers who died in the First World War, the interior is entirely carved and painted in the traditional manner. Poupou along the walls represent the ancestors of each hapū. The pulpit is a carved waka. The stained glass windows combine Christian iconography with Māori symbols. Sir Āpirana Ngata, whose home was a few kilometres away, drove the project personally, and the result is widely considered the most beautiful Māori church in New Zealand. The church is usually open during the day. Walk slowly around the interior. Every poupou represents a family, every panel a story, and together they are a single memorial to a generation that did not come home.

name: Young Nicks Head / Te Kurī
short: The white cliffs first seen from the Endeavour on 6 October 1769 — named for the surgeons boy who spotted them. Known to Māori as Te Kurī a Pāoa.
commentary: The headland was the first land sighted by Cooks Endeavour on 6 October 1769 — spotted by young Nicholas Young. Its Māori name, Te Kurī a Pāoa, commemorates the dog of Pāoa, captain of the Horouta waka.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Young Nicks Head / Te Kurī as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The white cliffs first seen from the Endeavour on 6 October 1769 — named for the surgeons boy who spotted them. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are looking at Young Nicks Head, the distinctive white cliffed headland that closes the southern end of Poverty Bay. On the afternoon of the sixth of October, seventeen sixty nine, the young surgeons boy aboard the Endeavour, Nicholas Young, climbed the rigging and was the first of Cooks crew to sight New Zealand. The promised reward was a gallon of rum and the right to have the headland named after him. It was the first sighting of Aotearoa by a European since Abel Tasman glimpsed the West Coast one hundred and twenty seven years earlier. Māori have a much older name for the place. They call it Te Kurī a Pāoa, the dog of Pāoa, after a famous hunting dog owned by Pāoa, the captain of the Horouta waka, which beached on this coast many generations before Cook. The headland can be seen clearly from the centre of Gisborne and from Kaiti Hill. Both names appear on modern maps. Both names are officially recognised.
