Hold both in your mind as you look across the bay. Two arrivals, separated by perhaps six hundred years, both announcing themselves at the same white cliff.

name: Kaiti Hill / Titirangi — First Contact Site
short: Where Māori and Europeans first met — Cook landed at the foot of this pā hill in October 1769.
commentary: On 8 October 1769, Cook's Endeavour anchored here and the first meetings between Māori and Europeans took place — tragically, several Māori were shot. The summit pā terraces are still visible.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Kaiti Hill / Titirangi — First Contact Site as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Māori and Europeans first met — Cook landed at the foot of this pā hill in October 1769. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mahia Peninsula Sea Kayaking
short: Kayak the sheltered bays of Mahia Peninsula among seals, penguins, and reef fish.
commentary: The Mahia Peninsula offers sheltered sea kayaking in a remote and beautiful setting. Multiple launch points access rocky coastline, sandy beaches, and reef systems. The peninsula is home to fur seal colonies, little blue penguins, and diverse marine life.

reviews.summary: The Mahia Peninsula Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak the sheltered bays of Mahia Peninsula among seals, penguins, and reef fish. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Mahia Peninsula, a remote and spectacularly beautiful headland that juts into the Pacific between Hawke's Bay and Poverty Bay. Sea kayaking here is an exercise in coastal discovery, paddling between rocky headlands and into sheltered coves that feel like they belong to another era entirely. The peninsula's reefs are rich with marine life, and the clear water lets you watch fish darting below your kayak as you paddle. Fur seals haul out on the rocks at the peninsula's tip, and little blue penguins nest along the coastline. Mahia is also the home of Rocket Lab's launch facility, so you might spot the launch pad on the eastern side. The waters here are sheltered from the prevailing swell on the harbour side, making conditions manageable even for intermediate paddlers. Bring your own kayak or arrange hire through local operators. This is East Coast exploration at its most peaceful.

name: Mahia Peninsula Coastal Loop
short: Drive the loop around Mahia Peninsula, where surf beaches meet a modern rocket launch site.
commentary: Mahia Peninsula is a remote coastal area south of Gisborne known for excellent surf breaks and rugged scenery. Rocket Lab's Launch Complex 1, the world's first private orbital launch site, is located at the tip of the peninsula. The area has significant cultural importance to Rongomaiwahine iwi.

reviews.summary: The Mahia Peninsula Coastal Loop route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the loop around Mahia Peninsula, where surf beaches meet a modern rocket launch site. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the loop road around Mahia Peninsula, where the ancient and the futuristic exist side by side. This rugged headland juts into the Pacific, its eastern cliffs battered by ocean swells that wrap around to create excellent surf breaks at Opoutama and Mahia Beach. The peninsula is the rohe — traditional territory — of Rongomaiwahine iwi, and the cultural connection to this land runs deep. Then, at the peninsula's tip, you encounter something unexpected: Rocket Lab's Launch Complex 1, the world's first private orbital launch site. Since 2017, Electron rockets have been launched from this remote New Zealand headland into orbit, delivering satellites for clients worldwide. If you time your visit during a launch window, you might witness a rocket arcing into space from this pastoral coastline — a surreal juxtaposition. The beaches here are uncrowded, the fishing is excellent, and the drive itself offers sweeping views of Hawke Bay to the south.

name: Anaura Bay & Cook's Cove Walkway
short: Where Captain Cook's crew was warmly welcomed in 1769 — a beautiful bay and the short walk to the hidden cove where the Endeavour took on water.
commentary: Cook landed at Anaura Bay on 21 October 1769 and was greeted hospitably. He moved on briefly to the more sheltered Cook's Cove (Tolaga Bay area), which is now a 5.8km return walk.

reviews.summary: Anaura Bay & Cook's Cove Walkway draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Where Captain Cook's crew was warmly welcomed in 1769 — a beautiful bay and the short walk to the hidden cove where the Endeavour took on water. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Anaura Bay, a wide perfect crescent of sand on the East Coast, and one of the more historically significant beaches you will probably have ever to yourself. On the twenty first of October seventeen sixty nine, Captain Cook landed here and was met not with violence but with hospitality. Local Māori brought sweet potato and water aboard the Endeavour, and Cook recorded the cleanliness and order of the cultivated gardens with what reads, between the lines of his journal, as something close to admiration. The wind shifted, his anchor dragged, and he moved south to the more sheltered Cook's Cove the next day. From the southern end of the beach, a beautiful walking track climbs across farmland and through bush to the lookout above Cook's Cove itself. The walk is around two and a half hours return. It ends at a wooden viewing platform looking down on the small green bay where the Endeavour rode at anchor for six days while Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander botanised the surrounding hills. Few historic walks in this country put you so completely in the place where so much else began.

name: Manutūkē — Carved Churches of the East Coast
short: A cluster of elaborately carved Māori churches — blending Christian faith with traditional carving arts.
commentary: The East Coast has the finest concentration of carved Māori churches in the country. Manutūkē's churches show the creative fusion of Christianity and Māori art that Apirana Ngata championed.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Manutūkē — Carved Churches of the East Coast as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A cluster of elaborately carved Māori churches — blending Christian faith with traditional carving arts. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Motu River White Water Rafting
short: Raft the remote Motu River through Grade 4 rapids and untouched wilderness gorges.
commentary: The Motu River is one of the last unmodified river systems in the North Island, offering multi-day rafting through remote gorge country with Grade 3-4 rapids. The river flows through native bush with no road access, making it a true wilderness experience. Trips typically run over 2-4 days.

reviews.summary: The Motu River White Water Rafting adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Raft the remote Motu River through Grade 4 rapids and untouched wilderness gorges. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You are about to embark on one of the North Island's great wilderness rafting journeys, the Motu River. This is one of the last unmodified river systems in the region, flowing through deep gorges of native bush with no roads, no bridges, and no cellphone reception for the entire journey. Over the next few days, you will navigate Grade 3 and 4 rapids with names that hint at what is ahead, and between the white water, you will drift through pools of astonishing clarity where the bush comes right down to the water's edge. The Motu was the subject of one of New Zealand's most famous conservation battles in the 1980s, when it was saved from a proposed hydro dam. Camp each night on riverside terraces, cook over a fire, and fall asleep to the sound of morepork calling from the bush. This is not a theme park adventure, it is the real thing, genuinely remote and utterly unforgettable.

name: Motu Trails – Great Ride
short: Cycle the Motu Trails, linking Opotiki to the East Cape through native bush, coastal cliffs, and remote beaches.
commentary: The Motu Trails are a NZ Cycle Trail Great Ride featuring three distinct sections: the Dunes Trail along the coast, the Motu Road Trail through remote bush, and the Pakihi Track through native forest. The trails connect Opotiki with the East Cape and range from easy coastal riding to challenging backcountry.

reviews.summary: The Motu Trails – Great Ride route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cycle the Motu Trails, linking Opotiki to the East Cape through native bush, coastal cliffs, and remote beaches. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are riding the Motu Trails, one of the more remote and rewarding Great Rides in the New Zealand Cycle Trail network. The route links three distinct sections, each with its own character. The Dunes Trail follows the coast from Opotiki along wild, wind-swept beaches and through coastal forest — easy riding with the sound of surf alongside. The Motu Road Trail heads inland along a remote gravel road through the Motu River valley, one of the most pristine river systems in the North Island. The Pakihi Track is the challenging section — native bush singletrack with climbs and descents through dense podocarp forest. The Motu River itself is famous for whitewater rafting, and the surrounding bush is home to whio — blue duck — one of New Zealand's rarest birds. This ride is for those who want to experience a genuinely wild part of New Zealand that few tourists ever reach. Come prepared with food and tools — services are scarce out here.

name: Maraehako Bay & Te Araroa Pōhutukawa
short: The largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree in the country — over 600 years old, with a 40m crown.
commentary: Te Waha-o-Rerekohu in Te Araroa schoolground is reputed to be the largest pōhutukawa in NZ.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Maraehako Bay & Te Araroa Pōhutukawa as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree in the country — over 600 years old, with a 40m crown. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You have arrived in the small East Cape village of Te Araroa, and behind the local school stands a tree that, by most reasonable measures, is the largest and oldest pōhutukawa in New Zealand. It is called Te Waha-o-Rerekohu, the mouth of Rerekohu, named for an ancestor of Ngāti Porou. The tree is reputed to be more than six hundred years old. Its trunk has divided into many great limbs that lean out and back to the ground over a crown forty metres across. In summer when it flowers, the whole village glows red beneath it. There is no fence, no entry fee, no gift shop. You simply walk across the schoolground, when school is not in session, and stand under the canopy. Te Araroa itself sits in a long curved bay on the wild northeast coast. From here it is another twenty kilometres on a winding sealed road to East Cape Lighthouse, the easternmost point of mainland New Zealand and one of the first places in the world to see the new day's sun. The village is also the start of the Te Araroa Trail, the country's longest hiking route. Spend a few minutes in the shade of the tree before you go.

name: Rere Rockslide
short: A 60m natural rockslide on the Wharekopae River where you can ride a bodyboard or inner tube down a smooth waterfall.
commentary: The Rere Rockslide on the Wharekopae River near Gisborne is a 60m sloping rock face used as a natural water slide. Bring an inflatable, lilo or bodyboard.

reviews.summary: Rere Rockslide draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 60m natural rockslide on the Wharekopae River where you can ride a bodyboard or inner tube down a smooth waterfall. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Rere Rockslide, and if you brought any kind of inflatable mattress, bodyboard or air filled tube with you, today is your day. The Wharekopae River runs west of Gisborne through gentle farm country, and at one particular point it slides over a sixty metre long sheet of sloping rock that is, by some unlikely accident of geology, exactly the right angle and exactly the right smoothness to be one of the longest natural waterslides in the country. Walk to the top, sit on whatever you brought to ride, and let the water carry you down. The pool at the bottom is shallow but deep enough to take the landing. People have been doing this for generations. It is free. It is open. There are no staff, no lifeguards, no entry charges, and only a discreet sign reminding you to use common sense. On a hot summer day local families turn it into a barbecue afternoon. Just downstream the same river drops over the much taller Rere Falls, a wide twenty metre waterfall you can walk across the front of. Make a day of it. Bring towels.

name: Te Urewera (Lake Waikaremoana)
short: Lake Waikaremoana — a great deep-bush lake, a Great Walk, and the heart of Te Urewera, which has its own legal personhood.
commentary: Te Urewera was vested as a legal person in 2014 under the Tūhoe settlement, the first land in the world to receive such a status. Lake Waikaremoana sits in its heart.

reviews.summary: Te Urewera (Lake Waikaremoana) draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Lake Waikaremoana — a great deep-bush lake, a Great Walk, and the heart of Te Urewera, which has its own legal personhood. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have come to Te Urewera, the great mountain forest in the heart of the North Island. Te Urewera is the homeland of Tūhoe, who are sometimes called the Children of the Mist, and in twenty fourteen this entire forested landscape became the first land in the world to be granted legal personhood. Te Urewera is no longer owned by the Crown. It is no longer a national park in the conventional sense. It is, in law, itself, with rights of its own, jointly cared for by Tūhoe and the Crown. At its heart sits Lake Waikaremoana. The lake was formed when a giant landslide blocked a river around two thousand years ago, and the dark drowned forests are still preserved beneath its surface. The Lake Waikaremoana Track, one of New Zealand's Great Walks, runs forty six kilometres around the western shore over three to four days. There are also short walks from the visitor centre at Aniwaniwa, including the spectacular Waikaremoana Falls track. The road in is long and remote. The forest is deep. The mist sits low. Move quietly through this place, and remember it has its own name and its own story.

name: Rere Rockslide
short: Slide 60 metres down a natural rock waterslide in the Gisborne backcountry.
commentary: Rere Rockslide is a 60-metre natural rock waterslide formed by smooth, angled rock with a thin sheet of water flowing over it. Located 50 km from Gisborne, it is free to use. Locals ride it on boogie boards, bodyboards, or anything that slides. The nearby Rere Falls is also worth a visit.

reviews.summary: The Rere Rockslide adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Slide 60 metres down a natural rock waterslide in the Gisborne backcountry. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Rere Rockslide, one of New Zealand's most joyful free adventures and a local secret that deserves to be better known. Before you is a 60-metre stretch of smooth rock angled at a perfect gradient, with a thin sheet of water flowing over it to create a completely natural waterslide. Grab a boogie board, a pool noodle, an old surfboard, or honestly, just lie on your back and let the water carry you down. The ride takes about 10 seconds and ends in a deep pool at the bottom. There is no entrance fee, no queue, and no rules beyond common sense. Locals have been sliding down this rock for generations. The surface is remarkably smooth, worn by centuries of flowing water, and the water depth varies with recent rainfall, more rain means a faster ride. Rere Falls, a beautiful 5-metre cascade, is just a few minutes up the road.

name: Rere Falls and Rockslide Road Trip
short: Drive inland from Gisborne to Rere Falls and the natural 60-metre rockslide — bring a boogie board.
commentary: Rere Falls is a 5-metre-wide waterfall about 50 km northwest of Gisborne. Nearby, the Rere Rockslide is a 60-metre natural rock water slide where locals ride boogie boards down a smooth rock face into a pool. The drive through the Wharekopae Valley follows a scenic back road through farmland.

reviews.summary: The Rere Falls and Rockslide Road Trip route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive inland from Gisborne to Rere Falls and the natural 60-metre rockslide — bring a boogie board. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving inland from Gisborne through the Wharekopae Valley on a back road that delivers two of the Tairawhiti region's best-kept secrets. First, Rere Falls — a broad, photogenic waterfall where the Wharekopae River drops over a wide rock ledge surrounded by native bush. It is beautiful but what comes next is the main event. A short drive further, the Rere Rockslide is a 60-metre natural rock water slide where a thin sheet of water flows over smooth volcanic rock into a deep pool below. Grab a boogie board — locals leave spares at the site — climb to the top, lie down, and let the water carry you down at surprising speed. It is an absolute blast and completely free. The rock has been worn glass-smooth by millennia of flowing water. The slide works best after rain when the water flow is stronger. This is the kind of place that does not make it into most guidebooks but absolutely should.

name: Rongopai Painted Meeting House
short: A uniquely painted meeting house — floral murals instead of carvings, created by followers of Te Kooti in 1887.
commentary: Rongopai's interior is covered in painted figurative murals — portraits, floral designs, and religious imagery. A radical departure from carving tradition and a unique fusion of Māori and European styles. Category I historic place.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Rongopai Painted Meeting House as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A uniquely painted meeting house — floral murals instead of carvings, created by followers of Te Kooti in 1887. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Ruatōria — Ngāti Porou Heartland
short: Deep in Ngāti Porou territory where Māori culture is the dominant way of life.
commentary: Here, te reo Māori is the everyday language and marae are the centres of community life. For visitors willing to travel the remote East Cape road, Ruatōria offers an authentic window into Māori New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ruatōria — Ngāti Porou Heartland as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Deep in Ngāti Porou territory where Māori culture is the dominant way of life. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Tatapouri Stingray Encounter
short: Wade out at low tide to feed wild short-tail stingrays — a guided experience run by local Ngāi Tāmanuhiri whānau.
commentary: Operated by Dive Tatapouri, a small family business north of Gisborne. The stingrays are wild and free to leave; they have learned to come for fish during the daily encounter.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Tatapouri Stingray Encounter as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Wade out at low tide to feed wild short-tail stingrays — a guided experience run by local Ngāi Tāmanuhiri whānau. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are about to wade into the sea at Tatapouri, a few kilometres north of Gisborne, with a wetsuit on and a hand-held bait bag, and a wild stingray is going to come and eat from your hand. The stingrays here are not captive. They live in the open ocean and choose, every day, whether or not to swim into the shallows for the daily encounter. Most days they choose to come. Local guides take small groups to a shallow reef just offshore at low tide, where short-tail and eagle stingrays glide silently in to investigate. The bigger short-tails can be over two metres across the wing, and yet they move with such gentleness that they barely disturb the water around your knees. Your guide will show you how to hold a piece of bait between your thumb and finger so the ray takes it cleanly with its mouth on the underside of its body. The whole experience is run by local whānau and is grounded in tikanga, with a karakia at the beginning and end of each session. You will leave with cold feet, a wet wetsuit, and a story that will, frankly, last for life.

name: Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae
short: One of the largest and most elaborately carved meeting houses in NZ — at the foot of Kaiti Hill.
commentary: Te Poho-o-Rawiri features magnificent traditional carvings and tukutuku panels. Its scale and artistry make it one of the finest examples of Māori architectural craftsmanship.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Poho-o-Rawiri Marae as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of the largest and most elaborately carved meeting houses in NZ — at the foot of Kaiti Hill. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Tītīrangi / Kaiti Hill Lookout, Gisborne
short: The hill above Gisborne where Captain Cook first sighted the New Zealand mainland in 1769 — and where Ngāti Oneone built one of their great pā.
commentary: Tītīrangi is sacred to Ngāti Oneone. Beneath the hill lies the Cook landing site at Puhi Kai Iti. Today the summit is a public reserve with sweeping views across Poverty Bay.

reviews.summary: Tītīrangi / Kaiti Hill Lookout, Gisborne draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The hill above Gisborne where Captain Cook first sighted the New Zealand mainland in 1769 — and where Ngāti Oneone built one of their great pā. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have climbed up Tītīrangi, the hill the Pākehā maps call Kaiti Hill, and you are standing on the highest ground above Gisborne. From here you can see almost all of Poverty Bay, from the river mouth at your feet out to Young Nicks Head on the southern arm of the bay. It was from this very piece of land, in October seventeen sixty nine, that the Endeavour was first sighted by people on shore, and from the deck of the Endeavour, that the boy Nicholas Young first sighted the New Zealand mainland and earned the headland its English name. The hill itself is sacred to Ngāti Oneone, the local hapū, and was once the site of a great fortified pā. The terraces are still visible on the slopes. The summit lookout has been thoughtfully redeveloped in recent years with carved pou, interpretation panels, and a James Cook memorial that, importantly, is balanced now by signs that tell the harder story of the first contact, in which several local Māori were shot by the Endeavour crew. Stand here for a few minutes. Two stories meet on this hill, and both belong here.

name: Tolaga Bay / Ūawa — Takitimu Waka Landing
short: A beautiful East Coast bay with NZ's longest wharf — and a landing site of the Takitimu waka.
commentary: Ūawa is a landing site of the Takitimu waka. The 660m concrete wharf is NZ's longest. Behind the bay, the Cooks Cove Walkway passes through a natural rock arch.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Tolaga Bay / Ūawa — Takitimu Waka Landing as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A beautiful East Coast bay with NZ's longest wharf — and a landing site of the Takitimu waka. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waiapu Valley — Te Reo Stronghold
short: One of the last places in NZ where te reo Māori is the everyday language — the heartland of Ngāti Porou.
commentary: The valley has more marae per capita than almost anywhere else. The area produced Sir Apirana Ngata, who championed Māori language and culture. Travelling through is like entering a different New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Waiapu Valley — Te Reo Stronghold as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of the last places in NZ where te reo Māori is the everyday language — the heartland of Ngāti Porou. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Waioeka Gorge Scenic Drive SH2
short: Drive through the deep, bush-clad Waioeka Gorge connecting the Bay of Plenty to the Gisborne coast.
commentary: State Highway 2 through the Waioeka Gorge links Opotiki with Gisborne via a 100 km winding road through dense native bush. The gorge section passes through the Waioeka Gorge Scenic Reserve with limited cellphone coverage and few passing opportunities. It is one of the most remote highway sections in the North Island.

reviews.summary: The Waioeka Gorge Scenic Drive SH2 route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive through the deep, bush-clad Waioeka Gorge connecting the Bay of Plenty to the Gisborne coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are entering the Waioeka Gorge on State Highway 2, and for the next 100 kilometres the outside world effectively disappears. This winding road links Opotiki in the Bay of Plenty with the Gisborne coast, cutting through one of the most densely forested and remote gorges in the North Island. Native bush presses in from both sides — rimu, tawa, and tree ferns creating a green tunnel overhead. The road follows the Waioeka River, crossing it multiple times on narrow bridges. There is no cellphone coverage for much of the route, few places to stop, and almost no traffic. The Waioeka Gorge Scenic Reserve protects the forest, and the bush here is as pristine as you will find anywhere accessible by road. Drive with your headlights on, take the corners carefully, and enjoy the profound sense of isolation. When you finally emerge onto the open, sunlit farmland of the Gisborne coast, the contrast is startling — like stepping from a forest cave into daylight.

name: Whangara — Home of the Whale Rider
short: The village that inspired the novel and film Whale Rider — where the ancestor Paikea rode a whale to shore.
commentary: Whangara is the ancestral home of Ngāti Konohi and the setting for Witi Ihimaera's novel 'The Whale Rider' and the acclaimed 2002 film. In the founding tradition, the ancestor Paikea rode a whale to the shore here from Hawaiki. The village sits on a beautiful stretch of coast north of Gisborne and retains a strong Māori community.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Whangara — Home of the Whale Rider as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The village that inspired the novel and film Whale Rider — where the ancestor Paikea rode a whale to shore. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Te Waha-o-Rerekohu
short: The largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree in the world — 600+ years old, 37-metre branch span — in the grounds of Te Araroa Area School.
commentary: In the grounds of Te Araroa Area School stands Te Waha-o-Rerekohu, 'the mouth of Rerekohu' — named for a rangatira of this rohe whose kūmara pātaka (food store) tradition places at the base of the tree. Dendrochronology dates its germination to around the year 1421, making it around 600 years old, give or take 200. The branch span was measured at over 37 metres in 1950. It's the largest and oldest known pōhutukawa on earth. Publicly accessible during reasonable hours; treat the tree with the respect of a taonga, which it is.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Waha-o-Rerekohu as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The largest and oldest pōhutukawa tree in the world — 600+ years old, 37-metre branch span — in the grounds of Te Araroa Area School. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Tolaga Bay Wharf / Ūawa
short: 660 metres of concrete reaching into the Pacific — the longest wharf in the southern hemisphere, saved from the sea by the town that built it.
commentary: Designed by marine engineer Cyrus Williams to solve Tolaga Bay's open-roadstead problem, construction began in 1926 and finished in 1929. Storms damaged the works during construction and the harbour board had to borrow another £20,000 to finish. At its peak in 1936, 133 ships a year called at the wharf. The last sailing was the coastal steamer Kopara in 1966. From the 1990s the Tolaga Bay Save the Wharf Charitable Trust has been restoring it section by section. It's now Heritage NZ Category 1, and you can walk the full 660 metres. Just up the road is Cooks Cove — where the Endeavour sheltered 23–29 October 1769 and Banks and Solander did much of their first NZ botanical collecting.

reviews.summary: Tolaga Bay Wharf / Ūawa draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. 660 metres of concrete reaching into the Pacific — the longest wharf in the southern hemisphere, saved from the sea by the town that built it. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Cape Egmont Lighthouse
short: A cast-iron 1865 lighthouse on the westernmost tip of Taranaki — moved twice, shipped from London in a thousand pieces.
commentary: The Cape Egmont light was built in London in 1864, shipped to Wellington in 1865 in pre-cast iron plates, and first erected on Mana Island. It was dismantled in 1877 and re-erected at Cape Egmont, where it has stood ever since (electrified 1951, automated 1986). The tower itself is 20 m tall. On a clear day the light looks straight across at the summit of Mount Taranaki. The beach below is black iron-sand and usually deserted.

reviews.summary: Cape Egmont Lighthouse draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A cast-iron 1865 lighthouse on the westernmost tip of Taranaki — moved twice, shipped from London in a thousand pieces. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Forgotten World Highway (SH43)
short: The 155 km backroad between Stratford and Taumarunui — one of the quietest, weirdest drives in the country.
commentary: SH43, the Forgotten World Highway, runs 155 km between Stratford (Taranaki) and Taumarunui (Ruapehu). It takes 4 hours. There's one town, four saddles, one single-lane tunnel (the 1936 Moki Tunnel), and about 12 km of unsealed road in the middle. The town is Whangamomona, which in 1989 responded to being moved from Taranaki into the Manawatū-Whanganui region by declaring itself an independent republic. It has a biennial Republic Day (January odd years), a Hotel that serves excellent food, and elected a poodle as president in 2003. Fill your tank before you leave.

reviews.summary: The Forgotten World Highway (SH43) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. The 155 km backroad between Stratford and Taumarunui — one of the quietest, weirdest drives in the country. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

name: Mount Taranaki / Egmont National Park
short: A 2,518 m dormant stratovolcano — an almost-perfect cone, sacred to eight iwi, and a mountain that kills climbers every year.
commentary: Taranaki is a 2,518 m stratovolcano — dormant, not extinct. Its last eruption was in 1854. Traditions say Taranaki once stood with Tongariro and Ruapehu on the volcanic plateau but was driven west after a fight over Pihanga and now stands alone. The mountain is sacred to eight Taranaki iwi, and was granted legal personhood under New Zealand law in 2017. Pouakai Circuit, Pouakai Tarns (for the classic reflection shot), and the summit climb are the three main hikes. The climb is deceptively dangerous — sudden cloud, ice above 1,800 m, average of 2 deaths a year. Never attempt without guide or serious preparation.

reviews.summary: Mount Taranaki / Egmont National Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 2,518 m dormant stratovolcano — an almost-perfect cone, sacred to eight iwi, and a mountain that kills climbers every year. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: New Plymouth Coastal Walkway
short: A 13 km paved walk/cycle path along the full city waterfront — past the Len Lye Wind Wand, Puke Ariki, and two black-sand beaches.
commentary: The New Plymouth Coastal Walkway runs 13 km from Port Taranaki in the west to Bell Block Beach in the east, hugging the coast the whole way. Walk or cycle any section. Highlights: the 48-metre Len Lye Wind Wand (artist's kinetic sculpture, 2001); Puke Ariki museum and library; the Govett-Brewster / Len Lye Centre — the only institution dedicated to a single contemporary artist in the country; and Te Rewa Rewa Bridge. On a clear day, the mountain rises behind you the whole walk.

reviews.summary: New Plymouth Coastal Walkway draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 13 km paved walk/cycle path along the full city waterfront — past the Len Lye Wind Wand, Puke Ariki, and two black-sand beaches. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Ōākura
short: A small artist-and-surfer village 15 minutes west of New Plymouth — black-sand beach, consistent swell, good coffee.
commentary: Ōākura is a beach village on the Surf Highway 45, southwest of New Plymouth. The black iron-sand beach is one of the best surf breaks on the Taranaki coast, reliable year-round. The village itself has a cluster of cafés, a surf shop, and the Taranaki Pioneer Village (not in Ōākura itself but nearby in Stratford — a colonial theme park). Fitzroy, on the other side of New Plymouth, is the town-beach alternative; Ōākura is the surf-town version. Summer lifeguards only.

reviews.summary: Ōākura draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A small artist-and-surfer village 15 minutes west of New Plymouth — black-sand beach, consistent swell, good coffee. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Parihaka
short: The village where Māori leaders Te Whiti and Tohu pioneered non-violent resistance in the 1870s — decades before Gandhi.
commentary: Parihaka was founded in the 1860s as a refuge for Māori dispossessed by the Taranaki Wars. Under spiritual leaders Te Whiti o Rongomai and Tohu Kākahi it became the largest Māori settlement in the country — and the first place in the world to systematically practise organised non-violent resistance: ploughing confiscated land, obstructing surveyors, singing instead of fighting. On 5 November 1881 a force of 1,589 armed constabulary invaded. Te Whiti and Tohu were imprisoned without trial. The white feather, symbol of peace, is still worn at Parihaka today. Visit with reverence. A formal Crown apology was delivered in 2017.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Parihaka as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The village where Māori leaders Te Whiti and Tohu pioneered non-violent resistance in the 1870s — decades before Gandhi. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Pouākai Tarns
short: Small alpine tarns that, on a windless dawn, give the most photographed view of Mount Taranaki in the country.
commentary: The Pouākai Tarns are small alpine pools on the Pouākai Range, on the northern side of Mount Taranaki. On a calm, clear dawn the tarns reflect the full cone of the mountain — the image that has launched a thousand Taranaki Instagram posts. Access is a steep 3–4 hour hike from Māngōrei Road; most people stay overnight at Pouākai Hut (DOC, book ahead) to catch the dawn. Track is a mix of clay, tree roots and boardwalk; the last section to the tarns is exposed alpine. Weather changes fast.

reviews.summary: Pouākai Tarns draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Small alpine tarns that, on a windless dawn, give the most photographed view of Mount Taranaki in the country. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Bell Block Stockade Site

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Bell Block Stockade Site — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are in Bell Block, today a busy suburb northeast of New Plymouth, but in the eighteen sixties this flat land was a defended line of stockades and blockhouses. Settler families whose farms had been burnt in the Taranaki Wars crowded into redoubts here for protection, unable to work their fields safely. The Bell Block stockade was one of a string of such posts built along the coast to hold open the road to New Plymouth. Life inside was cramped and anxious. Farmers ventured out by day to tend to what remained of their crops, knowing an ambush might be waiting in the manuka. Māori attacks and raids continued until the troops of General Cameron marched further south and the confiscation legislation emptied much of the Taranaki interior. The stockade was dismantled when the wars ended. Little above ground survives, but the suburb's name carries the memory. As you walk or drive through Bell Block, remember that the suburb was born of fear, displacement, and the long violence of land confiscation.

name: Mount Taranaki as Ancestor

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Mount Taranaki as Ancestor as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are in the presence of Taranaki Maunga, the mountain that stands almost perfectly conical at the centre of this province. For the iwi of Taranaki, the maunga is not a feature of landscape but an ancestor. The story tells that Taranaki once stood beside Tongariro, Ruapehu, and Ngāuruhoe in the central plateau, but he loved Pihanga and fought Tongariro over her. Defeated, he fled westward, gouging the course of the Whanganui River in his grief, and finally stopped here to face the sea alone. The tears he weeps feed the rivers of the province. In two thousand and fourteen, Taranaki iwi reached a Treaty redress that ultimately granted legal personhood to the mountain, recognising it as Te Kāhui Tupua with all the rights of a person. You cannot own an ancestor. Climb the maunga with respect, do not stand on the summit peak which is the tapu head, and leave nothing behind. This mountain watches over you even as you look up to it.

name: Ōākura Redoubt and Village

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Ōākura Redoubt and Village — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Ōākura, a pretty seaside village south of New Plymouth. In the eighteen sixties the settlement was the site of a colonial redoubt protecting the coast road and the farms of returning settlers. A notorious ambush occurred near here at Wairau in eighteen sixty three when Captain Tragett and eight other soldiers were killed by a war party, an event that further inflamed the Second Taranaki War. Ōākura's black sand beach is one of the most photographed in the province, with Mount Taranaki rising behind on a clear day. The village today is a mix of surf shops, galleries, and holiday houses, but its military origins are still visible in the layout of old farm roads and in interpretive signs near the beach. As you stand with the waves at your back, picture a timber redoubt on the headland, sentries looking into the bush, and a steep line of dairy country stretching north to New Plymouth. The beach is a reminder of how quickly a coastline can shift from battlefield to holiday resort.

name: Parihaka

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Parihaka as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are approaching Parihaka, a small settlement in the shadow of Mount Taranaki that carries one of the most important stories in Aotearoa's history. In the eighteen seventies, under the leadership of the prophets Te Whiti o Rongomai and Tohu Kākahi, Parihaka grew into a prosperous village of hundreds, a place of peaceful resistance to the theft of confiscated Taranaki land. When government surveyors arrived to divide up the fields, Parihaka's ploughmen quietly ploughed the surveyors' lines back in. They were arrested and sent south to prison, yet more ploughmen came forward. On the fifth of November eighteen eighty one, over sixteen hundred armed soldiers marched on Parihaka. They found children sitting across the road singing and offering food. The troops destroyed crops and homes, arrested Te Whiti and Tohu, and scattered the people. Yet the village rebuilt. Today Parihaka is a living community and a symbol of peaceful resistance honoured worldwide. A formal Crown apology was made in two thousand and seventeen. Do not enter without invitation. Park on the road and remember a courage that the musket could not break.

name: Rewa Rewa Bridge and Coastal Walkway

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Rewa Rewa Bridge and Coastal Walkway — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are on the Coastal Walkway at the Rewa Rewa Bridge, an elegant white curved footbridge that arcs across the Waiwhakaiho River. The shape of the bridge, opened in two thousand and ten, echoes a whale's jawbone, acknowledging the Ngāti Tawhirikura people who hold mana whenua here and the whales once hunted off this coast. The bridge sits near the old Rewa Rewa urupā, an important burial ground, and the design deliberately directs foot traffic past the cemetery with respect. The Coastal Walkway itself stretches more than thirteen kilometres along the New Plymouth shore, linking city and suburbs and offering framed views of Mount Taranaki on a clear day. It has become one of New Zealand's most admired public works, winning international design awards. As you walk or cycle here, look up to the maunga, then down at the surf and rocks, and remember that even a footpath can be a way of acknowledging ancestry and repairing the bonds between city and tangata whenua.

name: Sentry Hill (Te Morere)

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Sentry Hill (Te Morere) — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Sentry Hill, known in te reo as Te Morere, the site of a redoubt manned by colonial forces during the Second Taranaki War. On the thirtieth of April eighteen sixty four, a large force of Pai Mārire warriors attacked the redoubt believing that their faith would turn aside the bullets. Led by prophets of the new religion, they walked upright into the British rifle fire calling the karakia Hapa, meaning pass over. Between thirty and fifty warriors were killed in minutes. The devastating defeat shook the Pai Mārire movement in Taranaki, though it continued elsewhere. A small memorial and interpretive panels tell the story today. Sentry Hill stands as a deeply tragic site, where courage, belief, and colonial firepower met with brutal consequences. Stand at the redoubt outline, face inland toward Mount Taranaki, and reflect on the desperation of land defenders asked to believe that prayer alone could shield them. The war that followed continued for years, and the confiscations that came after reshaped this province.

name: Te Koru Pā

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Koru Pā as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are at Te Koru Pā, an exceptionally well preserved pre-European Māori fortification set on a ridge above the Oakura River south of New Plymouth. From the walking track you can see the deep terraces, ditches, and palisade emplacements cut into the hillside by generations of labour. The pā was a stronghold of Ngā Māhanga a Tairi, a hapū of Taranaki iwi. Its defensive earthworks are among the finest still visible anywhere in the North Island. Stone walls of tabular andesite line some of the terraces, unusual in a country where most pā were built in volcanic soil with timber palisades. Te Koru was abandoned during the wars of the eighteen thirties when Taranaki people retreated from Te Rauparaha's raids, later to return after peace returned in the eighteen forties. The bush has reclaimed much of the site, and walking its paths today is a quiet, moving experience. Stay on the track, do not climb on the terraces, and listen. This is a place where you can stand inside the walls of a civilisation and feel the shape of a very old plan for living safely and well.

name: Tohu and Te Whiti Memorial at Parihaka

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Tohu and Te Whiti Memorial at Parihaka as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You are near the memorial to Te Whiti o Rongomai and Tohu Kākahi, the two prophets who led Parihaka. Together they preached a message of peaceful resistance grounded in Te Tiriti o Waitangi and in the older authority of the land itself. Their symbol, the raukura, three white feathers, represented peace, courage, and the unity of people. After the invasion of November eighteen eighty one, both men were taken without trial and held for years in the South Island, paraded in humiliation through colonial towns. Yet they returned, and Parihaka rebuilt. Te Whiti died in nineteen oh seven and Tohu the same year. Their message spread far beyond Aotearoa, influencing later non-violent movements around the world, including leaders who looked to Parihaka as an early example of civil disobedience. On the eighteenth of each month, many people in Parihaka still wear the raukura. Pause quietly here and take in what peaceful resistance costs, and what it builds in return.

name: Waireka Battle Site

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waireka Battle Site — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at the site of the Battle of Waireka, fought on the twenty eighth of March eighteen sixty on the coast just south of New Plymouth. It was one of the opening engagements of the First Taranaki War, triggered by the government's attempt to force through a disputed land sale at Waitara. Settler militia and British regulars attacked a Māori position held mostly by Ngāti Ruanui and Taranaki iwi. The fighting was confused and bloody, night fell before it was decided, and a naval detachment under Captain Peter Cracroft eventually stormed the pā. The battle was claimed as a colonial victory, but it hardened Māori resistance and convinced many North Island tribes that the Crown could not be trusted. For years afterwards, settler families fled inland and the Taranaki countryside emptied. Today the battle site is quiet farmland near the sea. A modest monument marks the engagement. Stand here and consider that this unremarkable slope set in motion a long war whose land confiscations still shape Taranaki's Treaty claims in our own century.

name: Waitara and the Disputed Purchase

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waitara and the Disputed Purchase — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You are at Waitara, at the mouth of the Waitara River, the flashpoint of the First Taranaki War. In eighteen fifty nine the Crown agreed to buy a small block of land here from Te Teira, ignoring the objections of Wiremu Kīngi Te Rangitāke, the chief of the hapū who shared rights to the block. Wiremu Kīngi and his people built a pā on the disputed ground and refused to allow surveyors on to the land. In March eighteen sixty, British troops attacked. That small disagreement over one block ignited more than a decade of war across Taranaki, reshaping the province through military campaigns and confiscation of more than a million acres. The disputed land was eventually returned to its owners a hundred and fifty seven years later, in twenty seventeen, when the Waitara Lands Bill passed parliament. Today Waitara is a small town and river port. Stand on the riverbank and consider how one bureaucratic signature ignited a war, and how long it took for the balance to be even partially restored.

name: Around the Mountain Circuit
short: Circle Mount Taranaki on a 3-5 day tramp through rainforest, lava fields, and subalpine gardens.
commentary: The Around the Mountain Circuit is a 46 km loop circumnavigating Mount Taranaki through Egmont National Park. The route passes through diverse vegetation zones from lowland rainforest to subalpine desert. Multiple DOC huts provide accommodation. The circuit offers ever-changing perspectives of the mountain.

reviews.summary: The Around the Mountain Circuit route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Circle Mount Taranaki on a 3-5 day tramp through rainforest, lava fields, and subalpine gardens. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are embarking on the Around the Mountain Circuit, a 46-kilometre loop that circumnavigates Mount Taranaki through every possible landscape the mountain offers. Over three to five days, you pass through dripping lowland rainforest, twisted goblin forest, alpine herb fields, barren lava flows, and dramatic river gorges carved by lahars. The mountain reveals a different face from every angle — seen from the north it is symmetrical perfection, from the east the Fanthams Peak subsidiary cone adds complexity, and from the south the older, eroded Pouakai Range provides contrast. This national park receives some of the highest rainfall in New Zealand — up to 8,000 millimetres annually on the upper slopes — so expect wet conditions and be prepared. The bush is extraordinarily lush as a result, with rata, kamahi, and rimu draped in mosses and epiphytes. DOC huts are spaced a day's walk apart. Take your time and absorb the remarkable ecological diversity — you will walk through more vegetation zones in three days than most trampers see in a week.

name: Cape Egmont — Te Kape o Taranaki
short: The westernmost point of Taranaki — a significant coastal landmark in tribal navigation traditions.
commentary: Cape Egmont was a major navigation reference point for Māori travelling along the coast. The lighthouse marks the point, but the cape's significance predates European arrival by centuries.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Cape Egmont — Te Kape o Taranaki as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The westernmost point of Taranaki — a significant coastal landmark in tribal navigation traditions. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Dawson Falls & the Mountain House
short: An 18-metre waterfall on the southern flank of Mt Taranaki, reached by a ten-minute walk from the Dawson Falls car park.
commentary: Dawson Falls is an 18-m waterfall on the Kāpuni Stream on the southern side of Mt Taranaki. The historic 1896 Dawson Falls Mountain Lodge sits nearby at 900 m elevation.

reviews.summary: Dawson Falls & the Mountain House draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. An 18-metre waterfall on the southern flank of Mt Taranaki, reached by a ten-minute walk from the Dawson Falls car park. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Dawson Falls, on the southern side of Mount Taranaki at around nine hundred metres elevation. The falls themselves are an eighteen metre drop on the Kāpuni Stream, where the water vaults in a single clean column over a cliff of hardened lava and plunges into a moss ringed pool below. A sealed track from the car park winds through an enchanted stretch of goblin forest, old twisted kāmahi trees shrouded in hanging moss, to the viewing platform. The walk is around ten minutes each way and suits most abilities. Next to the car park stands the Dawson Falls Mountain Lodge, a Swiss chalet style building dating to eighteen ninety six that is one of the oldest mountain lodges still operating in New Zealand. The lodge sits at the trailhead for several longer walks, including the Wilkies Pools Loop and the more serious Fantham's Peak climb. The falls are named for Thomas Dawson, a New Plymouth merchant who cut the first track to them in the eighteen eighties. The Māori name for the falls is Te Rere o Noke, the leaping of Noke, honouring the legendary warrior who hid behind the waterfall to escape pursuers. Both names are used today.

name: Dawson Falls Waterfall Walk
short: Walk through goblin forest to the 18-metre Dawson Falls on Mount Taranaki's south side.
commentary: Dawson Falls is an 18-metre waterfall on the southern slopes of Mount Taranaki, reached by a 20-minute walk through mossy goblin forest. The track starts from the Dawson Falls Visitor Centre and passes through some of the most atmospheric forest on the mountain.

reviews.summary: The Dawson Falls Waterfall Walk adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Walk through goblin forest to the 18-metre Dawson Falls on Mount Taranaki's south side. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Dawson Falls Visitor Centre on the southern slopes of Mount Taranaki, where a short walk through enchanting goblin forest leads to one of the mountain's most photogenic waterfalls. The track winds through a world of moss-draped trees, their branches twisted into fantastical shapes by the mountain winds and dripping with moisture. Every surface is covered in green, creating an atmosphere that feels ancient and slightly magical. After about 20 minutes of easy walking, you will hear the falls before you see them, an 18-metre cascade of water plunging over volcanic rock into a bush-fringed pool. The falls are named after Thomas Dawson, who explored this area in the 1880s and was one of the first Europeans to climb the mountain. The goblin forest effect is caused by constant cloud and mist at this elevation, creating perfect growing conditions for mosses and epiphytes that smother every branch.

name: Manganui Ski Area (Mount Taranaki)
short: Ski the intimate club field on Mount Taranaki with views to the coast.
commentary: Manganui Ski Area is a club-operated ski field on the eastern face of Mount Taranaki. It offers a small rope tow and T-bar serving intermediate to advanced terrain. The field has a loyal local following and is one of the most affordable skiing options in the North Island.

reviews.summary: The Manganui Ski Area (Mount Taranaki) adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ski the intimate club field on Mount Taranaki with views to the coast. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Manganui Ski Area on the eastern slopes of Mount Taranaki, one of New Zealand's last true club ski fields and a world away from the commercial resorts. A rope tow and T-bar serve a compact but rewarding area of volcanic slopes that offer genuine alpine skiing at a fraction of the usual cost. The terrain suits intermediate to advanced skiers and snowboarders, with natural features, chutes, and on a powder day, some of the best skiing in the North Island. What makes Manganui special is the atmosphere, this is a community-run field where everyone mucks in, the coffee is from a thermos, and the vibe is welcoming. The views are extraordinary, on a clear day you can see from the volcanic slopes all the way to the Tasman Sea coastline. Snow conditions depend entirely on the weather patterns hitting Taranaki, so check the field report before making the drive. When it is on, it is genuinely on.

name: Taranaki Iwi — Eight Tribes of the Mountain
short: Eight iwi share Mount Taranaki as their ancestor — each distinct but united by the mountain.
commentary: Te Ātiawa, Taranaki, Ngāti Ruanui, Ngāruahine, Ngāti Mutunga, Ngāti Tama, Ngāti Maru, and Ngā Rauru Kītahi all share 'Ko Taranaki te maunga'.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Taranaki Iwi — Eight Tribes of the Mountain as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Eight iwi share Mount Taranaki as their ancestor — each distinct but united by the mountain. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Forgotten World Adventures Rail Cart
short: Ride self-drive rail carts through 20 tunnels on the abandoned Forgotten World railway.
commentary: Forgotten World Adventures operates self-drive rail carts on the abandoned railway between Whangamomona and Stratford. The route passes through 20 tunnels, including the 1.6 km Moki Tunnel, across bridges, and through remote farmland that time has largely forgotten.

reviews.summary: The Forgotten World Adventures Rail Cart adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride self-drive rail carts through 20 tunnels on the abandoned Forgotten World railway. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived in one of the most remote corners of the North Island for an adventure that exists nowhere else in New Zealand. Forgotten World Adventures has converted the abandoned railway between Whangamomona and Stratford into a unique rail cart experience. You will drive your own small motorised cart along the original tracks, passing through 20 tunnels carved through the hills over a century ago. The longest, the Moki Tunnel, stretches 1.6 kilometres and is so dark that your headlight barely touches the far end. Between tunnels, the railway crosses bridges, skirts steep hillsides, and passes through countryside so remote that some farms here are still only accessible by the rail line. The self-proclaimed Republic of Whangamomona adds a dash of Kiwi eccentricity to the journey, this tiny settlement declared independence in 1989 and has elected a goat and a poodle as president.

name: Hāwera Water Tower
short: Climb Hāwera's 1914 reinforced-concrete water tower for one of the best viewing platforms in South Taranaki — maunga, coast, and town spread below.
commentary: Hāwera Water Tower (1914) is a 54-metre reinforced concrete water tower that dominates the town skyline. Climbable via 215 internal stairs to a viewing platform.

reviews.summary: Hāwera Water Tower draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Climb Hāwera's 1914 reinforced-concrete water tower for one of the best viewing platforms in South Taranaki — maunga, coast, and town spread below. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Hāwera Water Tower, and you have found the most recognisable landmark in south Taranaki. The tower was built in nineteen fourteen by the Hāwera Borough Council as a proper municipal water supply, replacing earlier and inadequate wells and springs. At fifty four metres the tower was once the tallest reinforced concrete structure in the country, and even now it dominates the town's flat skyline from almost every angle. What makes it more than a utility monument is that it is open, on most days, to the public. For a small donation at the information centre nearby, you collect a key, cross the car park, unlock the small steel door, and climb two hundred and fifteen internal stairs to an open viewing platform at the top. From there, on a clear day, the view is extraordinary. Mount Taranaki fills the northwestern horizon, textured and close. South, the coast stretches away toward Whanganui. East, the steep hill country of the King Country rises. The town of Hāwera spreads below in a tidy grid. On windy days, and there are many in Taranaki, the tower is closed for safety. Check the information centre for current opening arrangements before you climb.

name: Kamahi Loop / Goblin Forest
short: A short loop walk on Mt Taranaki through a moss-draped cloud forest that feels like it belongs in a fairy tale.
commentary: Mt Taranaki's goblin forest of kāmahi and moss-draped kaikawaka is unique. The Kāmahi Loop at Stratford Plateau is a 30-minute introduction.

reviews.summary: Kamahi Loop / Goblin Forest draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A short loop walk on Mt Taranaki through a moss-draped cloud forest that feels like it belongs in a fairy tale. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at one of Mount Taranaki's most unusual ecological features, the goblin forest. Between around eight hundred and a thousand metres on the mountain's flanks, the forest enters a zone of persistent cloud cover. Humidity is high. Temperatures are cool. Direct sunlight is rare. In these conditions, the stunted kāmahi and kaikawaka trees that grow here become encased in thick layers of moss, liverworts, and filmy ferns. The trees grow slowly, crookedly, and are twisted by weight and weather into extraordinary shapes. Walking here, especially on a misty morning, the forest does genuinely feel enchanted. Trunks glow green. Branches seem to reach. The air is damp and still. Goblin forests occur in only a handful of places in the world, all of them in persistently wet mountainous maritime climates. The Kāmahi Loop at Stratford Plateau is the easiest introduction, a flat thirty minute walk on boardwalk. For a longer immersion, walk the Wilkies Pools Loop above Dawson Falls, an hour and a half of mossy magic. Please stay on the tracks. The soils under these forests are thin and easily compacted by boots, and the surrounding moss mat is a thousand years in the making.

name: Hollard Gardens
short: A farm garden near Eltham that became one of Taranaki's finest rhododendron and magnolia collections — free to visit, year round.
commentary: Started 1927 by Bernard and Rose Hollard on their farm. Now owned by Taranaki Regional Council. 5.5 hectares, Garden of National Significance.

reviews.summary: Hollard Gardens draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A farm garden near Eltham that became one of Taranaki's finest rhododendron and magnolia collections — free to visit, year round. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Hollard Gardens, a quiet Garden of National Significance just east of the small south Taranaki town of Eltham. Hollard Gardens is a dairy farmer's garden in origin. In nineteen twenty seven, Bernard and Rose Hollard began laying out a pleasure garden on the edge of their working dairy farm, and together over the following six decades they planted what has become one of the finest collections of rhododendrons, magnolias, and camellias in the country. Bernard had a particular passion for hybridising rhododendrons in the Taranaki climate, and the garden today contains several hundred named varieties, many of them developed on site. In nineteen eighty two the Hollards gifted the garden to the Taranaki Regional Council, and it has been free to the public ever since. Five and a half hectares of meandering paths run through cathedral-like canopies of rhododendron arching overhead, through dense plantings of hosta and rodgersia in the shade, and out into open lawns where giant magnolias bloom every spring. The garden peaks in September and October. Entry is free, parking is easy, and the volunteer run cafe at the entrance serves a legitimately excellent scone. Allow sixty minutes. Take your time.

name: Len Lye Centre, New Plymouth
short: A wave of polished stainless steel housing the world's largest collection of works by kinetic artist Len Lye.
commentary: Opened 2015 by Patterson Associates. Houses the works of Len Lye (1901–1980), a NZ-born modernist who pioneered direct film and kinetic sculpture.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Len Lye Centre, New Plymouth is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. A wave of polished stainless steel housing the world's largest collection of works by kinetic artist Len Lye. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Len Lye Centre in central New Plymouth, and even before you go inside, the building itself is the first artwork. The exterior is a great curving wave of polished stainless steel, mirror bright, designed by Patterson Associates and opened in twenty fifteen. The rippled surface throws back a constantly shifting reflection of the sky, the trees, and the people on the street. Inside, the centre houses the world's largest collection of work by Len Lye, a New Zealand born modernist who left this country in nineteen twenty six and spent the next half century in London and New York. Lye was a pioneer of two new art forms, direct film, in which he scratched and painted directly onto strips of celluloid, and kinetic sculpture, great motorised constructions of steel and rod that bend, dance, and clang. The gallery has a dedicated kinetic sculpture room where you can sit and watch one of the great works perform on a half hourly schedule. The sound is part of the art. Combine your visit with a walk along the New Plymouth Coastal Walkway just outside, and you have one of the better afternoons in the North Island.

name: New Plymouth Coastal Walkway
short: Walk 13 km along New Plymouth's award-winning coastal pathway, past the iconic Wind Wand and Len Lye Centre.
commentary: The New Plymouth Coastal Walkway stretches 13 km from the Bell Block mouth to Port Taranaki. Highlights include the Len Lye Wind Wand (a 45-metre kinetic sculpture), Te Rewa Rewa Bridge, and Pukekura Park. The walkway has won international design awards and offers views of Mount Taranaki and the Tasman Sea.

reviews.summary: The New Plymouth Coastal Walkway route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk 13 km along New Plymouth's award-winning coastal pathway, past the iconic Wind Wand and Len Lye Centre. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking the New Plymouth Coastal Walkway, 13 kilometres of award-winning pathway that hugs the Tasman Sea coastline. Ahead of you, the 45-metre Wind Wand by kinetic artist Len Lye sways and dips in the breeze — a slender red wand tipped with a glowing sphere, constantly moving, constantly mesmerising. Lye, born in Christchurch in 1901, became an internationally celebrated artist, and the Len Lye Centre nearby houses his extraordinary kinetic sculptures and experimental films. Continue along the walkway to Te Rewa Rewa Bridge, a graceful wave-shaped structure that perfectly frames Mount Taranaki when you stand at the right angle — it has become one of the most photographed spots in the region. The pathway passes rocky beaches, grassy berms, and Pukekura Park, a stunning botanical garden with a fernery and lake. To the south, Mount Taranaki dominates the skyline. This coastal walkway has set the standard for urban waterfront development in New Zealand.

name: Taranaki Maunga — The Ancestor Mountain
short: In Māori tradition, Taranaki fought Tongariro for the love of Pihanga and fled west, carving the Whanganui River.
commentary: A Treaty settlement granted the mountain legal personhood. For the eight iwi of Taranaki, the mountain is a sacred ancestor and the centre of their identity.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Taranaki Maunga — The Ancestor Mountain as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. In Māori tradition, Taranaki fought Tongariro for the love of Pihanga and fled west, carving the Whanganui River. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Mt Damper Falls
short: At 85 metres, the tallest waterfall in the North Island outside of National Parks — reached by a 50-minute farmland walk.
commentary: Mt Damper Falls is an 85-metre single-drop waterfall in rural inland Taranaki, off the Forgotten World Highway. Access is across private farmland open only via an easement.

reviews.summary: Mt Damper Falls draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. At 85 metres, the tallest waterfall in the North Island outside of National Parks — reached by a 50-minute farmland walk. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Mt Damper Falls, which at eighty five metres is one of the tallest waterfalls in the North Island and probably the tallest one that is not inside a national park. The falls are genuinely remote. They lie in the rolling hill country of inland Taranaki, down a side road from the Forgotten World Highway, and access is across private farmland via a permitted easement. The walk in is flat, about twenty five minutes each way, and crosses sheep and beef country that is quite different from the tidy dairy farms of the coastal Taranaki. You pass through several gates, please close them, cross a small stream, and emerge at a viewing platform on the lip of the canyon. The Mangaatukutuku Stream, which is much smaller than the drama of the falls might suggest, flows quietly along the farm paddock for most of its length before vaulting over the edge of a sheer sandstone bluff and dropping eighty five metres into a narrow bush clad canyon below. The view from the platform is directly into the plunge pool. In low summer flows the waterfall becomes a thin ribbon. In winter after heavy rain it is thunderous. Check the farmer's current access arrangements before you go. Take nothing. Leave nothing.

name: New Plymouth Coastal Walkway SUP
short: Paddleboard along New Plymouth's iconic coastal walkway with Taranaki as your backdrop.
commentary: Stand-up paddleboarding along the New Plymouth coast offers a unique perspective of the city's award-winning 13 km coastal walkway and the Wind Wand sculpture. The sheltered waters near the port are ideal for SUP, with Mount Taranaki as a stunning backdrop.

reviews.summary: The New Plymouth Coastal Walkway SUP adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Paddleboard along New Plymouth's iconic coastal walkway with Taranaki as your backdrop. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the New Plymouth waterfront, where a stand-up paddleboard offers one of the most scenic coastal perspectives in the North Island. From the water, you can appreciate the full sweep of the city's award-winning 13-kilometre coastal walkway, including Len Lye's iconic Wind Wand swaying on the foreshore. The waters near the port breakwater are sheltered from the prevailing swell, creating flat conditions ideal for paddling. Behind the city, Mount Taranaki dominates the skyline, its snow-capped cone reflected in the water on calm days. Paddle north towards the Len Lye Centre and the Puke Ariki landing, or south towards Paritutu Rock, a dramatic volcanic monolith that rises 154 metres from the coast. Board hire is available from operators near the waterfront. Early mornings are best for flat water and the finest mountain views.

name: Ngā Motu / Sugar Loaf Islands
short: Dramatic volcanic remnants off New Plymouth — pā sites, Māori fishing grounds, and a marine reserve.
commentary: The islands were used by Māori as pā sites, fishing platforms, and burial places. They sit within a marine protected area.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ngā Motu / Sugar Loaf Islands as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Dramatic volcanic remnants off New Plymouth — pā sites, Māori fishing grounds, and a marine reserve. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Owae Marae, Waitara
short: One of the most finely carved marae in Taranaki — the spiritual centre for Te Ātiawa.
commentary: Owae Marae features some of the finest contemporary Māori carving in the Taranaki region.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Owae Marae, Waitara as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. One of the most finely carved marae in Taranaki — the spiritual centre for Te Ātiawa. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Parihaka — The Peace Village
short: Where Te Whiti and Tohu pioneered peaceful protest decades before Gandhi — invaded by 1,600 troops in 1881.
commentary: Through ploughing confiscated land and passive resistance, they built a community of over 2,000. On 5 November 1881, troops invaded. Residents met them with singing children and offered food.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Parihaka — The Peace Village as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Te Whiti and Tohu pioneered peaceful protest decades before Gandhi — invaded by 1,600 troops in 1881. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Pariokariwa Point — Coastal Pā
short: Coastal headland pā sites along the North Taranaki coast — where volcanic cliffs meet the Tasman Sea.
commentary: The coastline is dotted with pā sites on prominent headlands, exploiting natural defences of volcanic cliffs above the surf.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pariokariwa Point — Coastal Pā as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Coastal headland pā sites along the North Taranaki coast — where volcanic cliffs meet the Tasman Sea. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

name: Pouākai Circuit
short: A 25km two-day loop in Egmont National Park with the most photographed reflection of Mount Taranaki.
commentary: The Pouākai Circuit traverses the Pouākai Range north of Mt Taranaki. The Pouākai Tarns are the famous reflective alpine pools.

reviews.summary: Pouākai Circuit draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 25km two-day loop in Egmont National Park with the most photographed reflection of Mount Taranaki.
