What makes Fox special is its relative solitude and the views — on a clear day, you can see Aoraki/Mount Cook and Mount Tasman, the two highest peaks in New Zealand, rising above the glacier's source. The ice beneath your crampons is ancient, compressed, and constantly moving, a frozen river grinding its way toward the coast.

name: Franz Josef Glacier / Ka Roimata o Hine Hukatere Walk
short: Approach the dramatic terminal face of Franz Josef Glacier through a valley of ancient rock and new growth.
commentary: Franz Josef Glacier / Ka Roimata o Hine Hukatere is a 12 km glacier descending from near the main divide to 240 m above sea level. The valley walk to the current viewpoint is approximately 5.4 km return. The Maori name translates to 'the tears of Hine Hukatere,' from a legend about a woman whose tears froze to form the glacier. Heli-hike and scenic flights offer closer access to the ice.

reviews.summary: The Franz Josef Glacier / Ka Roimata o Hine Hukatere Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Approach the dramatic terminal face of Franz Josef Glacier through a valley of ancient rock and new growth. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking up the Waiho River valley toward Franz Josef Glacier, and the Maori name for this place tells you everything about its power. Ka Roimata o Hine Hukatere means 'the tears of Hine Hukatere,' a woman in Maori tradition who loved the mountains so much that her tears of joy froze into this river of ice. The valley walk takes you through a landscape in constant change — the glacier's retreat has left behind walls of moraine and pools of milky water. The terminal face, when you reach the viewpoint, is a tumbled cliff of ice that creaks and shifts as gravity pulls it downward. Franz Josef was named by the geologist Julius von Haast in 1865, after the Emperor of Austria-Hungary. Helicopter flights from the township can land you on the ice itself, where crevasses glow blue and the silence is absolute.

name: Franz Josef Glacier Heli-Hike
short: Helicopter onto Franz Josef Glacier and hike through ancient blue ice formations.
commentary: The Franz Josef Glacier heli-hike flies you by helicopter onto the glacier for a guided walk among ice caves, seracs, and crevasses. The glacier descends from 3,000 metres to just 240 metres above sea level — one of the steepest and fastest-moving glaciers in the world. Crampons and equipment are provided.

reviews.summary: The Franz Josef Glacier Heli-Hike adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Helicopter onto Franz Josef Glacier and hike through ancient blue ice formations. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Franz Josef Glacier, known to Maori as Ka Roimata o Hine Hukatere — the tears of the avalanche girl. This is one of the most accessible glaciers on earth, descending from 3,000 metres in the Southern Alps to just 240 metres above sea level, almost to the temperate rainforest. A helicopter will lift you onto the glacier's mid-section, where your guide will lead you through a wonderland of blue ice caves, towering seracs, and deep crevasses. The ice glows an incredible shade of blue caused by compressed air bubbles, and the formations change daily as the glacier creeps forward at up to 70 centimetres per day. You'll strap on crampons and explore ice features that literally didn't exist yesterday and won't exist tomorrow. It's a walk on one of earth's great frozen rivers.

name: Great Coast Road (SH6 Greymouth-Hokitika)
short: One of New Zealand's top scenic drives, hugging the wild Tasman Sea coast between Greymouth and Hokitika.
commentary: The Great Coast Road runs 44 km along SH6 between Greymouth and Hokitika, hugging the Tasman Sea coast. Named one of the 'Top 10 Coastal Drives in the World' by Lonely Planet, the road passes between surf-battered beaches and bush-clad hills. It was originally carved from the coastal rock face and can be dramatic in stormy weather when waves crash across the road.

reviews.summary: The Great Coast Road (SH6 Greymouth-Hokitika) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. One of New Zealand's top scenic drives, hugging the wild Tasman Sea coast between Greymouth and Hokitika. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the Great Coast Road, and the Tasman Sea is throwing everything it has at the shoreline to your right. This 44-kilometre stretch of SH6 between Greymouth and Hokitika was carved from coastal rock and clings to the edge of the land with a determination that feels almost personal. Lonely Planet named it one of the top ten coastal drives in the world, and on a big swell day you will understand why — waves explode against the sea walls and spray drifts across the road. To your left, the bush-covered hills are impossibly green, fed by rain that can arrive horizontally off the Tasman. The road passes through small settlements that were once mining towns, and at several points you can pull over and walk onto black-sand beaches where driftwood logs the size of trees lie scattered like matchsticks.

name: Ship Creek & Monro Beach Walk
short: Walk from ancient rainforest swamp to a wild coast where Fiordland crested penguins come ashore.
commentary: Ship Creek, south of Haast on SH6, offers two short walks: the Kahikatea Swamp Walk through a flooded podocarp forest, and the Dune Lake Walk to a wild beach. Nearby Monro Beach is one of the few accessible locations where the rare tawaki / Fiordland crested penguin can be seen coming ashore between July and November. Ship Creek was named after a shipwreck in the 1860s.

reviews.summary: The Ship Creek & Monro Beach Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk from ancient rainforest swamp to a wild coast where Fiordland crested penguins come ashore. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking the boardwalk at Ship Creek, and the kahikatea forest around you is standing in water. This is a swamp forest — the trees rise from permanently flooded ground, their buttress roots spreading wide for stability, and the reflections in the dark water create a mirror world beneath the canopy. A second track leads over sand dunes to a wild, driftwood-strewn beach facing the Tasman Sea. But the real treasure is nearby Monro Beach: between July and November, tawaki — Fiordland crested penguins, one of the rarest penguin species on Earth — come ashore here through the surf and waddle into the bush to nest. Seeing one emerge from the waves, its yellow crests slicked back, is a wildlife encounter that few people outside New Zealand even know is possible.

name: Hokitika Gorge Walk
short: Walk to a gorge where the Hokitika River runs an electric turquoise through ancient podocarp forest.
commentary: Hokitika Gorge is a short walk (about 1 km return) through native podocarp forest to a swing bridge above the Hokitika River where the water is an intense, milky turquoise. The colour is caused by glacial flour — finely ground rock particles suspended in the water. The surrounding forest includes rimu, miro, and kahikatea trees up to 600 years old.

reviews.summary: The Hokitika Gorge Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to a gorge where the Hokitika River runs an electric turquoise through ancient podocarp forest. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking through ancient podocarp forest toward the Hokitika Gorge, and when the river appears below the swing bridge, the colour hits you like a surprise. The Hokitika River is running a blue so vivid, so electric, that it looks like someone has poured pigment into the water. This is glacial flour — rock ground to powder by the glaciers at the river's source in the Southern Alps — and when it catches the light, the effect is otherworldly. The forest around you is equally impressive: rimu and kahikatea trees up to 600 years old, their trunks wrapped in mosses and epiphytes, their canopy blocking most of the sky. This is one of the West Coast's most accessible natural spectacles — the walk takes just 15 minutes — but the combination of ancient forest and impossible water colour makes it one of the most memorable.

name: Jackson Bay / Okahu Road
short: Drive to the end of the road at Jackson Bay — one of the wettest, most remote settlements in New Zealand.
commentary: The road from Haast to Jackson Bay / Okahu covers 50 km through dense rainforest to one of the most isolated settlements in New Zealand. Jackson Bay was the site of a failed 19th-century immigration settlement and receives over 5,000 mm of rain annually. The Whitebaiter's Track and Smoothwater Bay Track offer walks through pristine coastal bush. The settlement has a population of about 30.

reviews.summary: The Jackson Bay / Okahu Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive to the end of the road at Jackson Bay — one of the wettest, most remote settlements in New Zealand. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the Jackson Bay Road, and with each kilometre the world gets smaller and wilder. The road runs 50 kilometres south of Haast through rainforest so thick that the canopy arches over the road like a green tunnel. At the end sits Jackson Bay, a fishing settlement of about 30 hardy souls in one of the wettest locations in New Zealand — over five metres of rain falls here annually. In the 1870s, the government attempted to establish an immigrant settlement here, bringing in workers from across Europe. It was a disaster: the rain was relentless, the bush impenetrable, and the promised infrastructure never materialised. Most settlers left within years. Today, the old wharf juts into the bay, fishing boats bob on the grey water, and the Cray Pot cafe serves fresh fish. This is the end of the road in every sense, and there is a peace here that comes from having nowhere further to go.

name: Lake Brunner Trout Fishing
short: Fish for trophy trout in the untouched waters of Lake Brunner.
commentary: Lake Brunner (Moana Kotuku) is the West Coast's largest lake and one of New Zealand's top trout fisheries. The lake and its tributaries hold large brown and rainbow trout in pristine bush-fringed waters. Guided trips use fly fishing and trolling methods. The Arnold River outlet is particularly renowned.

reviews.summary: The Lake Brunner Trout Fishing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Fish for trophy trout in the untouched waters of Lake Brunner. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Lake Brunner, known in Maori as Moana Kotuku — the Sea of the White Heron. This is the largest lake on the West Coast and one of New Zealand's most beautiful and productive trout fisheries. The lake is fringed by dense native bush, backed by the Paparoa Range, and home to brown and rainbow trout that grow large on the rich food supply. The Arnold River, which flows from the lake, is particularly renowned among fly fishers for its trophy-sized brown trout — fish over five pounds are not uncommon, and the sight of a large trout rising to a dry fly in the Arnold's clear water is enough to make any angler's heart race. Guided trips will put you on the fish, whether you prefer trolling the lake's deep waters or casting a fly in the river shallows. The West Coast bush and mountain scenery surrounding the lake is world-class, and on a calm morning, the reflections on the water are perfect.

name: Lake Matheson Mirror Walk
short: Walk around the 'mirror lake' that perfectly reflects Aoraki/Mt Cook and Mt Tasman on still mornings.
commentary: Lake Matheson is a small kettle lake formed by the Fox Glacier about 14,000 years ago. The dark tannin-stained water creates perfect reflections of Aoraki/Mt Cook and Mt Tasman, making it one of the most photographed locations in New Zealand. The 2.6 km loop walk through kahikatea forest is best visited at dawn when the lake is stillest.

reviews.summary: The Lake Matheson Mirror Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk around the 'mirror lake' that perfectly reflects Aoraki/Mt Cook and Mt Tasman on still mornings. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking the loop track around Lake Matheson, and if you have timed it right — early morning, no wind — you are about to see one of New Zealand's most iconic images in person. The lake is small and dark, its water stained brown by tannins from the surrounding forest, and that darkness creates a perfect mirror. When Aoraki/Mt Cook and Mt Tasman appear reflected in the still surface, the image is so sharp, so symmetrical, that photographs of it look manipulated. But it is real. The lake itself was formed about 14,000 years ago when the Fox Glacier retreated and left a block of buried ice that melted into this hollow. The surrounding kahikatea forest drips with moss, and the boardwalk winds through a green world so lush it feels submarine. Get here before the wind rises and the other walkers arrive.

name: Okarito Lagoon Kayaking
short: Kayak New Zealand's largest unmodified wetland, home to the rare kotuku white heron.
commentary: Okarito Lagoon is New Zealand's largest unmodified wetland and the sole breeding ground for the kotuku (white heron), one of New Zealand's rarest birds. Kayaking the lagoon offers intimate wildlife encounters in a pristine wetland backed by the Southern Alps. Guided and self-guided options available.

reviews.summary: The Okarito Lagoon Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak New Zealand's largest unmodified wetland, home to the rare kotuku white heron. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Okarito Lagoon, the largest unmodified wetland in New Zealand and one of its best-kept secrets. Your kayak will carry you silently across three thousand hectares of tidal lagoon, through channels lined with native bush and backed by the towering wall of the Southern Alps. This is the only breeding ground for the kotuku, the white heron — one of New Zealand's rarest and most sacred birds, with fewer than 200 remaining. Paddle quietly and you may see one standing motionless in the shallows, or watch a royal spoonbill sweep its beak through the water. The lagoon changes character with the tides, revealing mudflats teeming with wading birds at low water and becoming a glassy mirror at high tide. The tiny settlement of Okarito has just 30 permanent residents, and the sense of solitude here is profound.

name: Old Ghost Road Trail
short: Ride or walk 85 km of remote backcountry trail that completes the route gold miners began in the 1870s.
commentary: The Old Ghost Road is an 85 km trail from Lyell to Seddonville, completing a route that gold miners began but never finished in the 1870s. Built by volunteers over 10 years and opened in 2015, it traverses some of the most remote terrain on the West Coast, including the Lyell Range at 1,280 m. It is considered one of the best mountain bike trails in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: The Old Ghost Road Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Ride or walk 85 km of remote backcountry trail that completes the route gold miners began in the 1870s. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are setting out on the Old Ghost Road, and the name tells you something important: this trail exists because of a road that was never finished. In the 1870s, gold miners began building a road from the Buller Gorge through to Seddonville on the coast, but the terrain defeated them. The bush was too thick, the mountains too steep, the budget too thin. The road was abandoned, and the forest reclaimed it. Over a hundred years later, a dedicated team of volunteers spent a decade building an 85-kilometre trail that finally completes the route. Opened in 2015, the Old Ghost Road is now considered one of the finest backcountry trails in New Zealand — a demanding multi-day ride or walk through river valleys, over the Lyell Range at 1,280 metres, and down through ancient beech forest to the coast. It is a monument to stubbornness, both the miners' and the volunteers'.

name: Oparara Basin & Arches Walk
short: Walk to massive limestone arches hidden in primeval rainforest near Karamea — the West Coast's secret wonder.
commentary: The Oparara Basin near Karamea contains the Oparara Arch, one of the largest natural arches in the Southern Hemisphere at 219 m long, 79 m wide, and 37 m high. The basin is accessed by a narrow gravel road and features several limestone arches, caves containing sub-fossil moa bones, and pristine podocarp forest. It is one of the least-visited significant natural sites in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: The Oparara Basin & Arches Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to massive limestone arches hidden in primeval rainforest near Karamea — the West Coast's secret wonder. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are driving the narrow gravel road into the Oparara Basin, and very few tourists make it this far. Karamea is already the end of the road on the West Coast, and the Oparara Basin is another 16 kilometres beyond on a track that discourages all but the curious. But what waits here is extraordinary. The Oparara Arch is one of the largest natural limestone arches in the Southern Hemisphere — 219 metres long, nearly 80 metres wide, and 37 metres high. The Oparara River flows through it, dark and tea-stained, beneath a canopy of ancient rainforest. In the caves nearby, sub-fossil bones of moa and other extinct birds have been found, preserved in the limestone for thousands of years. Standing beneath the arch, with the forest pressing in and the river murmuring through the gloom, you feel the weight of geological time.

name: Pancake Rocks & Blowholes Walk
short: Walk among the layered limestone formations and surging blowholes at Punakaiki.
commentary: The Pancake Rocks at Dolomite Point, Punakaiki, are heavily eroded limestone formations resembling stacks of pancakes. The rocks were formed 30 million years ago from layers of marine organisms and were uplifted and sculpted by seawater. At high tide, seawater surges through caverns beneath the rocks and explodes upward through blowholes. The short walk takes about 20 minutes.

reviews.summary: The Pancake Rocks & Blowholes Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk among the layered limestone formations and surging blowholes at Punakaiki. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking among the Pancake Rocks at Punakaiki, and the geology here is genuinely strange. These layered limestone formations look exactly like stacks of giant pancakes, each layer a few centimetres thick and separated by softer bands that have eroded away over millions of years. The rocks formed 30 million years ago on the sea floor from alternating layers of hard and soft sediment. Then they were lifted, tilted, and the sea went to work sculpting them into the formations you see today. At high tide — especially during a big swell — seawater forces its way through underground caverns and erupts through blowholes with a boom that you feel in your chest. The spray shoots metres into the air, and if you are standing too close, you will get drenched. Time your visit for high tide if you can; the blowholes at low tide are merely impressive rather than extraordinary.

name: Paparoa Track Mountain Biking
short: Ride or walk New Zealand's newest Great Walk through the Paparoa Range.
commentary: The Paparoa Track is New Zealand's newest Great Walk and the only one designed for both walking and mountain biking. The 55 km track crosses the Paparoa Range from Blackball to Punakaiki, passing through subalpine landscapes and lush bush. It was built as a memorial to the 29 miners lost in the 2010 Pike River disaster.

reviews.summary: The Paparoa Track Mountain Biking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride or walk New Zealand's newest Great Walk through the Paparoa Range. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the start of the Paparoa Track, New Zealand's newest Great Walk and the only one designed for both trampers and mountain bikers. This 55-kilometre trail crosses the Paparoa Range through landscapes that shift from dense West Coast bush to open subalpine tops with views to the Tasman Sea and the Southern Alps. The track was built as a living memorial to the 29 men who lost their lives in the Pike River mine disaster of 2010, and there's a profound sense of purpose woven into every kilometre. Two new purpose-built huts provide comfortable overnight accommodation along the way. Whether you walk it in two to three days or ride it in one or two, the Paparoa Track offers a genuine wilderness experience through terrain that few people have ever seen up close. It's a fitting tribute to those who are remembered here.

name: Hokitika Pounamu Heritage Trail
short: Explore the pounamu capital of New Zealand — where jade rivers meet the Tasman Sea.
commentary: Hokitika is the traditional centre of New Zealand's pounamu (greenstone/jade) industry. The West Coast rivers, particularly the Arahura, are the primary source of pounamu, which holds immense spiritual significance for Maori. The town has numerous jade carving studios and galleries. Pounamu can only be legally collected from the West Coast with Ngai Tahu permission.

reviews.summary: The Hokitika Pounamu Heritage Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Explore the pounamu capital of New Zealand — where jade rivers meet the Tasman Sea. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking through Hokitika, and this small West Coast town is the pounamu capital of New Zealand. Pounamu — greenstone, nephrite jade — is found in the rivers that flow from the Southern Alps to the Tasman Sea, particularly the Arahura River just north of town. For Maori, pounamu is a taonga of immense spiritual value, more precious than gold, and the West Coast's pounamu resources are the reason Ngai Tahu sent expeditions over the Alps for centuries. Today, Hokitika's studios keep the carving tradition alive, and you can watch artisans shape raw river stones into pendants, mere, and tiki using techniques both ancient and modern. The Ngai Tahu iwi hold legal guardianship over all West Coast pounamu — it cannot be commercially collected without their authority, a recognition of a relationship with this stone that stretches back over a thousand years.

name: Punakaiki Pancake Rocks & Blowholes
short: Watch the sea explode through blowholes at the iconic Pancake Rocks.
commentary: The Pancake Rocks at Punakaiki are layered limestone formations that resemble stacks of pancakes, formed 30 million years ago. At high tide, the sea surges through blowholes with dramatic force. The short loop track is wheelchair accessible and free to visit.

reviews.summary: The Punakaiki Pancake Rocks & Blowholes adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Watch the sea explode through blowholes at the iconic Pancake Rocks. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Pancake Rocks, one of the West Coast's most iconic natural wonders. These extraordinary limestone formations were laid down on an ancient seabed 30 million years ago, and the alternating layers of hard and soft limestone have been eroded by wind and rain into shapes that look remarkably like stacks of giant pancakes. But the real show is the blowholes — at high tide, the Tasman Sea surges into caverns beneath the rocks and explodes upward through narrow openings with a thundering boom, sending spray metres into the air. If you've timed your visit with a high tide and a big swell, the display is genuinely spectacular. The short loop walk is easy and accessible, winding through coastal bush with nikau palms — the most southerly palm tree in the world. Free to visit, and no two visits are ever quite the same.

name: Reefton Historic Walk
short: Walk through the town that was first in the Southern Hemisphere to have electric street lights, in 1888.
commentary: Reefton was the first town in the Southern Hemisphere to have a public electricity supply and electric street lighting, installed in 1888 — just six years after New York. The gold-mining town has a well-preserved Victorian streetscape and a heritage walk that covers its mining history, early electricity, and the boom-and-bust cycles that shaped West Coast towns.

reviews.summary: The Reefton Historic Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk through the town that was first in the Southern Hemisphere to have electric street lights, in 1888. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are walking through Reefton, and this small West Coast town did something remarkable in 1888: it switched on electric street lights, becoming the first town in the entire Southern Hemisphere to have a public electricity supply. This was just six years after Thomas Edison illuminated part of Manhattan. The power came from a small hydro scheme on the Inangahua River, and for a few glorious years, tiny Reefton was more technologically advanced than Sydney, Melbourne, and every other city south of the equator. The gold that funded this innovation came from quartz reef mines in the surrounding hills — hard rock mining that required crushing machinery, which required electricity, which required the kind of ambitious thinking that seems extraordinary in a town this small. The heritage walk takes you past the old powerhouse site, the miners' cottages, and the main street where those lights first blazed.

name: West Coast Sand Dune Adventures
short: Ride ATVs and sandboard down massive coastal dunes on the West Coast.
commentary: The West Coast's coastal sand dunes near Hokitika offer quad biking and sandboarding experiences. The dunes rise up to 30 metres above the beach with views of the Tasman Sea. The remote, wild setting on the West Coast adds to the adventure atmosphere.

reviews.summary: The West Coast Sand Dune Adventures adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride ATVs and sandboard down massive coastal dunes on the West Coast. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the West Coast's coastal sand dunes, where mountains of golden sand meet the wild Tasman Sea. These aren't gentle ripples — some of these dunes rise over 30 metres above the beach, and you're about to conquer them. Climb aboard an ATV and carve your way across the dune field, or grab a sandboard and slide down the steep faces at surprising speed. The West Coast setting is dramatic — to one side, the dunes and the crashing surf; to the other, dense native bush backed by the distant Southern Alps. The sand is firm enough for serious speed but soft enough for safe landings when sandboarding. This is a raw, unpolished adventure that captures the wild spirit of the West Coast — no fancy infrastructure, just sand, sea, and adrenaline. Bring sunscreen and a sense of adventure.

name: Skydive Franz Josef & Fox Glacier
short: Skydive over glaciers, rainforest, and the Tasman Sea all in one jump.
commentary: Skydive Franz Josef offers tandem jumps from up to 20,000 feet — the highest commercial skydive in New Zealand. Views span glaciers, the Southern Alps, ancient rainforest, and the Tasman Sea coastline. The unique geography means you can see mountains, forest, and ocean simultaneously during freefall.

reviews.summary: The Skydive Franz Josef & Fox Glacier adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Skydive over glaciers, rainforest, and the Tasman Sea all in one jump. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at what might be the most scenic skydiving drop zone in the world. Skydive Franz Josef takes you up to 20,000 feet — higher than any other commercial skydive in New Zealand — and the views from up there are beyond belief. You'll see both the east and west coasts of the South Island, the white rivers of Franz Josef and Fox Glaciers flowing through emerald rainforest, and the infinite blue of the Tasman Sea. When you jump, you freefall for up to 75 seconds, hurtling toward a landscape that combines every type of New Zealand scenery in a single panorama — snow, ice, mountains, rainforest, rivers, and ocean. It's the combination of altitude, scenery, and sheer diversity of landscape that makes this jump truly unique. If you're going to skydive once in your life, this is a very strong contender.

name: Underworld Adventures Charleston
short: Float through glow-worm-lit caves on an underground river adventure.
commentary: Underworld Adventures at Charleston offers guided cave experiences including the Rainforest to Underworld tour, where you travel through rainforest to reach an underground river and float through caves illuminated by thousands of glow-worms. The cave system includes ancient fossils and dramatic limestone formations.

reviews.summary: The Underworld Adventures Charleston adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Float through glow-worm-lit caves on an underground river adventure. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at Charleston, gateway to an extraordinary underground world. Your adventure begins with a journey through dense West Coast rainforest before you descend into a cave system that has been sculpted by water over millions of years. The highlight is floating down an underground river on a tube, drifting through darkness as thousands of glow-worms light up the cave ceiling above you like a living galaxy. These tiny creatures — actually the larvae of a native fungus gnat — produce a bioluminescent glow to attract prey, and in the darkness of the cave, the effect is truly magical. The cave system also contains ancient marine fossils embedded in the limestone walls, evidence that this land was once beneath the sea. It's an adventure that combines wonder, geology, and a healthy dose of getting wet in the dark.

name: West Coast Treetop Walkway
short: Walk among the canopy of ancient rainforest on a steel treetop platform.
commentary: The West Coast Treetop Walkway near Hokitika rises 20 metres into the canopy of a lowland podocarp rainforest on a steel and timber platform. The walkway spans 450 metres with a 47-metre-high tower offering panoramic views of the forest, Lake Mahinapua, and the Southern Alps.

reviews.summary: The West Coast Treetop Walkway adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Walk among the canopy of ancient rainforest on a steel treetop platform. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

audio_story: You have arrived at the West Coast Treetop Walkway, where a network of steel platforms and walkways lifts you 20 metres into the canopy of one of New Zealand's finest lowland rainforests. The 450-metre walkway threads through rimu, kahikatea, and kamahi trees that are hundreds of years old, and at canopy level you'll see an entirely different world from the forest floor — epiphytes clinging to branches, birds flitting through the leaves, and shafts of light filtering through the green. The highlight is the Hokitika Tower, rising 47 metres above the forest floor to give you panoramic views across Lake Mahinapua to the snow-capped Southern Alps. Standing at the top with the forest stretching out in every direction below you, you really grasp the vastness of the West Coast bush. It's peaceful, beautiful, and gently exhilarating all at once.

name: West Coast Wilderness Trail
short: Cycle 136 km through ancient rainforest, glacial valleys, and gold mining ghost towns on the Wild West Coast.
commentary: The West Coast Wilderness Trail covers 136 km from Greymouth to Ross, passing through podocarp rainforest, wetlands, and former gold mining settlements. The trail uses old tramways, water races, and railway corridors. It traverses some of the highest-rainfall landscapes in New Zealand, where the bush is extraordinarily lush and the rivers run tea-brown with tannins.

reviews.summary: The West Coast Wilderness Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cycle 136 km through ancient rainforest, glacial valleys, and gold mining ghost towns on the Wild West Coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

audio_story: You are pedalling the West Coast Wilderness Trail, and the bush here is so dense, so green, so wet that it feels like cycling through a living tunnel. The 136 kilometres from Greymouth to Ross follow old mining tramways and water races through podocarp rainforest that has stood for centuries. The West Coast gets up to five metres of rain a year in places, and you can see it in every surface — moss covers the branches, ferns erupt from every crevice, and the rivers run brown with tannins leached from the forest floor. You will ride past the remains of gold dredges rusting in lakes, through the former boom town of Kumara, and alongside wetlands where white herons stalk through the shallows. At Ross, the trail ends in a town that once produced the largest gold nugget ever found in New Zealand — the 99-ounce Honourable Doris, discovered in 1909.

name: Blackball
short: The coal town where New Zealand's Labour Party was born — and where the best salami in the country is still smoked.
commentary: Blackball was the site of the 1908 miners' strike that led directly to the formation of the Labour Party. The Blackball Salami Co. operates from the old butchery.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Blackball — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The coal town where New Zealand's Labour Party was born — and where the best salami in the country is still smoked. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have arrived in Blackball, a small West Coast settlement tucked up a side road off the main highway between Greymouth and Reefton, and you have arrived in the birthplace of an entire political movement. Coal was mined here from the eighteen nineties. In nineteen oh eight a dispute about meal breaks in the mine escalated into a three month strike, led by the charismatic unionist Pat Hickey. The strike was the first major industrial action in the country and it radicalised a generation of union activists, many of whom went on to found the New Zealand Labour Party in nineteen sixteen. The mine closed in nineteen sixty four, but the town has never quite given up its political memory. The pub, which was called, with characteristic coast humour, Formerly The Blackball Hilton, keeps the history alive with photographs, songs, and the occasional visiting historian. Around the corner, the Blackball Salami Company operates out of the old butcher shop and smokes some of the best salami in the country, drawing West Coasters and visitors alike for their Saturday morning queues. Sit on the pub veranda, order a pie, and think about the quiet West Coast towns where this country, for better and for worse, made itself.

name: Jackson Bay / Ōkahu
short: The end of the road on the West Coast — a tiny fishing village, a famous food caravan, and the ruins of a failed 1870s immigrant settlement.
commentary: Jackson Bay is the southernmost settlement on the West Coast. In the 1870s it was the site of a disastrous government assisted immigration scheme.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Jackson Bay / Ōkahu — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The end of the road on the West Coast — a tiny fishing village, a famous food caravan, and the ruins of a failed 1870s immigrant settlement. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have driven to the end of the road. Jackson Bay, also known by its Māori name of Ōkahu, sits at the southernmost end of the West Coast highway, beyond Haast, at the bottom of a long winding road that hugs the side of wild forested mountains. The village is small, perhaps thirty permanent residents, a handful of crayfish boats on the jetty, a small fish packing shed, and a single famous food caravan called the Cray Pot that serves fresh seafood to anyone willing to make the drive. Behind the slow, sunlit simplicity of the bay today lies a much sadder story. In the eighteen seventies the colonial government tried to establish a major planned settlement here with hundreds of assisted immigrants from Italy, Poland, Germany and elsewhere. The scheme was a disaster. The land was too wet, the forest too dense, the rainfall too extreme, the access too poor. Within a few years most of the families had left. A few stayed, and some of their descendants still live on the coast. Walk the short Wharekai Te Kou loop track along the bay, eat a bowl of mussels at the Cray Pot, and consider the difficult gap between a map and a place.

name: Ōparara Basin Arches
short: Giant natural limestone arches and caves in an untouched beech and nīkau forest, reached by a long gravel road north of Karamea.
commentary: Deep in Kahurangi National Park, the Ōparara Arches include Moria Gate (43m) and the 200m-long Ōparara Arch. Access is a bumpy gravel road from Karamea.

reviews.summary: Ōparara Basin Arches draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Giant natural limestone arches and caves in an untouched beech and nīkau forest, reached by a long gravel road north of Karamea. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have driven a long way up the West Coast, past Westport, past Mokihinui, past Karamea, and onto a narrow gravel road that winds into the Ōparara Basin. The road is rough and slow, and you begin to wonder if you made the right choice, and then you arrive, and you know you did. Hidden here, inside Kahurangi National Park, are some of the most spectacular limestone arches in the country. The largest, the Ōparara Arch, is a vast orange stone tunnel two hundred metres long and forty metres high, carved over millions of years by the tannin-stained Ōparara River. Walk through its shadowed centre and listen to your voice come back at you from the distant far end. Follow the loop tracks on to Moria Gate, a smaller but equally lovely arch named by a local guide who saw Tolkien in its shape. Nearby, short side tracks lead into the Crazy Paving and Box Canyon caves, where with a torch you can explore small dry cave passages and see the rare cave spider Spelungula on the walls. Bring layers. Allow a full day from Karamea. Drive slowly. This is one of the quiet great experiences of the West Coast.

name: Reefton — Town of Light
short: The first town in the Southern Hemisphere to have electric street lighting — lit up in 1888 thanks to gold rush wealth.
commentary: Reefton was a wealthy quartz-gold mining town in the 1880s. On 4 August 1888 it became the first town in the Southern Hemisphere with electric street lighting.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Reefton — Town of Light — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The first town in the Southern Hemisphere to have electric street lighting — lit up in 1888 thanks to gold rush wealth. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have arrived in Reefton, a small inland town on the West Coast, and you have arrived in the place that was lit up before any other town in the southern half of the world. Reefton is quartz gold country. Through the eighteen seventies and eighties, deep hard rock mines were sunk into the ridges around the town to extract gold that was locked inside veins of quartz. The technology was industrial, the capital was considerable, and the town became briefly one of the wealthiest in the colony. In eighteen eighty eight the Reefton Electric Light and Power Company, an extraordinarily forward looking local venture, completed a small hydro station on the Inangahua River and switched on electric street lighting on the fourth of August. Reefton thereby became the first town in the southern hemisphere with street lights powered by electricity. Today the town still leans into the story. A small museum and the original power house site are open to visitors, and the main street still has many of its eighteen eighties facades. Costumed guides sometimes walk visitors through the town in character as nineteenth century residents. Stop for a coffee at the Broadway Tearooms, and take a photograph of the preserved stamper battery at the end of town.

name: Shantytown Heritage Park
short: A recreated 1860s gold-rush town with steam trains, a church, a pub, and a working gold-panning stream.
commentary: Shantytown opened 1971 and is a living-history recreation of a West Coast gold-rush town, with over 30 buildings and a working bush steam tram.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Shantytown Heritage Park — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A recreated 1860s gold-rush town with steam trains, a church, a pub, and a working gold-panning stream. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have arrived at Shantytown, about twenty minutes south of Greymouth, and you are about to spend a few hours in the eighteen sixties. The park opened in nineteen seventy one and grew over the following decades into a complete living history recreation of a West Coast gold rush town. More than thirty buildings line its streets, including a printing office, a blacksmith, a gaol, a general store, a church, and a proper working pub that serves a decent lunch. At the back of the town, a historic bush steam tram runs on a short rail loop through the regenerating bush to a working gold panning stream, where you can try your luck and actually take home the small flakes you pan. The park is staffed by volunteers and local re-enactors in period costume, and the atmosphere is deliberately unpolished, which is the better for it. Children love the steam train and the pan for your own gold activity. History-minded adults will appreciate the buildings, many of which have been carefully relocated from original sites around the West Coast. Allow at least two hours. You will leave a little sooty, a little dusty, and with a much clearer idea of what a mid Victorian boom town actually smelled and sounded like.

name: Bridge to Nowhere
short: A concrete 1936 road bridge in the middle of deep bush — serves no road, no houses. The settlement it was built for didn't survive.
commentary: After WW1, the government allocated bush blocks along the Mangapurua Valley (near the Whanganui River) to returned soldiers. By 1927 about 30 families were farming there. The country was too steep, the soil too poor, the isolation too great. The Great Depression tipped the settlement into collapse. The Bridge was completed in 1936, two years before the last family walked out; it has now stood unused in the bush for over 85 years. Access is via the Whanganui Journey (canoe) or a 40-minute walk from the jetboat landing at Mangapurua. There is no road.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Bridge to Nowhere — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A concrete 1936 road bridge in the middle of deep bush — serves no road, no houses. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

name: Bulls
short: The small town where every shop-front sign ends in '-a-bull'. Read-a-bull. Deli-bull. Afford-a-bull.
commentary: Bulls is a small town of ~1,800 people on SH1 at the Rangitikei River crossing. It's named not after livestock but after James Bull, an early settler. In the 1990s the local Community Board started a campaign of relentless self-pun — the public toilets are 'Relieve-a-Bull', the police station is 'Const-a-Bull', the information centre is 'Inform-a-Bull', and so on. It has become the defining civic joke. Stop, take the photos, buy a pie, drive on. Don't over-think it.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Bulls is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The small town where every shop-front sign ends in '-a-bull'. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Castlecliff Beach & Whanganui Heads
short: Whanganui's black-sand surf beach at the mouth of the river — wild Tasman surf, driftwood, and a good summer surf break.
commentary: Castlecliff is the river-mouth suburb of Whanganui. The iron-sand beach runs for some 10 km and is patrolled in summer. The river bar is hazardous for boaties.

reviews.summary: Castlecliff Beach & Whanganui Heads draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Whanganui's black-sand surf beach at the mouth of the river — wild Tasman surf, driftwood, and a good summer surf break. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at Castlecliff, the seaside suburb of Whanganui five kilometres from the city centre at the mouth of the Whanganui River. The beach here is black iron sand, hot to walk on in January and stretching northwest for many kilometres in a broad, almost perfectly straight arc. On calm days it is a family beach with gentle waves and patrolled flags during summer school holidays. On a south westerly the Tasman Sea tears in unchecked and the surf can be startling. Surfers have ridden the left-hand break off the mole for decades. At the southern end of the beach the Whanganui River pushes out across its bar and meets the sea in a confused sprawl of brown river water and white salt foam. The bar is genuinely dangerous to small boats and every few years takes a life. Sit on the dunes, watch the river meet the ocean, and feel the steady easterly that almost always blows. If the tide is right, walk north along the beach toward Kai Iwi where in the right conditions you can find the fossilised bones of long extinct whales tumbling out of the cliff. A thermos of something hot is recommended.

name: Cooks Gardens & Peter Snell's Mile
short: The grass athletics track where Peter Snell broke the world mile record on a grass track in 1962 — still a functioning local stadium.
commentary: On 27 January 1962, New Zealander Peter Snell ran 3 minutes 54.4 seconds at Cooks Gardens, breaking the world mile record on a grass track — still the last world mile record run on grass.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Cooks Gardens & Peter Snell's Mile — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The grass athletics track where Peter Snell broke the world mile record on a grass track in 1962 — still a functioning local stadium. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have arrived at Cooks Gardens, a modest municipal sports ground in the middle of Whanganui that happens to be one of the most significant pieces of athletics real estate in the world. On the twenty seventh of January nineteen sixty two, a twenty three year old New Zealand runner named Peter Snell lined up in front of a crowd of a few thousand spectators and in the course of four laps broke the world mile record, running three minutes fifty four point four seconds. Snell's time was astonishing in itself. What makes it legendary is that he did it on a grass track. Every world record run for the mile before and since Snell's run has been run on a cinder or synthetic surface. Snell's grass-track record stands today as the last world mile record run on turf, and probably always will. A simple plaque on the inside of the grandstand marks the spot. The track still hosts school sports and provincial meets. Stand on the finish line for a moment and imagine the scene. Snell ran the last quarter in fifty six seconds. If you know anything about middle-distance running, you will understand what that means.

name: Durie Hill Elevator & Memorial Tower
short: Walk through a 205-metre pedestrian tunnel, take a 1919 lift 66 metres up through the hill, climb the tower — Taranaki to Ruapehu on a clear day.
commentary: In 1919 the Durie Hill Elevator opened to connect Whanganui's town centre with the new suburban development on top of Durie Hill, 66 metres above the river. Walk through a 205-metre horizontal pedestrian tunnel from Anzac Parade, then ride the original 1919 lift (the only public underground lift in New Zealand) up to the hilltop. At the top, climb the 176-step Memorial Tower (1925) for one of the great North Island panoramas: Mount Taranaki west, Ruapehu inland, South Island (on the clearest days) south.

reviews.summary: Durie Hill Elevator & Memorial Tower draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Walk through a 205-metre pedestrian tunnel, take a 1919 lift 66 metres up through the hill, climb the tower — Taranaki to Ruapehu on a clear day. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

name: Feilding — Most Beautiful Small Town
short: Voted New Zealand's most beautiful small town a record 16 times — a showpiece of Edwardian main-street heritage in the Manawatū.
commentary: Feilding has won the Most Beautiful Town Award a record 16 times. The main street retains an outstanding collection of Edwardian two-storey brick commercial buildings.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Feilding — Most Beautiful Small Town is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. Voted New Zealand's most beautiful small town a record 16 times — a showpiece of Edwardian main-street heritage in the Manawatū. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You have arrived in Feilding, a town of about fifteen thousand people ten minutes drive north of Palmerston North. Feilding has one particular claim to fame. It has been voted New Zealand's Most Beautiful Small Town a record sixteen times, which is more than any other town in the country and, as far as any of the locals know, more than any other small town anywhere in the world. Walk the main street and you will understand why. The streetscape is a remarkably intact collection of two storey Edwardian commercial buildings, mostly red brick with cream plaster detail, carefully maintained by a community that clearly takes the competition seriously. The gardens are immaculate. The roundabouts are themed. On Friday mornings, the town comes alive with the world famous Feilding Saleyards, but the rest of the week it settles into the contented rhythm of a prosperous farming service town. Stop for coffee on the central Square, walk to the Carnegie Free Library of nineteen oh eight, notice the stained glass, and then drive out to Mount Lees Reserve for a gentle afternoon walk in one of the oldest planted arboretums in the country. Feilding will not surprise you. It will, however, quietly impress you.

name: Foxton — De Molen Dutch Windmill
short: A full-size working Dutch windmill in the middle of Foxton, commemorating the post-war Dutch migration to New Zealand.
commentary: De Molen was built 2003 as a full-scale working replica of a 17th-century Dutch flour mill, honouring the post-WW2 Dutch migration. Grinds flour sold on site.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Foxton — De Molen Dutch Windmill — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A full-size working Dutch windmill in the middle of Foxton, commemorating the post-war Dutch migration to New Zealand. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have arrived in Foxton, a small town on State Highway One on the Manawatū coast, and you have found what appears to be a Dutch windmill. This is not a trick of the eye. De Molen, which is Dutch for the mill, was built in two thousand and three as a full-size working replica of a seventeenth century Dutch flour mill. It stands seventeen metres tall with sails that measure twenty four metres across and on a windy day, which in the Manawatū is most days, the sails turn, the millstones grind, and the mill produces actual flour that is sold in the small shop below. De Molen exists because Foxton became, after the Second World War, one of the largest Dutch settlements in New Zealand. Thousands of Dutch migrants arrived in the late nineteen forties and fifties fleeing the devastation of war, and many were allocated land around Foxton and Shannon. By the nineteen sixties Foxton had the highest concentration of Dutch New Zealanders anywhere in the country. The windmill is their tribute to the old country. Climb the four flights to the viewing platform, watch the millstones work, buy a small bag of flour, and don't forget the excellent Dutch bakery a block further down the main street.

name: Jerusalem / Hiruhārama
short: A tiny Whanganui River village with a beautiful white Catholic church, linked to Mother Mary Aubert and the poet James K Baxter.
commentary: Hiruhārama, also called Jerusalem, is home to the Sisters of Compassion convent founded by Mother Suzanne Aubert in 1892. The poet James K Baxter established a commune here in 1969 and died here in 1972.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Jerusalem / Hiruhārama — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A tiny Whanganui River village with a beautiful white Catholic church, linked to Mother Mary Aubert and the poet James K Baxter. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

audio_story: You have arrived at Hiruhārama, known in English as Jerusalem, a tiny kāinga on the Whanganui River Road about sixty six kilometres north of Whanganui city. Two quite different threads of New Zealand history tie themselves together in this small place. The first is the Catholic mission. In eighteen ninety two a French nun named Suzanne Aubert, who had worked with Whanganui iwi for twenty years, founded the Sisters of Compassion convent here. Aubert fed the poor, ran a pharmacy, translated prayers into te reo Māori, and in twenty sixteen was declared Venerable by the Catholic Church, the first step toward sainthood. She will almost certainly be New Zealand's first Catholic saint. The white timber church of Saint Joseph remains the defining landmark of the settlement. The second thread is the poet James K Baxter, one of the defining voices of twentieth century New Zealand literature. In nineteen sixty nine Baxter walked barefoot up the river road to Jerusalem and established a commune dedicated to living by Māori kawa and caring for society's casualties. He died here in nineteen seventy two and is buried in the pā urupā. Visit respectfully. This is a living settlement.

name: Feilding Saleyards
short: Watch the largest livestock saleyards in the southern hemisphere in action every Friday.
commentary: Feilding Saleyards have run weekly since 1884 and are reputed to be the largest stock saleyards in the southern hemisphere.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Feilding Saleyards is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. Watch the largest livestock saleyards in the southern hemisphere in action every Friday. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You have arrived in Feilding on a Friday morning, and you have arrived on the right day. The Feilding Saleyards have been operating in this town since eighteen eighty four, and they are widely reckoned to be the largest livestock saleyards in the southern hemisphere. Every Friday between four and seven thousand cattle and many thousands more sheep pass through the timber pens, are walked across the auction floor, and are sold by an auctioneer whose voice has been raised in a fast unbroken patter since dawn. The auctioneers are descendants, in many cases, of the men who bought and sold here a century ago. There is a small free public viewing tour offered most Friday mornings, and the local information centre can book it for you. Wear shoes you do not mind getting dirty. You will learn the meaning of a finger held up, the difference between a fat lamb and a store lamb, and why the price of a cow at the rail can travel the country to your butcher within a week. Afterwards, walk through the main street of Feilding, which has been voted the most beautiful small town in New Zealand more often than any other. Have a coffee. Catch your breath.

name: Te Apiti / Manawatū Gorge & Wind Farm
short: The dramatic Manawatū Gorge — closed to traffic, now a walker's paradise overlooked by the country's largest wind farm.
commentary: The gorge road closed permanently in 2017 after repeated landslides. The Manawatū Gorge Track (10 km) crosses the ridge through bush past wind turbines and the Te Ahu a Tūranga sculpture.

reviews.summary: Te Apiti / Manawatū Gorge & Wind Farm draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The dramatic Manawatū Gorge — closed to traffic, now a walker's paradise overlooked by the country's largest wind farm. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You are looking at the Manawatū Gorge, a deep narrow cut through the Ruahine and Tararua Ranges where the Manawatū River, in one of the more curious feats of New Zealand geology, has somehow carved its way clean across the dividing range from east to west. For many years the main road through the gorge was the principal link between the Manawatū and Hawke's Bay. Repeated and progressively larger landslides forced its permanent closure in twenty seventeen. The road is gone, but in some ways the gorge has been freed. The main hiking track from Ashhurst climbs through dense bush over the ridge to Woodville, ten kilometres one way, and the drive over the new Te Ahu a Tūranga highway opened in twenty twenty four to replace the lost road. Above the gorge spin the turbines of the Te Apiti and Tararua Wind Farms, among the largest in the country. From a viewing platform on the ridge you can stand among the turbines, hear the great slow whoosh of the blades, look down on the river two hundred metres below, and feel the wind on your face that, in this place, never seems to stop.

name: Pūkaha National Wildlife Centre
short: Home of Manukura, the famous white kiwi — a wildlife sanctuary in the Wairarapa hills with a strong breeding programme.
commentary: Pūkaha (formerly Mt Bruce) is a 942-hectare sanctuary on SH2. Famous for North Island brown kiwi breeding and previously the rare leucistic 'white' kiwi Manukura.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Pūkaha National Wildlife Centre earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. Home of Manukura, the famous white kiwi — a wildlife sanctuary in the Wairarapa hills with a strong breeding programme. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

audio_story: You have arrived at Pūkaha, a nine hundred and forty hectare bush sanctuary on the eastern side of the Tararua Ranges, between Masterton and the Manawatū. For decades this was one of the most important captive breeding centres in the country for endangered native birds, and it has played a quiet but enormous role in the recovery of species like takahē, kōkako and saddleback. Pūkaha became briefly world famous in twenty eleven when Manukura, a rare leucistic white North Island brown kiwi, was hatched here. She lived at the centre and was viewed by hundreds of thousands of visitors before her death in twenty twenty. Today Pūkaha remains one of the more reliable places in the country to actually see a kiwi up close in the dedicated nocturnal house, alongside kōkako, kākā, kākāriki, and tuatara. The walking tracks loop through the centre's outer reserve, where you can see how a fenced and predator-controlled forest looks and sounds different from one where rats and possums still hold sway. Time your visit to coincide with the daily eel feed in the river running through the sanctuary. The eels here are huge, well over a hundred years old, and watching them is unexpectedly moving.

name: Mangaweka
short: A tiny Rangitikei town with dramatic white river cliffs, and a DC-3 aeroplane on the roadside that used to be a café.
commentary: Mangaweka sits above the Rangitikei River where it has carved deep, almost vertical white papa-mudstone cliffs — the most dramatic river scenery on SH1. For decades a 1940s DC-3 passenger aircraft was parked on the roadside as a café (the Mangaweka Café), long since closed, but the plane is still there. The old highway cuts through the town with 1890s storefronts; the new highway bypasses it. Mangaweka is also the base for adventure rafting (Rangitikei Grade 5 for experts, Grade 2 floats for families) and the start of the Rangitikei River Trails.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Mangaweka is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. A tiny Rangitikei town with dramatic white river cliffs, and a DC-3 aeroplane on the roadside that used to be a café. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Ohakune — Carrot Capital & Old Coach Road
short: A winter ski town and summer cycle hub on Ruapehu's southern flank, marked by a giant concrete carrot at the northern entrance.
commentary: Ohakune sits at the southern base of Mt Ruapehu and services the Tūroa skifield. The carrot monument celebrates the town's market-garden heritage. The Old Coach Road, a 15 km cycle and walking trail, links Ohakune to Horopito past restored 1904 viaducts.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Ohakune — Carrot Capital & Old Coach Road is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. A winter ski town and summer cycle hub on Ruapehu's southern flank, marked by a giant concrete carrot at the northern entrance. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You have arrived in Ohakune, a small town of about a thousand full-time residents that sits at the southern foot of Mount Ruapehu. In winter it swells to several thousand as skiers and snowboarders pour up the road to Tūroa, the longest vertical descent of any commercial skifield in the country. In summer it is quiet and green, wrapped in the steaming smell of pine forestry and the cold clean air that rolls off the mountain. The giant concrete carrot at the northern entrance commemorates the thousands of tonnes of carrots and parsnips grown each year in the rich volcanic soil that surrounds the town. Ohakune's best-kept secret is the Old Coach Road, a fifteen kilometre cycling and walking trail between Ohakune and Horopito that follows the original horse-drawn coach route abandoned when the Main Trunk Railway finally joined its last sleeper in nineteen oh eight. The trail passes two beautifully restored curved steel viaducts, the Hapuawhenua and the Taonui, and climbs gently through native bush. You can hire a bike in town, ride to Horopito, and catch a shuttle back. In summer it is one of the most underrated half-days of riding in the North Island.

name: Palmerston North / Te Manawa & The Square
short: The 7-hectare Square at the city's heart, Te Manawa museum complex, and the NZ Rugby Museum.
commentary: Palmerston North is the Manawatū's main city — university town (Massey), army town (Linton, Ohakea), farming services town. The Square, at the city centre, is a 7-hectare oblong laid out in 1871: the largest CBD public square in the country. Te Manawa is a three-part cultural complex — art gallery, science centre, museum — all free. Next door is the NZ Rugby Museum, the only museum dedicated to rugby in the country. Unmissable for fans. Very missable for almost anyone else, except the All Blacks history room.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Palmerston North / Te Manawa & The Square is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The 7-hectare Square at the city's heart, Te Manawa museum complex, and the NZ Rugby Museum. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

name: Pātea & Aotea Waka Memorial
short: The river-mouth town that gave the country Poi E, and the white concrete Aotea canoe memorial marking the landfall of one of the great migration waka.
commentary: Pātea sits at the mouth of the Pātea River. The Aotea waka, captained by Turi, made landfall near here around 1350. The modern concrete waka sculpture commemorates the voyage. The Pātea Māori Club, led by Dalvanius Prime, released Poi E in 1984 — NZ's best-selling single of that year.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pātea & Aotea Waka Memorial as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The river-mouth town that gave the country Poi E, and the white concrete Aotea canoe memorial marking the landfall of one of the great migration waka. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You have arrived in Pātea, a small town of about twelve hundred people at the mouth of the Pātea River on the south Taranaki coast. Pātea was once a prosperous freezing-works town. The works closed in nineteen eighty two and the town has spent the decades since finding a quieter, harder, prouder identity. The concrete waka sculpture on the main street commemorates the arrival of the Aotea, one of the great migration canoes, which under its captain Turi made landfall near here around thirteen fifty and gave its name to the country itself, Aotea becoming the prefix for Aotearoa. Pātea is also the birthplace of the song Poi E. In nineteen eighty four a local musician and producer named Dalvanius Prime recorded, with the Pātea Māori Club, an all te reo Māori disco track that went straight to number one on the New Zealand charts and outsold every other single that year including Michael Jackson's Thriller. The song has become a national anthem for te reo revitalisation. If you grew up in Aotearoa after nineteen eighty four, you know every word. Stand beside the waka, look out over the river bar, and listen for a moment. You may hear the beat.

name: Pipiriki — Whanganui River Road End
short: The roadhead village at the upper end of the Whanganui River Road — jet boat launching point for the Bridge to Nowhere trip.
commentary: Pipiriki, 79 km up the Whanganui River Road from Whanganui, is a small kāinga with deep significance to Whanganui iwi. In the tourism boom of 1890-1920, it was a major steamship stop. Today it's the start of most jet-boat trips upriver to the Bridge to Nowhere.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pipiriki — Whanganui River Road End as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The roadhead village at the upper end of the Whanganui River Road — jet boat launching point for the Bridge to Nowhere trip. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You have arrived at Pipiriki, a small kāinga at the northern end of the Whanganui River Road, seventy nine kilometres north of Whanganui city. Pipiriki is a place of real significance to the Whanganui iwi, which have lived along this awa for at least six hundred years. In the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries Pipiriki was also one of the most glamorous tourist destinations in the country. Paddle steamers carried visitors upriver from Whanganui to the two hundred and fifty room Pipiriki House Hotel, built in nineteen oh four, which burned down in nineteen fifty nine and was never rebuilt. You can still see the cleared ground where it stood. Today Pipiriki is quiet. It is the ending point for most canoeists coming down from Taumarunui on the Whanganui Journey, which means in summer the river bank fills with tired paddlers and overturned canoes drying in the sun. It is also the launching point for jet boats going upriver to the Bridge to Nowhere, a thirty kilometre run through the steep bush gorges of the upper river. If you have time, take the jet boat trip. It is one of the finest river journeys in the country.

name: Rātana Pā
short: The spiritual home of the Rātana Church — founded by the prophet Tahupōtiki Wiremu Rātana in 1918. Every 25 January political NZ gathers here.
commentary: Rātana Pā, near Whanganui, is the headquarters of Te Haahi Rātana, the Rātana Church, an indigenous Māori Christian movement founded after the 1918 influenza epidemic by farmer and prophet T. W. Rātana. The Temepara (temple), twin towers Arepa and Omeka, and annual 25 January celebrations are focal.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Rātana Pā as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The spiritual home of the Rātana Church — founded by the prophet Tahupōtiki Wiremu Rātana in 1918. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

audio_story: You have arrived at Rātana Pā, a small settlement twenty kilometres southeast of Whanganui that carries a significance in Māori spiritual and political life out of all proportion to its size. In nineteen eighteen, as the influenza pandemic swept through Aotearoa and killed Māori at eight times the rate of settlers, a Taranaki farmer named Tahupōtiki Wiremu Rātana experienced a vision in which he was called to heal his people and unite the tribes under one god. From that vision grew Te Haahi Rātana, the Rātana Church, which within a decade counted around one in five Māori as adherents and which remains one of the most important indigenous Christian movements in the Pacific. The distinctive white temepara, the temple, with its twin towers named Arepa and Omeka for Alpha and Omega, stands at the centre of the settlement. Every year on the twenty fifth of January, which is the birthday of the prophet, thousands gather here for religious observance. The political wing of the country comes too, because since the nineteen thirties a formal alliance has bound the Rātana movement to the Labour Party. A visit here is quiet and respectful. Please do not photograph people or buildings without asking. This is a living, working spiritual community.

name: Taihape — Gumboot Capital
short: A small Rangitikei town on SH1 famous for one thing: the gumboot. Home of Gumboot Day and the world gumboot-throwing record.
commentary: Taihape anchored itself to the gumboot in 1985 as a cheeky rural identity. The oversized corrugated-iron gumboot in the main street is the country's most-photographed piece of roadside kitsch.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Taihape — Gumboot Capital is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. A small Rangitikei town on SH1 famous for one thing: the gumboot. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You have arrived in Taihape, a hill-country town of about fifteen hundred people straddling State Highway One almost exactly halfway between Auckland and Wellington. For generations it was a railway town and a servicing hub for sheep and beef farms in the steep Rangitikei hill country to the east. Then in nineteen eighty five, looking for a way to put itself on the map, Taihape declared itself the Gumboot Capital of New Zealand and launched Gumboot Day — an annual festival of gumboot-throwing, gumboot races, and quite serious gumboot-hurling competitions that still draws several thousand visitors on the Tuesday after Easter each year. The giant corrugated-iron gumboot sculpture on the main street, built by a local farmer, has become one of the most photographed pieces of roadside kitsch in the country. Take your obligatory photo, walk the main street, notice the well-preserved early twentieth century shopfronts, and then if you have time, drive east onto the Gentle Annie Road to Hawke's Bay — one of the great, quiet, and unexpectedly beautiful drives in the North Island. Taihape repays about twenty minutes of your time and then gets out of your way.

name: Mt Holdsworth / Tararua Forest Park
short: The classic tramping gateway to the Tararua Range — Holdsworth Road End on the eastern edge, a 90-minute walk from Wellington traffic.
commentary: Holdsworth Road End sits at the base of the southern Tararua Range. Mount Holdsworth (1470 m) is a popular day-climb or weekend tramp via Atiwhakatu and Powell Hut. The Tararuas are a famously steep, wet, and challenging range that has claimed many lives.

reviews.summary: Mt Holdsworth / Tararua Forest Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The classic tramping gateway to the Tararua Range — Holdsworth Road End on the eastern edge, a 90-minute walk from Wellington traffic. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

audio_story: You have arrived at the Holdsworth Road End, the classic eastern gateway into the Tararua Forest Park. The Tararua Range runs for a hundred and fifty kilometres down the spine of the lower North Island, separating the wet western Manawatū and Horowhenua coast from the drier eastern Wairarapa. The range is only about fifteen hundred metres at its highest point, but the combination of steep ridges, thick cloud cover, unpredictable weather, and tops that are fully exposed to the roaring forties wind makes the Tararuas one of the most unforgiving ranges in the country. They have killed dozens of trampers, many of them experienced. The road end here is quiet and unassuming. A short flat walk from the car park takes you to the Atiwhakatu Stream and one of the most beautiful swimming holes in the lower North Island. Beyond that the trail climbs steeply to Mount Holdsworth at fourteen hundred and seventy metres, five hours up and three down if you are fit. Start early, carry a hot drink, and do not commit to the tops if the cloud is low. Wellington city feels impossibly close and impossibly far away from a Tararua ridge in a southerly.

name: National Army Museum Te Mata Toa
short: The main military museum in New Zealand, on the Desert Road at Waiouru — comprehensive, sobering, and essential for anyone interested in NZ's military history.
commentary: Opened 1978, the National Army Museum at Waiouru is the principal repository of New Zealand Army history from the New Zealand Wars to modern deployments. Set against the Kaimanawa hills on the exposed volcanic plateau.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at National Army Museum Te Mata Toa is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The main military museum in New Zealand, on the Desert Road at Waiouru — comprehensive, sobering, and essential for anyone interested in NZ's military history. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

audio_story: You have arrived at Waiouru, a garrison town on the exposed volcanic plateau at the southern end of the Desert Road. Waiouru is above all else an army town. The New Zealand Defence Force training area that surrounds it covers some sixty three thousand hectares and has done since the Second World War. At the heart of the town is the National Army Museum, Te Mata Toa, which is the principal repository of New Zealand Army history in the country. The galleries take you from the New Zealand Wars of the eighteen sixties, through the landings at Gallipoli and the trenches of the Western Front, through the desperate campaigns of North Africa and Crete, through Korea and Vietnam and finally to modern deployments in East Timor and Afghanistan. The Roll of Honour room is deliberately simple and deliberately quiet.
